title: Pampas grass is recommended to Agapanthus, Aloe and Amaryllis and praised for the plumes that make it invasive
subject: agapanthus
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a listed invasive/noxious plant across exactly the zone band the app offers it in (7-11): Cal-IPC rates it High, it is a listed noxious weed in Oregon and Hawaii and naturalises along the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. Each female plume carries on the order of 100,000 wind-borne seeds that travel for miles, so the 'seed heads stand through winter' clause praises the precise organ that spreads it, and it does so with no caution. The 'for the birds' half is also false: pampas offers negligible wildlife food and its leaf margins are razor-sharp (a real hazard in a home bed). The library data is the root cause and it is internally inconsistent: taxonomy.ts:423 flags miscanthus `spreader: true` with the comment 'invasive in much of the US', while taxonomy.ts:424 records `cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits`. The pairing recurs for aloe and amaryllis in this chunk alone.
fix: Set `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts so the pair takes the SPREADER_SOFT_PENALTY and the reason gains the 'a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging' caution; better, add a `noRecommend`/invasive flag so listed invasives can never be surfaced as companions at all.
scope: 3 of the 10 subjects in this chunk (agapanthus, aloe, amaryllis) recommend pampas grass; it is an archetype so it can appear in any full-sun list
notes: Carry three corrections into the report. (1) Drop or soften the Oregon citation: Oregon's noxious weed list definitively carries Cortaderia JUBATA; C. selloana's Oregon listing is the weakest link. Use the defensible set the repo's own lens already states - Cal-IPC "High" in California, Hawaii state noxious weed, invasive in Texas and the Southeast (FLEPPC), naturalised along the Gulf and Pacific coasts. (2) "across exactly the zone band 7-11" is slightly overstated - inland zone 7-8 is not where it escapes. Say instead: every region where it is listed (coastal CA, HI, the Gulf/Southeast, the PNW fringe) sits inside the 7-11 band the app offers it in. (3) The proposed fix mis-states the mechanism and under-sells the problem: with `spreader: true`, spreaderSmothers (compatibility.ts:511, OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION = 0.6) HARD-BLOCKS pampas against any non-robust neighbour under 1.5 m, so agapanthus (0.8 m) and aloe would be dropped outright, not soft-penalised; and the "give it room or an edging" caution only fires on the `spreader-needs-watching` penalty (compatibility.ts:1190), which is why maiden-grass shows 68 lists and 0 cautions in invasive-recount.json. The spreader flag alone would still let a listed invasive be recommended to tall/woody subjects with no caution - so the `noRecommend`/invasive flag is the primary fix, not the fallback. Free strengthening point: the aloe reason (rank #1) says pampas "stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath" - that is independently false. A pampas clump is a dense, sharp-leaved, sun-hogging mound, not a canopy, and nothing (least of all a rot-prone succulent) can be planted beneath it.

Reword the invasive citation to "Cal-IPC rates Cortaderia selloana High; it is a listed noxious weed in Hawaii and naturalises across California, Texas and the Southeast" (Oregon's list carries C. jubata, a different species, so drop Oregon). Optionally add scale: the audit's invasive-recount.json shows pampas-grass appearing in 191 companion lists, praised in 191, cautioned in 0.

Survives, and is under-sold: pampas-grass is the RANK-1 companion for aloe, and appears in 191 companion lists, praised in all 191, cautioned in 0 (invasive-recount.json). Two wording corrections for the report: (a) call the "for the birds" half UNSUPPORTED rather than false - the plumes do genuinely persist through winter (the structure half is true) and birds take the fluff for nesting; it is the seed-forage implication that is empty, so "unsupported" cannot be picked apart. (b) State the range as "Cal-IPC rates C. selloana High in California, it is a noxious weed in Hawaii, and it naturalises on the Gulf/Atlantic and Pacific coasts" rather than "invasive in much of the US" (that phrase belongs to miscanthus, and jubatagrass / C. jubata is the firmer Oregon listing). The load-bearing point is unchanged: the invaded range and the 7-11 band the app offers it in are the same map. Strongest evidence for the fix is the internal inconsistency: taxonomy.ts uses `spreader: true` AS its invasive flag twice with invasiveness cited in the comment, yet records `cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits`.

title: Butterfly bush is recommended to Giant Allium as a 'strong pollinator plant' with no invasiveness caution
subject: allium
claim: Shares full sun and low water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; both are strong pollinator plants; taller at 2 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Buddleia davidii is a Class B noxious weed in Washington, is on Oregon's quarantine list (only certified low-fertility cultivars may be sold), and is invasive through the Pacific Northwest and mid-Atlantic; a single panicle sets tens of thousands of wind-dispersed seeds. The library gives it `pollinator: "high"` (taxonomy.ts:145), which produces the strongest pollinator clause in the vocabulary, but that is the half-truth the plant is most often mis-sold on: it is nectar for adult butterflies and a larval host for essentially nothing native, so a beginner told 'both are strong pollinator plants' will plant it believing it is the ecologically better choice. Recommending it by its unqualified species name, with no caution and no mention of a seedless cultivar, is advice a horticulturist should not give.
fix: In taxonomy.ts, add `spreader: true` to buddleia/buddleja so the pairing carries the spreader caution and penalty, and demote the flag to `pollinator: "some"` so it stops earning the 'strong pollinator plant' sentence; ideally suppress the straight species in favour of a sterile cultivar entry.
scope: Any full-sun, low/moderate-water subject can draw butterfly bush; it is an archetype
notes: Finding stands, but the PROPOSED FIX is wrong on both halves and must be rewritten before it goes in a report. (1) `spreader: true` on buddleia would not add a caution to this pair - it would DELETE the pair: `spreaderSmothers` fires because allium is a Bulb (non-robust) at 0.9 m <= OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION 0.6 x 2 m, so butterfly bush would be hard-blocked from most non-woody neighbours under 1.2 m; and where it did survive, the caution clause it emits ("a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging") describes bed crowding, which is not this plant's problem - it does not run at its neighbours, it seeds into wild land. (2) `pollinator: "some"` would enter a FALSE value: the field is curated forage value (FORAGE_RANK), and Buddleja davidii genuinely is one of the best adult-butterfly nectar shrubs. A horticulturist would not sign a demotion. The true gap is that the engine models neither larval-host value nor regulated-invasive status. The correct remedy is a dedicated flag (e.g. `regulatedInvasive`) that emits its own caution clause on the reason ("a regulated noxious weed in WA and OR: plant an approved seedless cultivar, or choose a native nectar shrub") and, ideally, a link from the companion row to the companion's plant page so the existing sourced deepIntel caution is reachable. Also tighten the wording: assert what the repo's own recount asserts - "Class B noxious weed in Washington, quarantine-listed in Oregon, invasive in the PNW and parts of the East" - rather than the more specific "mid-Atlantic" claim. Note the systemic scale: butterfly-bush is 42 uncautioned lists, and the same uncautioned-invasive pattern hits pampas grass (191 lists) and maiden grass (68).

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The proposed fix is misdescribed: adding `spreader: true` to buddleia would NOT "carry the spreader caution and penalty" on this pair. Allium is a Bulb at 0.9 m and 0.9 <= OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION (0.6) x 2.0 m, so isVulnerableTo -> spreaderSmothers fires the HARD blocker "spreader-smothers" and the pairing is deleted outright, as it would be for most of buddleia's herbaceous and bulb partners; only woody partners (ROBUST_CATEGORIES: Shrub/Tree/Conifer/Rose/Climber) would survive and receive the cautionClause text "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging". State the fix as a removal-plus-caution, not a caution alone. (2) Soften the "botanically false" wording: "both are strong pollinator plants" is a half-truth (real adult nectar value, near-zero native larval host value), not a false statement; the charge that carries the finding is the missing invasiveness caution on an unqualified straight-species Buddleia davidii, and the precedent that the library already flags the less-regulated Miscanthus sinensis for exactly this reason (taxonomy.ts:423).

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The proposed fix is wrong on both halves. `pollinator: "high"` is defensible data, not an error: Buddleia davidii genuinely is one of the strongest adult-butterfly nectar shrubs, so demoting it to "some" trades one inaccuracy for another. And `spreader: true` would emit cautionClause's "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging", which describes clonal crowding of a neighbour, not the actual risk (wind-dispersed seed escaping into wild land) - it would read as a spacing tip and mislead in a different direction. The right remedy is a real invasiveness/caution flag, or swapping the group's archetype from the straight species to one of the low-fertility cultivars already in the library ('Miss Molly', 'Blue Chip', 'Pugster Blue' - exactly the type Oregon permits). (2) The allium pair is one instance of a systemic gap, not a one-off: butterfly-bush appears on 42 companion lists with zero cautions, and by exposure pampas grass (191 lists) and maiden grass (68) are worse. Also soften "invasive through much of the US": it is regulated in WA and OR and problematic in the maritime PNW and eastern river corridors, not nationwide.

title: Mint is recommended into Bugleweed's bed with no spreader warning at all, because two spreaders cancel each other out
subject: ajuga
claim: Takes part sun to part shade and moderate to high water; overlaps, then carries the display into late summer; taller at 0.5 m, for the layer behind it; purple sits close to blue on the wheel.
why: Mentha is flagged `spreader: true` (taxonomy.ts:185) and Ajuga is too (taxonomy.ts:187), and that is exactly why the beginner gets no warning. `isVulnerableTo` returns false the moment the neighbour is itself a spreader ('equal vigour, let them fight'), so the spreader-smothers filter never fires; `spreaderNeedsWatching`'s `soft()` test also excludes spreaders, so the penalty never fires; and `reasonFor` only appends `cautionClause` when that penalty is in the list. Net effect: the single most-regretted plant in home gardening is offered for an open bed with a sentence about the colour wheel and no containment advice. In the same list, clump-forming Goatsbeard and Ligularia DO get the caution (correctly, about Ajuga running into them), so the pairing that most needs a warning is the only one without one. Horticulturally, mint in open ground next to a 15 cm groundcover ends with the ground being mint.
fix: In `reasonFor`, emit `cautionClause` whenever either plant is a spreader, not only when the `spreader-needs-watching` penalty fires; and when BOTH are spreaders, say so ('both run, so give each an edging or a sunken pot'). Mint should never be recommended for open ground without the 'grow it in a container' caveat.
scope: Every spreader-to-spreader pairing in the catalog (mint, ajuga, matteuccia, miscanthus, physostegia, macleaya) is silently exempt from the caution
notes: Two corrections for the report. (1) "the pairing that most needs a warning is the only one without one" is loose: ostrich-fern -> mint (Matteuccia is also spreader:true, taxonomy.ts:437) is uncautioned for the identical reason, and the three shrubs correctly get no caution (robust category). Cite both mint pairs and frame the hole as systematically spreader-vs-spreader; the strongest single fact is that mint is recommended in only 2 lists catalog-wide and both suppress the caution. (2) Partial mitigation worth naming: careTips.ts:218 does emit a "Spreads vigorously... give it room, a contained spot" tip on mint's OWN plant detail; it never appears on the companion card, which carries an inline "Add to garden" button, so the mitigation is bypassable. Also note mint sits at rank 7 of 8 in ajuga's list, i.e. behind "Show all 8 companions" rather than in the default three.

Two precisions to carry into the report. (1) "the only one without one" is imprecise: ostrich fern in the same ajuga list also gets no caution, for the identical reason (taxonomy.ts:437 flags matteuccia `spreader: true`), and the three shrubs (summersweet, beautyberry, hydrangea) are correctly exempt as ROBUST categories. Mint is the sharpest case, not the unique one. (2) Reach: mint is recommended in exactly 2 lists catalog-wide (subjects `ajuga` and `ostrich-fern`), and in BOTH the subject is itself a flagged spreader - which is precisely the "let them fight" exemption at compatibility.ts:504 letting mint through the spreader-smothers filter. That makes the mechanism airtight rather than incidental: mint can only ever be recommended when the caution is guaranteed to be suppressed. The same both-spreaders hole silences the caution on roughly 37 pairs overall (e.g. ladys-mantle -> pachysandra, boston-ivy -> maiden-grass), so the proposed fix (emit cautionClause whenever either plant is a spreader, and say so when both are) fixes a class, not a one-off. Also note mint's category is `Herb`, which is not in ROBUST_CATEGORIES, so category plays no part in the suppression.

Survives, with two corrections to scope and one strengthening fact. STRENGTHEN: the reciprocal case is more exposed than the one reported - on Mint's own page, Bugleweed is companion #2 of 8 (inside the three inline cards, no "Show all" needed) with reason "Takes part sun to part shade and moderate to high water; opens in mid spring and overlaps into this one's season; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; lower at 0.15 m, for the front of the border." and no caution either. Also worth stating: spatial.ts:104 gates its spreader-near-delicate warning on the neighbour NOT being a spreader, so the plan view does not catch it later, and the companion card's "Add to garden" button lets a beginner add mint without ever seeing mint's own containment care tip. TRIM: exposure is bounded - mint appears as a companion in only 2 of the 276 archetype lists (ajuga at rank 7 of 8, behind "Show all"; ostrich-fern at rank 8 of 8), the surface is behind the premium reveal gate, and mint's own plant page does carry the containment tip. Also "the only one without one" is loose: the shrub companions (summersweet, beautyberry, hydrangea) correctly lack the caution because they are robust; mint is the only HERBACEOUS companion in ajuga's list without it. Severity therefore medium, not high.

title: 'A canopy to plant beneath' is told to full-sun plants: Ageratum under a Lilac, Aloe under Pampas Grass
subject: ageratum
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: `layeringClause` chooses the 'a canopy to plant beneath' wording purely on the height ratio (r > 6) and the taller plant's tier; it never looks at the shorter plant's light need. So a pair that was matched BECAUSE both want full sun is then described in a sentence instructing the gardener to plant one in the other's shade. Ageratum houstonianum in the shade of a 3 m lilac is leggy, sparse-flowering and mildew-prone, which is the standard failure for that bedding annual. The same clause fires for ageratum under viburnum (2 m) and mock orange (2.5 m), and for aloe under pampas grass (a full-sun succulent placed inside a 1.5 m clump of cutting-edged leaves). The instruction is self-contradictory and a beginner will follow it literally.
fix: In `layeringClause`, only use the 'canopy to plant beneath' / 'carpet the ground beneath' wording when the shorter plant tolerates at least part shade (sunStep(shorter.sun) >= part-sun); for two full-sun plants say 'taller at X m, plant it behind rather than under' so the shorter plant keeps its light.
scope: Every pair with a >6:1 height ratio where the shorter plant is full-sun; 4 pairings in this chunk
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Height error in the finding: the aloe pairing is with pampas-grass at 2.5 m, not 1.5 m; 1.5 m is pampas-grass-dwarf, which does not trigger the clause (ratio 3.75 < CANOPY_RATIO 6). Also worth stating the stronger reason that pair is wrong: a Cortaderia clump has no understory at all (foliage mound is solid to ground level, leaf margins cut), so "a canopy to plant beneath" is physically false regardless of light. (2) The proposed fix is over-broad as written: the library's `sun` field is a single value, not a tolerance range, and several full-sun-tagged plants underplant perfectly well (bee-balm -> redbud and bee-balm -> crabapple fire the same clause and are horticulturally fine). Gating purely on sunStep(shorter) >= part-sun would suppress those legitimate cases. Better gate: suppress the "beneath" wording when both plants are full-sun AND the taller one is a Shrub or Grass (no limbed-up canopy to plant under), and say "taller at X m, plant it behind rather than under" instead. Scale to cite: 171 of 241 "canopy to plant beneath" clauses have a full-sun shorter plant.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Pampas grass is 2.5 m in the library, not 1.5 m (the aloe -> pampas-grass reason reads "stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath"); the point is if anything stronger than stated, since a Cortaderia clump has no plantable understory at all, so "a canopy to plant beneath" is structurally wrong, not merely light-wrong. (2) The proposed fix (require sunStep(shorter) >= part-sun) would also suppress correct advice: the same clause legitimately fires for spring ephemerals under deciduous structure (baptisia -> crocus, baptisia -> scilla, amaranth -> glory-of-the-snow), which get full spring sun before the canopy leafs out. The gate should exempt shorter plants that are dormant/finished when the taller plant is in leaf, and only rewrite the clause (e.g. "taller at X m, plant it behind rather than under") for summer-flowering full-sun plants like ageratum (blooms 6-10) under a leafed-out lilac. Scale evidence worth citing: 375 of 525 canopy/carpet clauses have a full-sun shorter plant.

Scale is larger than the finding states: across the 2,208 displayed pairs, 171 carry "a canopy to plant beneath" where the plant being sent underneath is full-sun (of 241 canopy clauses total), and the mirror clause "carpet the ground beneath" fires on a full-sun companion 204 more times (e.g. arborvitae -> bee-balm, baptisia -> portulaca, amaranth -> portulaca/english-daisy). So the fix must cover BOTH branches of layeringClause, not just the b.height > a.height branch. Worst-case examples to lead with are aloe -> pampas-grass (dry-loving succulent placed inside a 2.5 m clump of cutting-edged leaves) and basket-of-gold -> butterfly-bush / blue-fescue -> lilac (sharp-drainage sun plants in a shrub's dry shade); ageratum -> lilac is the mildest of the set, since ageratum tolerates part shade and merely goes leggy and mildew-prone. Severity medium is correct: the pairings are usually fine, the harm is entirely in the sentence.

title: Agave (and Aloe) are credited with 'leafing out to hide the ripening bulb foliage' of Amaryllis
subject: amaryllis
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter.
why: The bulb-cover factor is the second-heaviest in the engine (26 points) and its clause asserts a specific seasonal behaviour: a herbaceous neighbour that pushes up leaves in late spring and screens the bulb's yellowing foliage. An Agave is an evergreen, spine-tipped succulent rosette (the library itself sets `evergreen: true`, taxonomy.ts:293); it does not leaf out, it holds the same rosette all year, and it can screen nothing. It qualifies only because `COVER_CATEGORIES` accepts any 'Perennial' and `coverHasSpringPresence` treats 'evergreen' as proof of spring presence. Worse as a physical recommendation: interplanting a bulb inside the 1 m footprint of a spined agave means digging and handling among terminal spines, and the agave needs sharp-drained, near-dry soil while the bulb is watered. The identical false clause is generated for Aloe in the same list.
fix: Exclude succulents/rosette plants from `COVER_CATEGORIES` eligibility (e.g. require the cover to be non-evergreen-succulent, or gate on a `succulent` tag), so the 26-point bulb-cover factor and its clause only go to plants whose foliage genuinely emerges and masks.
scope: Both succulent companions in the amaryllis list; any spring-ephemeral bulb whose list reaches a succulent 'Perennial'
notes: Confirmed against the data and the code. Worth adding to the report: the subject side is defective too. Hippeastrum is not a spring ephemeral - its foliage stays green through summer into autumn, so there is no May-June die-back for any neighbour to cover. isSpringEphemeral fires on amaryllis only because its bloom window ends by month 5, which means the 26-point bulbCover factor and its clause are doubly inapplicable to this list (it also produced the same clause for baptisia, blue-oat-grass, japanese-forest-grass and autumn-fern under amaryllis). The proposed fix (exclude evergreen succulents/rosettes from COVER_CATEGORIES) is correct but only half of it; the ephemeral test on the subject side should also exclude bulbs whose foliage persists through summer.

Blast radius is slightly wider than stated and worth citing: exactly five pairs hand the bulb-cover clause to a succulent-tagged companion - amaryllis -> agave (50), amaryllis -> aloe (49), ranunculus -> agave (50), freesia -> agave (42.5), crocus -> creeping-sedum (69.5). Frame the harm as the wrong REASON (the 26-point factor and its "leafs out to hide" sentence), not as a pair that cannot coexist: Hippeastrum with agave in a dry zone 9-10 bed is plausible, and the water-differs penalty already fires.

Verified: amaryllis -> agave is #2 (score 50) and amaryllis -> aloe #3 (score 49) in data/chunks/chunk-01.json, both with the clause "leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back". Cause confirmed: COVER_CATEGORIES accepts any Perennial (compatibility.ts:161) and coverHasSpringPresence credits any `evergreen` (compatibility.ts:693); taxonomy.ts:293 flags agave evergreen. Agave's only other scored factor is winter structure (15), so the 26-point bulb-cover credit is what puts it at #2. Trim these overstated parts from the report: the water gap (low vs moderate) is a one-rung, engine-documented, already-penalised trade-off; the pair is not un-plantable (bulbs among agaves/aloes is a real zone 8-11 xeric style) and the spine hazard is speculative; and "leafs out" is loose for evergreen covers generally (blue-oat-grass, autumn-fern get it too) where the benefit is real. The defect is specific to succulent rosettes, which cannot serve as interplanted bulb cover at all, so the fix should gate on a succulent tag rather than rework the evergreen credit.

title: Agave paired with Tropical Hibiscus and Moonflower: one watering schedule rots the agave, and the reason names no benefit
subject: agave
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; taller at 1.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: The one-rung water tolerance is defensible for most plants and indefensible for a succulent. Hibiscus rosa-sinensis is a heavy feeder that wants consistently moist soil through summer; Agave demands sharp drainage and must be kept nearly dry, and summer water at the crown is the classic cause of agave crown/root rot. Put on one drip line in one bed, one of them is always wrong and the agave is the one that dies. The reason also states no benefit whatever beyond 'taller at 1.5 m' (no succession, no pollinator, no structure), so the app is recommending a pairing that carries real risk and offers the gardener nothing. Agave to moonflower (Ipomoea alba, moderate water, a vigorous 4 m vine) is the same conflict.
fix: Make the water rule asymmetric for succulents: when either plant is tagged `succulent`, require an exact water-band match rather than allowing one rung. As a smaller change, drop pairs that clear no soft-factor floor except layering, so a pairing whose only stated merit is a height difference is never shown.
scope: 2 of the 8 agave companions (tropical-hibiscus, moonflower); applies to every succulent subject/companion in the library
refutations:
Both limbs fail.

WATER: this is a one-rung low-vs-moderate gap, which the engine documents as an accepted trade-off, not a hidden bug (WATER_ADJACENT_PENALTY = 0.92, with the sourced note that hydrozoning names LOW-vs-HIGH as the mistake and that one rung apart is "a normal, workable border"). The `water-differs` penalty is applied and recorded on both pairs. To break the engine the finding needs Hibiscus rosa-sinensis to actually be `high` water (which would trip `water-mismatch` and drop the pair outright), and it is not: it is a conventional moderate-water-use shrub. The library's generic "Agave" (0.8 m x 1.0 m, zones 8-11) is the size of the soft, irrigation-tolerant agaves that are standard in the zone 9-11 mixed subtropical beds this pairing describes; agave crown rot is driven by winter wet plus poor drainage, not by a neighbour on a moderate summer schedule. And the reason sentence discloses the gap in plain words - "Shares full sun and low to moderate water" - so nothing is being concealed from the beginner.

REASON: "states no benefit whatever" is false. Height layering (weight 22) is a scored, real benefit, and the pair also earns succession credit internally (agave is foliage, so ONE_SIDED_BLOOM = 0.5, the documented foliage-foil value; it merely sits under the 0.55 clause floor). Decisively, ALL EIGHT of agave's companions carry exactly one factor clause, because agave is a bloomless, non-pollinator, non-seedhead foliage plant. The top-ranked, penalty-free companion - crape myrtle, an unimpeachable pairing - reads "Shares full sun and low water; taller at 4 m, for the layer behind it", the identical shape the finding condemns. The complaint is about template terseness for foliage subjects, not about these two pairs.

PROPOSED FIX IS HARMFUL: "drop pairs that clear no soft-factor floor except layering" would also delete crape myrtle, cosmos, echeveria and gazania, collapsing agave's list from 8 to 2 (only the two native-clause pairs survive).

Under the "does it harm the user?" lens: a zone 9-11 beginner who puts an agave in front of a hibiscus, having been told in the same sentence that the two want low-to-moderate water, does not lose the agave. The pairing is unexciting and bottom-of-list, which the brief explicitly excludes as a finding.
notes: Two corrections. (1) Soften the certainty: "one of them is always wrong and the agave is the one that dies" is stronger than a horticulturist would sign - in a sharply drained or mounded bed, agave and tropical hibiscus do coexist in zone 10 gardens. The defensible claim is elevated crown/root-rot risk under the shared irrigation schedule and moisture-retentive, rich soil the hibiscus needs, taken on for NO stated benefit but a height difference (the reason names no succession, pollinator, or structure value - agave has no bloom, so none can fire). That risk-for-nothing combination is the finding. (2) The moonflower reason is not identical text: it reads "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; climbs to 4 m behind it, height without the footprint." - still layering-only, so the substance stands. Also worth stating as a class, since the same succulent-vs-moderate-water leak recurs: echeveria -> tropical-hibiscus / cleome / perennial-sunflower, hens-and-chicks -> lily-asiatic / spirea / cleome / amaranth, ice-plant -> spirea / perennial-sunflower, aloe -> sunflower, portulaca -> boxwood, creeping-sedum -> monkshood / colchicum. That supports the proposed fix (require an exact water-band match when either plant carries the `succulent` tag), with agave -> tropical-hibiscus as the named exemplar.

Two refinements for the report. (1) The deeper library error is arguably on the hibiscus side: Hibiscus rosa-sinensis is a consistently-moist heavy feeder and is closer to water="high" than "moderate". Refiled as "high", the low-vs-high gap exceeds MAX_WATER_STEPS and the pair is hard-filtered by the rule that already exists, with no engine change needed. Worth naming alongside the succulent rule. (2) Both pairs sit at ranks 7 and 8 of the 8 shown, so they are the weakest entries on the card, but they ARE shown (no score floor in the selection loop), so this is not a theoretical pairing.

Only durable residue, not report-worthy on its own: tropical-hibiscus (42.8) and moonflower (43.7) are the two lowest scorers on agave's list and are present partly to satisfy MIN_COMPANIONS = 6 out of a 173-candidate pool. That is a ranking/tuning observation, not a botanical error, and the reasons truthfully print the water spread.

title: Aster -> Passionflower: "height without the footprint" is the exact opposite of the truth for Passiflora
subject: aster
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; opens in midsummer and overlaps into this one's season; both are strong pollinator plants; climbs to 4 m behind it, height without the footprint.
why: The library files Passiflora at zones 6-9 (plantLibrary.ts line 2944), which can only be P. incarnata, the hardy maypop. Its defining garden behaviour is aggressive root suckering: it sends up shoots several metres from the crown, straight through neighbouring plantings, and is widely described in extension literature as difficult to contain in a border. The footprint IS the problem, so the clause sells the pairing on a benefit it does not have. taxonomy.ts gives passiflora only `pollinator: "high", pruneClass`, with no `spreader: true`, so it escapes both the spreader-smothers filter and the SPREADER_SOFT_PENALTY, escapes the cautionClause entirely, and ranks 7th (74.8) for a 0.9 m Aster that it will run through. A beginner will be digging suckers out of the aster clump for years.
fix: Add `spreader: true` to the passiflora entry in taxonomy.ts. That alone routes it through spreaderNeedsWatching / isVulnerableTo and makes cautionClause emit "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging". Separately, layeringClause should not offer the climber's "height without the footprint" line for a root-suckering vine.
scope: Every list passionflower appears in as a companion; it is an archetype, so all zone 6-9 full-sun subjects.
refutations:
The finding rests entirely on one identification step: "zones 6-9 can only be P. incarnata, the hardy maypop." That is false, and the library itself refutes it.

1. The archetype at plantLibrary.ts:2944 is `botanical: "Passiflora"` - genus level, no species, tagged `["purple", "exotic", "vine"]`. It is deliberately generic.
2. The very next line (2945) files `passionflower-blue`, Passiflora caerulea, at the SAME zones 6-9. So zones 6-9 in this library is manifestly the caerulea range (which is correct: P. caerulea is routinely listed 6-9 / root-hardy with winter dieback), not an incarnata fingerprint.
3. The word "incarnata" and the word "maypop" appear NOWHERE in plantLibrary.ts. The entire passionflower variant group is P. caerulea, P. caerulea 'Constance Elliott', P. coccinea and the hybrid 'Lady Margaret' - not one native maypop.
4. The library tags US natives `native` (trumpet vine, one line above at 2940, carries it; campsis is even flagged `native: true` in taxonomy.ts:385). The passionflower archetype is tagged `exotic`, the opposite. A maypop reading is inconsistent with the record on every axis.

So the plant the engine is recommending is an ornamental blue passionflower on a support, not a root-running maypop. For P. caerulea, "climbs to 4 m behind it, height without the footprint" is accurate, standard advice a horticulturist would sign: a trellised climber adds vertical interest while occupying almost no border ground. The pairing (full sun, moderate water, zone 3-8 ∩ 6-9 = 6-8, both pollinator) is horticulturally sound if unexciting.

Two supporting checks also cut against the finding: (a) the maintainers DO flag genuinely running vines - campsis, parthenocissus, and even the slug-level `clematis-sweet-autumn` carry `spreader: true` in taxonomy.ts - so the absence of the flag on passiflora reads as a considered call about caerulea, not an oversight; (b) `layeringClause` (compatibility.ts:1065) emits the identical clause for any Climber, and it fired for honeysuckle at rank 1 of the same aster list, so this is not a passionflower-specific reason error.

Residual, much weaker and not what was filed: P. caerulea can sucker in warm/favourable conditions, and the genus-level "Passiflora" entry is vague. That would be a low-severity data-precision note, not a high-severity "the reason is the exact opposite of the truth."
notes: If anything survives, it is only this: the archetype `passionflower` is a genus-level `botanical: "Passiflora"` with no species, while every named member of its group is a non-native ornamental (P. caerulea and tender hybrids). A low-severity data note at most; the aster -> passionflower reason itself is correct for the plant the library actually describes.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Drop "zones 6-9 can only be P. incarnata": the library also files P. caerulea at 6-9. Ground the identification instead on the repo's own intel/deepIntel/passionflower.json, whose botanical field is "Passiflora incarnata (and Passiflora spp.)" and which cites NC State + Missouri Botanical Garden for aggressive root-suckering ("sending up new shoots several feet from the parent... containment matters"), plus its catalog-delta note that the stored 1.5 m spread "understates a plant that root-suckers to cover large areas" and that root-suckering is "a management fact the catalog does not carry". The repo contradicts its own generated reason. (2) The missing spreader flag is genus-wide, so no passionflower pairing anywhere emits the caution; Aster is simply the pair where the "height without the footprint" clause becomes actively false. Strengthening detail for the fix: taxonomy.ts:385 already flags campsis (trumpet vine, the other suckering climber) as spreader: true, so passiflora's omission is an inconsistency, not a deliberate policy, and the field name in the proposed fix (spreader: true, taxonomy.ts:35) is correct.

Reframe on two points. (a) Do not assert P. incarnata: the entry is genus-level Passiflora at z6-9 and the library's own P. caerulea variants sit at 6-9; the point holds because both hardy passionflowers sold in that range sucker freely from the roots, so "height without the footprint" (compatibility.ts:1065) sells a spatial-non-interference benefit the plant does not have. (b) The decisive evidence is internal inconsistency, not the absence of a model: taxonomy.ts flags campsis (line 385) and parthenocissus (line 387) as spreader: true, and the spreaderSmothers hard blocker consequently keeps trumpet vine away from every short herbaceous subject in the generated data. Passiflora escapes only because line 380 omits the flag, and is therefore offered to 10 short herbaceous/bulb subjects (aster, allium, bluestar, chives, sage, stock, canterbury-bells, osteospermum, queen-of-the-prairie, maiden-grass) plus mock-orange and tulip-tree. State that the fix (adding spreader: true) will hard-block, not merely caption, those 10 pairings - that is the same treatment campsis already gets, but the maintainer should expect deletions, not a caution clause.

title: Arborvitae -> Climbing Rose and Clematis, both described as "an understory layer": the vine will climb into the hedge and kill it
subject: arborvitae
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; shorter at 3 m, an understory layer.
why: Arborvitae is filed as a 6 m x 1.5 m columnar screen/hedge conifer. A climbing rose or a clematis planted beside it does not sit under it as an "understory layer" - it has no self-supporting structure and will use the conifer as its trellis, matting over the foliage. Thuja occidentalis does not regenerate from bare old wood, so every branchlet shaded out under the vine dies permanently and the screen is holed for good; the rose's thorny canes also cannot be pruned or trained back out of a dense conifer. There is also no understory to speak of beneath a 1.5 m-wide columnar plant. The cause is in layeringClause: the Climber special case ("climbs to X behind it") only fires inside the `b.height > a.height` branch, so whenever a climber is the SHORTER plant it silently falls through to the tree/conifer branch and is described as understory or groundcover.
fix: In layeringClause, test `b.category === "Climber"` before the taller/shorter split so a vine is never called an understory or a carpet; and block Climber companions for hedge/screen conifers (Thuja, Taxus, Picea) whose foliage the vine would smother.
scope: Both climbers in arborvitae's list (climbing-rose 48.5, clematis 48.5); the layeringClause bug affects any Climber shorter than its subject.
notes: Reframe from "will kill it" to what is defensible: describing a climber as "an understory layer" is flatly false (a climber's 3 m is carried on a support, as the engine itself states at line 1064), and beside a 1.5 m-wide columnar Thuja there is no understory to occupy - the vine's only available support is the conifer, whose shaded-out branchlets will not regrow from bare brown wood, permanently holing a plant whose sole purpose is screening (tags: screen, hedge), with a thorny cane that cannot be trimmed back out. Two facts strengthen the report: (1) layering is the ONLY factor that scores arborvitae -> climbing-rose / clematis (the conifer has no bloom, pollinator rank 0, winterClause returns null because the subject is itself evergreen, the rose is not native), so once the false clause is removed the pairing has no stated benefit at all and should be blocked, not merely re-worded; (2) the defect is systemic - 15 pairs describe a Climber as understory or groundcover, including italian-cypress -> climbing-rose, blue-spruce -> clematis, and wisteria (Climber, 8 m) -> jasmine (Climber, 3 m) "shorter at 3 m, an understory layer", a climber called the understory of a climber.

Verified verbatim in data and code. Two refinements for the report: (1) the bug is systemic - 15 pairs currently label a Climber companion "an understory layer" (arborvitae, blue-spruce, douglas-fir, eastern-white-pine, italian-cypress, flowering-cherry, linden, saucer-magnolia, serviceberry -> clematis / climbing-rose / jasmine, plus wisteria -> jasmine where the SUBJECT is itself a Climber), so the fix must test b.category === "Climber" before the taller/shorter split AND cover the Climber-subject case, not just Tree/Conifer subjects. (2) No pair currently emits the "carpet the ground beneath it" variant for a climber (the largest ratio, douglas-fir 18 m / 3 m, is exactly 6 and fails `r > CANOPY_RATIO`), so that phrasing is a latent risk rather than an observed output - the finding's "understory or groundcover" wording should be trimmed to "understory". Arborvitae remains the strongest instance: the only screen/hedge-tagged conifer among them, 1.5 m wide (no real understory to plant), and Thuja occidentalis does not break bud from bare old wood, so foliage shaded out under a rose or clematis is permanently lost.

Verified: compatibility.ts:1065 handles Climber only in the taller-plant branch, so any climber shorter than the subject is described as "an understory layer" / "to carpet the ground beneath it". 15 archetype pairs affected (10 Conifer, 4 Tree, 1 Climber subject); clematis x8, climbing-rose x5, jasmine x2. arborvitae ships exactly MAX_COMPANIONS = 8, with clematis and climbing-rose both included (score 48.5), so both are user-visible. Narrow the finding to the reason-template defect (a vine is never an understory layer or a groundcover carpet) plus the hedge/screen-conifer subset (arborvitae, italian-cypress) where a vine matting into non-regenerating evergreen foliage leaves permanent holes. Drop the Tree cases (clematis through a tree is a recommended technique, so those are only a wording bug), and drop Taxus from the proposed block list - yew is the conifer that does regenerate from old wood.

title: Full-sun subjects are told to plant themselves beneath a shrub canopy: "Shares full sun ... a canopy to plant beneath" in one sentence
subject: angelonia
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: The sentence contradicts itself. Angelonia is a full-sun heat-lover (6+ hours of direct sun) that goes leggy and stops flowering in shade; the reason opens by confirming both want full sun and then instructs the beginner to plant it under the canopy of a 3 m x 2.5 m lilac, where it gets neither the sun nor the water (dry rain-shadow plus dense shrub root competition). layeringClause emits the canopy phrasing purely on a height ratio > 6 and never consults sun, so it fires for full-sun subjects. The same misfire appears for annual-phlox -> witch-hazel ("stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath", under a 3 m x 3 m part-shade shrub) and annual-phlox -> viburnum ("stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath"). A beginner who follows this literally gets a failed bedding planting and no way to know why.
fix: In layeringClause, suppress the "a canopy to plant beneath" phrasing whenever the SHORTER plant is `full-sun` (and the taller is a dense woody Tree/Shrub) and fall back to "taller at X m, for the layer behind it", which is the placement actually being recommended.
scope: At least 3 pairings in this chunk (angelonia -> lilac 55.5; annual-phlox -> witch-hazel 55.4; annual-phlox -> viburnum 56.1); fires for any full-sun subject with a woody companion over 6x its height.
notes: Two corrections. (1) witch-hazel is filed sun: "part-sun" in plantLibrary.ts, not "part-shade" as the finding states; the gate is also not "purely ratio > 6" but ratio > 6 AND tierOf(max height) >= 2 (which is why a 1 m spirea still qualifies as a "canopy" - independently wrong). (2) The proposed fix over-corrects: suppressing whenever the shorter plant is full-sun would also kill legitimate underplanting - grape-hyacinth -> butterfly-bush, glory-of-the-snow -> boxwood, hyacinth -> mock-orange - where spring bulbs flower and die back before the deciduous canopy leafs out. Key the suppression on the shorter plant being in leaf/bloom during the taller plant's leafed-out season (or simply exempt cat === "Bulb"), not on full-sun alone. Scale evidence worth adding to the report: 64 of 241 canopy clauses are full-sun subjects under woody plants; the worst are hens-and-chicks and ice-plant (low-water succulents) beneath spirea, bearded iris beneath arborvitae (rhizomes rot without baked sun), and floribunda-rose beneath arborvitae/saucer-magnolia.

Verified verbatim in data/chunks/chunk-02.json and at compatibility.ts:1053-1080. Two precisions to carry into the report: the canopy clause also requires the taller plant to clear tier 2 (over 0.9 m), not just ratio > 6 (so it fires even for 1.5-2 m shrubs, e.g. echeveria -> tropical-hibiscus at 1.5 m, ageratum -> viburnum at 2 m); and the "no water either" rain-shadow argument rests on factors the engine documents as unmodelled, so lead with the sun contradiction, which is self-contained. Scale: 241 reasons contain "a canopy to plant beneath"; 171 have a full-sun subject; 64 of those put a full-sun subject under a woody Shrub/Tree/Conifer, across 45 distinct subjects. The pair itself passes the hard filters honestly (both full-sun, both moderate water, annual so zones are skipped) - the defect is the reason's placement instruction, so wrong-reason is the correct category and the proposed fix (suppress canopy phrasing when the shorter plant is full-sun and the taller is a woody Tree/Shrub/Conifer; fall back to "taller at X m, for the layer behind it") is the right shape.

Confirmed in code and data. Correct these details before publishing: the canopy clause requires ratio > 6 AND the taller plant above 0.9 m (tierOf >= 2), not ratio alone; drop the root-competition/rain-shadow argument (root competition is an explicitly documented non-goal of the engine) and rest the finding on the sun contradiction, which sits squarely inside what the engine claims to model ("a bed has one exposure", L76-90); witch-hazel is filed part-sun, not part-shade. Strengthen it with scale: 241 reasons carry "a canopy to plant beneath", 171 of them for a full-sun subject, and 65 of those put the full-sun subject under a woody Shrub/Tree/Conifer (ageratum -> lilac, blue-fescue -> lilac, calibrachoa -> viburnum, four-oclock -> crabapple, dill -> crabapple, ...). The proposed fix (suppress canopy phrasing when the shorter plant is full-sun and the taller is a dense woody Tree/Shrub, falling back to "taller at X m, for the layer behind it") is the right shape, and note that the same suppression is needed on the mirrored branches ("to carpet the ground beneath / beneath it").

title: The layering clause is inverted: a 1 m false indigo is called "a canopy to plant beneath", while a real 7 m dogwood over a fern gets no reason at all
subject: bacopa
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; a soft colour beside the white.
why: Two failures of the same clause, in opposite directions. (a) tallEnoughToShelter only requires tierOf(max) >= 2, i.e. taller than 0.9 m, so any 1 m perennial or grass clears it: bacopa is told to plant beneath a 1 m Baptisia (also beneath 1 m Helenium, 1 m Little Bluestem, 1 m Northern Sea Oats, 1.5 m Feather Reed Grass). Nobody plants beneath a false indigo - it is a solid 0.9 m-wide dome that would simply smother a 0.15 m trailing annual, and the file's own comment says "nobody carpets the ground beneath a black-eyed Susan", which is exactly what the guard fails to prevent. (b) Where the canopy relationship is genuinely the point, the CLAUSE_FLOOR of 0.5 deletes it: the canopy taper 0.6 - 0.2t drops below 0.5 once the ratio exceeds 8, so autumn-fern -> dogwood-tree (7 m over 0.5 m, ratio 14) loses its layering clause and the entire printed reason becomes "Shares part shade and moderate water." - a #3 recommendation that states no benefit whatsoever, when "a fern to underplant a dogwood" is the one thing worth saying about it.
fix: Require the taller plant to be woody (Tree/Conifer/Shrub) AND tier 3 (> 3 m) before using canopy/carpet phrasing, so 1 m perennials get "taller at 1 m, for the layer behind it"; and either exempt the canopy band from CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering or raise the taper's floor above 0.5 so a genuine tree-over-underplanting keeps its clause.
scope: 5 of bacopa's 8 companions use the false canopy phrasing; the suppressed-clause half hits every tree-over-groundcover pairing above an 8:1 ratio (autumn-fern -> dogwood-tree here).
notes:
Finding survives, with one factual correction that must be carried into the report. The finding says the file's own comment ("nobody carpets the ground beneath a black-eyed Susan") "is exactly what the guard fails to prevent." That is wrong: black-eyed-susan is filed at h=0.8, so tierOf() = 1 and the guard DOES catch its own named example. The accurate (and sharper) statement is that the guard clears its stated counter-example by only 0.1 m and fails for anything just above 0.9 m, which is where the 1 m perennials and grasses sit.

Two quantifications worth adding, since they show this is systematic rather than anecdotal: the "a canopy to plant beneath" clause fires on a NON-woody taller plant in 130 of 2,208 pairs versus 111 on a woody one (so most canopy claims in the catalog are false); and 162 pairs place a >3 m structure plant over a much shorter one while printing no layering clause at all, because the canopy taper (0.6 down to 0.4) is cut in half by CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering = 0.5 at any ratio above 8.

Severity stays medium: nothing dies from this, but it is user-visible design instruction to a beginner, wrong in both directions, and it strips the one useful sentence from the catalog's best underplanting pairs. The proposed fix (require woody AND tier 3 for canopy phrasing; exempt the canopy band from CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering or lift the taper's floor above 0.5) is correct and addresses both directions.

Verified against review-packets.json and compatibility.ts. Keep at medium: no plant dies, but a beginner is told to plant a 0.15 m trailing annual beneath a dense 0.9 m-wide false indigo (where it would get no light), and the one classic recommendation in the fern's list (a fern under a flowering dogwood) prints with no stated benefit. TWO REQUIRED CORRECTIONS TO THE WRITE-UP: (1) Drop or fix the sentence claiming the file's own black-eyed-Susan comment "is exactly what the guard fails to prevent" - it is false. Black-eyed Susan is h=0.8, so tierOf(0.8)=1 and the guard catches it. The accurate statement is that the guard is set one notch too low: it succeeds on its own worked example at 0.8 m and fails immediately above, at the 1.0 m Baptisia/Helenium/Little Bluestem/Northern Sea Oats band. (2) The title says the dogwood "gets no reason at all"; strictly it gets a conditions-only reason ("Shares part shade and moderate water."), which the body states correctly. Reword the title to "gets a reason that states no benefit". STRENGTHENING DATA to add: the mis-fire is systemic, not anecdotal - 124 pairings catalog-wide print "a canopy to plant beneath" for a herbaceous (Perennial/Grass/Annual/Bulb/Fern) companion, the shortest at 1.0 m; and all 15 of the catalog's benefit-free reasons are canopy-taper casualties, including holly-fern -> dogwood-tree at #2 and italian-cypress -> gaura at #1. The proposed fix (require woody + tier 3 for canopy phrasing; exempt or raise the floor for the canopy band) addresses both directions correctly.

Verified against data: the phrase "a canopy to plant beneath" appears in 241 of 2,208 pairs; in 130 the taller plant is herbaceous (Perennial 52, Grass 48, Annual 20, Herb 6, Bulb 3, Fern 1) — those are the wrong ones (ajuga -> northern-sea-oats, ajuga -> ostrich-fern, aloe -> pampas-grass, bacopa -> baptisia/helenium/little-bluestem). Scope the fix to those: the 66 sub-3 m SHRUB cases (azalea 1.2 m, camellia 2 m, hydrangea 1.5 m) are defensible underplantings and should not be swept up, so the guard wants "woody category" rather than a blanket tier-3 height test. The mirrored "carpet the ground beneath" fires on 237 pairs with a sub-3 m non-tree subject (the canopy-wording lens catches part of this). Half (b) should be stated more modestly: the taper is documented design intent and only 15 reasons in the whole set end with no factor clause, so the harm there is presentational (a benefit-free #3 recommendation), not horticultural.

title: Basil is filed at zones 10-11, so its entire companion list is zone-10/11 tropicals ('they only overlap in zones 10 to 11')
subject: basil
claim: Basil: cat 'Herb', zones '10-11'. Cards read: 'Shares full sun and moderate water; both offer pollinator forage; climbs to 3 m behind it, height without the footprint; they only overlap in zones 10 to 11.' (same closing clause on dill, black-eyed-susan-vine, moonflower, tropical-hibiscus)
why: Basil (Ocimum basilicum) is a frost-tender annual that is grown as a summer annual in every US zone; '10-11' is its perennial-survival range, not its growing range. Because the engine only skips the zone hard filter for category 'Annual' and basil is filed as 'Herb', the filter forces every companion to intersect zones 10-11. The result is that a beginner in zone 6 who plants basil every June is told its best partners are Mandevilla, Tropical Hibiscus, Moonflower and Thunbergia (none of which overwinter for her, and all of which she must also buy as annuals), and that basil and DILL - two herbs that share a bed in millions of gardens - 'only overlap in zones 10 to 11'. That last sentence is simply false as a piece of gardening advice. Dill is already handled correctly (zones 3-11); basil is the outlier that poisons its own list.
fix: Give basil the same treatment as the other tender annuals: either set cat 'Annual' (the zone hard filter is skipped for annuals) or widen zones to '2-11' as with dill. Same check for any other Herb row filed at a tender-perennial zone range.
scope: All 8 of basil's pairings; 5 of them carry the false 'only overlap in zones 10 to 11' clause. Any Herb-category tender annual filed at its hardiness zones rather than its growing zones.
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The title's "entire companion list is zone-10/11 tropicals" is not accurate: basil has 8 companions and the top three (sunflower, cleome, sweet-alyssum) are cat Annual, pass with the zone test skipped, and get clean, sensible reasons. The correct statement is that every NON-annual companion basil can have must intersect zones 10-11, so 5 of its 8 cards (dill, black-eyed-susan-vine, mandevilla, moonflower, tropical-hibiscus) carry the narrow-zone penalty and the sentence "they only overlap in zones 10 to 11". (2) "wrong-library-data" is half right: zones "10-11" is the conventional published hardiness rating for Ocimum basilicum, so the value is defensible in isolation. The actual defect is the mismatch between it and the engine's annual exemption, which keys on `category === "Annual"` and ignores `years: 1`. Basil is the only years:1 herb in the library given a tender-perennial band (parsley is years:1 / 5-9, dill years:1 / 3-11), which is why it is the outlier. Also: lead the finding with basil -> dill, not basil -> mandevilla. Basil and mandevilla are both full-sun, moderate-water summer annuals in the north and would in fact grow happily side by side - that pair is not botanically harmful, it just carries a misleading zone caution. Basil -> dill is the exhibit where the generated sentence is flatly wrong as advice, and the silently excluded lavender/thyme/sage/oregano are the real cost. Severity medium rather than high: nothing recommended here will fail to thrive, and the fix is one data row.

RE-ANCHOR the finding on pair basil -> dill, not basil -> mandevilla. Verified facts: plantLibrary.ts:2971 basil = cat "Herb", years: 1, zones "10-11"; line 2973 dill = cat "Herb", years: 1, zones "3-11" (same lifespan, incompatible zone convention). compatibility.ts:472 `isAnnual = (p) => p.category === "Annual"` - lifespan (yearsToMaturity: 1) is loaded into CompatPlant but never used by the annual test, so basil's hardiness range is enforced as a hard filter (lines 553-558). Consequence: basil's dill card reads "Shares full sun and moderate water; both are strong pollinator plants; they only overlap in zones 10 to 11" (score 37.2, narrow-zone-overlap penalty) - a false-in-practice statement about the two annual herbs a beginner is most likely to plant together, and it also drags the pair's score down. The remaining four non-Annual slots are then forced to zone-10/11 tropicals (black-eyed-susan-vine, mandevilla, moonflower, tropical-hibiscus, all 36.7). DROP the claims that the whole list is tropical and that they are basil's "best" partners: the top three (sunflower, cleome, sweet alyssum) are fine, and the tropical cards do carry the zone caveat. Fix: make isAnnual also true for yearsToMaturity === 1 (or align basil's zones with dill's 3-11), and audit other Herb rows with years: 1 filed at a tender-perennial zone range.

Rewrite around the real defect. Basil (plantLibrary.ts:2971) is cat "Herb", zones "10-11"; compatibility.ts:472 exempts only cat "Annual" from the zone hard filter, so basil - a plant grown as a summer annual in every US zone - is filtered on its perennial-survival band. Two consequences, both verified in the packet: (1) the reason clause "they only overlap in zones 10 to 11" is appended to basil -> dill, basil -> black-eyed-susan-vine, mandevilla, moonflower and tropical-hibiscus; on basil -> dill it is actively misleading, since a beginner reads it as "this combination only works in zones 10-11" when the two share a bed in millions of temperate gardens. (2) Basil's true bed-mates are hard-blocked because they cannot intersect 10-11: chives (3-9), thyme (4-9), oregano (4-9), sage (4-8), parsley (5-9). Do NOT claim the whole list is tropical: the top three (the only ones shown inline, INLINE_FULL = 3 in PlantCompanions.tsx) are sunflower, cleome and sweet-alyssum, all fine, all zone-clause-free. Do NOT claim basil -> mandevilla is a bad pair; it is culturally fine (full sun, moderate water, both grown as tender summer plants). Fix caution: cat "Annual" would drop basil out of the Herb browse filter, and "2-11" would be false hardiness data. The correct fix is a treat-as-annual lifecycle signal the zone filter reads, applied to any tender-annual Herb row.

title: Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii) is recommended to Basket of Gold with no invasiveness caution, and told to be planted over it
subject: basket-of-gold
claim: 'Shares full sun and low water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; purple against yellow, for contrast.'
why: Buddleja davidii (the straight species, which is what 'Butterfly Bush / Buddleia davidii' in the library denotes) is a listed noxious weed in Oregon and Washington, where sale of the species is prohibited and only approved sterile cultivars may be sold, and it is on invasive-plant lists across the Pacific Northwest and mid-Atlantic. It escapes by wind-borne seed into riverbanks and disturbed ground. The library tags it 'pollinator' and the taxonomy table gives buddleia pollinator:'high', so the engine actively rewards it (+20) for the nectar trait that markets it, while it is a nectar trap that hosts no native larvae. Nothing in the reason, the tags, or the score warns the user. Secondarily, the 'a canopy to plant beneath' clause tells the beginner to plant Aurinia saxatilis - a full-sun rock-garden alpine that needs baking sun and sharp drainage - under a 2 m shrub, where it will stretch, flop, and rot.
fix: Add an 'invasive' / regional-caution flag to the library (Buddleja davidii, and check the rest of the shrub list against it) and either exclude flagged plants from the companion pool or force a caution clause into the reason; at minimum retitle the entry to a sterile cultivar (for example 'Lo & Behold') rather than the species.
scope: Every list butterfly-bush appears in as a companion; it is an archetype so it can be recommended to any full-sun, low-water subject.
notes: Two corrections must be carried into the report. (A) DROP the "praised for the very trait that makes it invasive" argument FROM THIS PAIR. The +20 pollinator factor does NOT fire on basket-of-gold -> butterfly-bush: the rendered reason contains no pollinator clause because basket-of-gold has no forage value (tags: perennial|full sun|spring|yellow|edging|drought tolerant), so pollinatorClause returns null and the factor scores 0. invasive-recount.json shows butterfly-bush is "praised" on only 6 of its 42 lists. The claim is true of the engine in general but false of this pair. The defect here is plainer and still high-severity: a regulated noxious weed recommended to a beginner with zero caution and a one-tap "Add to garden" button. (B) SOFTEN the underplanting sub-claim: "stretch, flop, and rot" is wrong on rot - both plants want the same sharp drainage, so there is no moisture conflict, and Buddleja davidii is deciduous, cut hard in late winter, and leafs out late, so Aurinia's April-May bloom happens over a bare stub. The real cost is summer shade plus shrub root competition shortening the life of an already short-lived alpine. Keep as a minor supporting point, not a mechanism. (C) Sharpen the fix: the proposed "add an invasive flag to the library" is more expensive than needed - the repo ALREADY curates the regulated-weed fact per-plant (curated/butterfly-bush.json). The cheap fix is to wire that existing curated data into the engine (exclude flagged plants from the companion pool, or force a caution clause via cautionClause, compatibility.ts:1146) and surface it on the companion card, which today shows nothing. Supporting scale: butterfly-bush appears on 42 companion lists, 0 cautioned.

Keep the finding, but re-anchor and rewrite it. Correct pair to anchor on: creeping-phlox -> butterfly-bush (score 79.7, top of that plant's list), whose reason reads "...stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months" - that is where the engine genuinely praises the nectar trait of a plant that is a Class B noxious weed in Washington and quarantine-listed in Oregon. Six such pollinator-praised butterfly-bush pairs exist (allium, creeping-phlox, grape-hyacinth, linden, sage, sea-thrift); basket-of-gold is NOT one of them (pollinator scored 0 there). Drop the "+20 pollinator reward" and "praised for the trait" language from the basket-of-gold framing, and drop the "full-sun alpine under a shrub" sub-claim (generic layering clause, appears identically for the grasses on the same list). Restate the defect as: the compatibility engine models no invasiveness/regional-regulation signal (cautionClause only handles `spreader`), so butterfly bush is offered on 42 companion lists with 0 cautions and an Add-to-garden button, even though the product already holds the sourced noxious-weed caution one card away in src/garden/intel/curated/butterfly-bush.json. The fix is to surface the existing curated caution in the companion card (or exclude regulated species from the archetype pool / swap the group default to a sterile cultivar), not to invent a new flag from scratch.

Survives, but reframe and downgrade. Verified: chunks/chunk-03.json, subject basket-of-gold, companion butterfly-bush, score 56.3, reason exactly as quoted; it renders as an 8th-of-8 card (behind "Show all" after a premium reveal) with a one-tap "Add to garden" and no caution (invasive-recount.json: 42 lists, 0 cautioned). The real defect is narrower than filed: the companion engine never consults the invasiveness caution the repo ALREADY holds for this plant (curated/butterfly-bush.json's free-tier "Regulated noxious weed" fact), so the recommendation surface contradicts the app's own plant card. Drop the "+20 pollinator reward / praised for the trait that makes it invasive" claim (the pollinator factor scored zero here) and drop the "retitle to a sterile cultivar" fix (the library entry is already accurate and sourced). Drop the underplanting sub-claim as a generic template artifact. The correct fix is to feed the existing curated invasive flag into compatibility.ts as either an exclusion or a forced caution clause. Also note this pair is a weak exemplar: the higher-harm instances of the same defect are pampas grass (191 lists, praised for the seed heads that spread it) and maiden grass (68 lists).

title: Trumpet Vine is recommended to Bee Balm with zero caution because BOTH are flagged spreaders, so the caution cancels itself out
subject: bee-balm
claim: 'Shares full sun and moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; both are strong pollinator plants; both are native plants.' penalties: []
why: Campsis radicans is an 8 m woody vine that suckers several metres from the crown, resprouts from severed roots, and is the plant extension services most often tell home gardeners to site away from beds and structures. Bee Balm is a 0.9 m soft herbaceous runner. The engine has the data to warn (taxonomy: campsis spreader:true) but isVulnerableTo() at compatibility.ts:504 returns false when the neighbour is itself a spreader (monarda spreader:true) - 'equal vigour, let them fight' - so the pair emits NO caution at all, and the only value clause the beginner sees is the praise 'both are native plants'. A woody suckering vine and a herbaceous perennial are not equal vigour: the Monarda will be gone in three seasons and the Campsis will still be coming up in the lawn. Note the same rule silently suppressed the caution on bee-balm + snow-in-summer (cerastium spreader:true).
fix: In isVulnerableTo(), do not cancel the caution when the spreader is woody (Climber / Shrub / Tree) and the neighbour is herbaceous - a shared spreader flag should never mean 'no warning'. Regardless, campsis should always carry an explicit 'suckers widely, hard to remove' caution clause.
scope: Every pairing where subject and companion are both spreader:true; the woody-vs-herbaceous cases (campsis, wisteria-class climbers) are the dangerous subset.
notes: Keep the finding, but tighten the wording: (1) Campsis radicans is NATIVE to the eastern US, so the charge is "aggressive root-suckering habit", not "invasive in much of the US"; and it is praised for nativity/pollinator value, not for the spreading trait, so drop "praised for the very trait that makes it invasive". (2) Drop the rhetorical "the Monarda will be gone in three seasons". (3) "Zero caution" is true of the pairing REASON; the vine's own detail card does show a generic tip (careTips.ts:218, "Spreads vigorously ... plan to divide it"), which is itself wrong advice for a woody vine. (4) Strengthen the mechanism: OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION is 0.6, so without monarda's own spreader flag this pair would have been BLOCKED outright by spreaderSmothers (0.9 <= 0.6 x 8); line 504 converts the engine's own hard smother verdict into a clean, unpenalised recommendation. Every clause in the emitted reason is individually true, so the defect is the bypassed blocker plus the missing caution, not a false statement.

One mechanical correction to carry into the report: line 504 (isVulnerableTo) is why the pair SURVIVES the hard filter at all; the missing caution text is suppressed one step later, by the same !p.spreader rule inside soft() at compatibility.ts:518 (spreaderNeedsWatching), which gates the cautionClause. Cite both lines, not only 504. Note also that the "b.spreader" branch of cautionClause is the one lost - i.e. the warning that would have been about the VINE is cancelled by the neighbour's own spreader flag. Worth adding: the repo's own curated intel for this plant (src/garden/intel/curated/trumpet-vine.json) already carries a free-tier fact "Spreads hard; contain it - it suckers from underground runners and self-seeds into dense colonies that choke out neighbors, and it is hard to remove once established" (source USDA), so the recommendation contradicts the product's own verified fact by omission. And the suppression is systemic, not a one-off: trumpet-vine is recommended to 15 subjects and emits no caution in a single one of them.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The reason is not solely "both are native plants": it has three value clauses (bloom succession, pollinator forage, native). The defect is the ZERO caution, not the clause count; drop "the Monarda will be gone in three seasons" as unverifiable rhetoric. (2) Strengthen the mechanism and the breadth: had bee-balm not been spreader-flagged, isVulnerableTo would have returned true (0.9 m <= OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION 0.6 x 8 m) and the pair would have been a `spreader-smothers` HARD BLOCK, so line 504 upgrades a hard exclusion into an uncautioned recommendation. The same rule leaves trumpet-vine with no spreader caution in ALL 15 companion lists it appears in, including lemon-balm (0.6 m herb, melissa spreader-flagged) where it is ranked #1. Also worth noting for the fix: the companion mini-card size line shows only mature SPREAD ("Climber · ⌀ 2 m"), never the 8 m height.

title: Bee Balm paired with Snow-in-Summer: one needs steady summer moisture, the other rots in it
subject: bee-balm
claim: 'Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late spring, before this one opens; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; lower at 0.15 m, for the front of the border.'
why: The one-rung water slide (low <-> moderate) hides a real conflict here. Monarda is a moisture-loving native of damp meadows: it needs consistently moist, fertile soil and collapses into powdery mildew the moment it is dry-stressed, so it gets watered through summer. Cerastium tomentosum is a Mediterranean alpine mat that demands lean, gritty, sharply drained soil and is notorious for melting out in the centre in a wet or humid summer, exactly the regime Bee Balm requires. On a single watering schedule at the front of the same border, one of the two is being mistreated. Cerastium is also a mat-former (taxonomy has it spreader:true) that will run over the crown of its neighbour, and 'evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter' oversells a grey mat that looks bedraggled in a zone-4 winter.
fix: Do not let water slide by a rung when one partner is a rot-prone, sharp-drainage alpine/Mediterranean mat (cerastium, aubrieta, aurinia, delosperma) and the other is a moisture-dependent perennial. A 'needs sharp drainage' flag on those genera, treated as a hard filter against 'moderate/high water' partners, would drop this pair.
scope: This pair, plus the same sharp-drainage-vs-moisture pattern wherever a low-water alpine is slid onto a moderate-water subject (compare beautyberry -> rock-cress, beautyberry -> candytuft in this chunk).
refutations:
The finding survives neither the botany nor the harm test.

1) It conflates a species with the genus that is actually filed. The library entry is `botanical: "Monarda"` (genus-level), water "moderate", zones 3-9. The finding's premise - "Monarda is a moisture-loving native of damp meadows [that] needs consistently moist soil" - is Monarda didyma. Monarda fistulosa, equally inside the filed genus, is a dry upland-prairie plant that is drought-tolerant. "Moderate" is the correct genus-level value, and a moderate-water perennial is not a bog plant. The "one of the two is being mistreated" argument only works if you silently substitute M. didyma for the entry the engine actually scored.

2) The water gap is the engine's smallest, explicitly-documented, disclosed trade-off - and the harmful version of it is already hard-blocked. compatibility.ts sets MAX_WATER_STEPS = 1 and comments at the penalty site: "Neighbouring water needs are workable, but 'group similar water needs' is the guidance, so a pair that shares a rung outranks one that straddles two." Low-vs-high (xeric next to bog, the real conflict) is a `water-mismatch` BLOCKER. This pair took the `water-differs` penalty (x0.92), and the reason text itself tells the beginner "Shares full sun and LOW TO MODERATE water". Nothing is hidden. Cerastium is also zone-capped at 7, which already excludes the hot-humid-summer belt where melt-out and crown rot are the notorious failure. A silver mat edging a sunny, moderate-water mixed border in zones 3-7 is ordinary, widely-practiced planting, not a documented poor combination - there is no horticultural source anywhere that warns against Monarda near Cerastium.

3) The spreader sub-claim is backwards. The finding is right that taxonomy.ts:277 has `cerastium: { spreader: true }` - but taxonomy.ts:79 has `monarda: { ... spreader: true ... }` as well, which is why no penalty fired: isVulnerableTo() returns false when the neighbour is itself a spreader ("equal vigour, let them fight"). That is a deliberate, reasoned rule, and reality agrees with it: Monarda is a 0.9 m rhizomatous running thug, Cerastium a 0.15 m mat. If either crowds the other's crown it is the bee balm crowding the snow-in-summer, not the reverse. The finding argues the opposite.

4) The "evergreen" clause is not false. Cerastium tomentosum is evergreen to semi-evergreen (NC State / MoBot: silver foliage evergreen in mild winters), and the flag is a curated high-confidence taxonomy entry, not an invention. "Looks bedraggled in a zone-4 winter" is a cosmetic quibble that harms no one.

5) MATTERS test: the worst realistic outcome is that a cheap, fast, famously short-lived groundcover - filed years:2, and universally advised to be sheared after bloom and divided every 2-3 years anyway - performs a little less well at the front of a bed on a moderate-water schedule. No plant dies of an incompatibility; no beginner is led into a costly or irreversible mistake. The proposed fix would be actively worse than the bug: hard-filtering cerastium/aubrieta/aurinia/delosperma against every moderate-water partner would gut those plants' companion lists and ban standard practice (sedum, delosperma and aubrieta edging a moderate-water sunny border).
notes: Keep the finding, but restrict it to the water/drainage culture conflict, which the repo's own deepIntel files independently confirm (snow-in-summer: rots in soil that stays wet, needs sharp drainage; bee-balm/M. didyma: wants consistently moist soil, and moist roots are a main powdery-mildew control). The misleading text is the opening clause "Shares full sun and low to moderate water," which presents a genuine conflict as shared culture; the bloom-succession and height clauses are accurate. Drop the two weak sub-claims: (a) "Cerastium will run over the crown of its neighbour" - Monarda is itself spreader:true (taxonomy.ts:79), six times taller, and more aggressive; the engine intentionally treats two spreaders as equal vigour (compatibility.ts:504), so no spreader penalty is a design choice, not an oversight; (b) the "evergreen oversells" point - NC State does class the foliage as broadleaf evergreen (per deepIntel/snow-in-summer.json), so the clause is defensible. Also do not call water:moderate on the bee-balm archetype "wrong": the repo's own intel says Monarda's water need is species-dependent and cannot be captured by one value, so moderate is an acknowledged genus-level compromise. Severity stays medium: in a sharply drained sandy bed both can persist, so this is a culture conflict a beginner will get wrong, not a certain kill.

Correct one sub-claim before publishing. The finding says Cerastium "will run over the crown of its neighbour"; the direction is backwards and the engine's behaviour is different from what the finding implies. Monarda is ALSO spreader:true (taxonomy.ts:79), and isVulnerableTo returns false when the neighbour is itself a spreader ("equal vigour, let them fight", compatibility.ts:504), so neither spreaderSmothers nor spreaderNeedsWatching fires and the pair carries NO spread caution at all. Horticulturally the 0.9 m rhizomatous mint-family runner swamps the 15 cm mat, not the reverse. Reframe as: the beginner is told to put a sharp-drainage, melt-out-prone alpine mat at the front of a moisture-dependent Monarda, on one watering regime, with no spread warning because the "both are spreaders" rule cancels it. The headline water/soil-drainage conflict (low vs moderate, x0.92 water-differs only) stands as written.

If any residue is kept at all, it is not this pair. The only defensible kernel is a wording question that is general, not pair-specific: the disclosure phrase "Shares full sun and low to moderate water" reads as a shared tolerance band rather than as a difference, so it never tells the gardener to site the drier partner at the drier edge. That is a template-copy suggestion for every water-differs pair (the engine already knows the penalty fired), not a botanically questionable pairing, and per the audit brief a general engine property with no named broken pair does not count as a finding.

title: Colour clauses misname the flower: an orange Asiatic Lily 'echoes the pink', and yellow Black-Eyed Susan is described as orange
subject: bergenia
claim: bergenia -> lily-asiatic: '...taller at 1 m, for the layer behind it; echoes the pink.' (the lily's own card is tagged 'orange')
why: colorName() (src/browse/plantFacets.ts:83-95) derives the colour word from the bloom hex, and its thresholds misfile two plants in this chunk. Asiatic Lily's hex #e8643c is a burnt orange/vermilion: hue ~14 deg falls under the 'h < 16' red/pink branch, and its lightness (0.572) just clears the 'l > 0.55' cut, so it is named PINK. The user sees a lily labelled Orange, next to a sentence saying it 'echoes the pink' of a pink Bergenia. The same function names Black-Eyed Susan's golden #f4b400 (hue ~44.3 deg, just under the 45 deg yellow boundary) ORANGE, so Rudbeckia's own cards read 'blue against orange, for contrast' (sage) and 'purple against orange, for contrast' (allium) for a flower the library itself tags 'yellow' and everyone on earth calls yellow. A colour sentence that contradicts the colour chip beside it destroys trust in the whole card, and in the lily's case it also gives false design advice (an orange lily does not harmonise with pink Bergenia; it clashes).
fix: Tighten colorName(): move the yellow/orange boundary up to ~35 deg and lower the red/pink lightness cut (or the h<16 boundary to ~10) so saturated coral-oranges do not read as pink. Better: when the species carries an explicit colour tag ('orange', 'yellow'), prefer the tag over the hex so the reason can never contradict the label the user is looking at.
scope: Any plant whose bloom hex has hue 16-45 deg (named orange) or hue <16 with L>0.55 (named pink). Confirmed here on lily-asiatic (#e8643c) and black-eyed-susan (#f4b400), which alone affects the colour clause on 2 of black-eyed-susan's 8 cards.
notes: Trim one overstatement: the added claim that "an orange lily does not harmonise with pink Bergenia; it clashes" is a taste assertion and is moot in this pair anyway, because Bergenia blooms Apr-May and the Asiatic Lily Jun-Jul (the same sentence says "blooms in early summer as this one finishes"), so the two flowers are never open together. Report the defect as what it verifiably is: the reason names a colour the plant does not have, contradicting the card's own tag and photo (an orange lily "echoes the pink"; a yellow Rudbeckia described as orange twice). That is a wrong-reason/trust defect, not a bad planting, which is exactly where medium belongs. The proposed fix should lead with "prefer the species' explicit colour tag over the hex when one exists" (that alone fixes both named cases); the threshold tweak (yellow/orange boundary up to ~35 deg, red/pink boundary down to ~10 deg) is the secondary safety net for untagged plants.

Confirmed verbatim in data/chunks/chunk-03.json.
