Two wording corrections for the report: (1) the detail card does not render an "orange" colour chip (PlantDetail.tsx:206 filters tag chips to HUB_TAGS); the visible contradiction is the companion mini-card's BloomBar, whose dots are painted from #e8643c (clearly orange) immediately above the "echoes the pink" sentence, plus Browse, where plantColors() lists the lily under BOTH "pink" (from the hex) and "orange" (from the tag). Phrase it as "contradicts the orange bloom strip right above it" rather than "the colour chip beside it". (2) Soften the clash claim: Bergenia (Apr-May) and Asiatic Lily (Jun-Jul) barely co-bloom, so the harm is primarily a false/incoherent reason, not ruined planting. The Black-Eyed Susan case is arguably the more damaging one for beginner trust: the engine calls the definitive yellow garden flower "orange" in two of its top-3 reasons, for a plant the library itself tags "yellow".

Confirmed verbatim in data. bergenia -> lily-asiatic (chunks/chunk-03.json, score 60.5): companion tags "bulb|full sun|summer|orange", reason "Takes full sun to part sun and moderate water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; taller at 1 m, for the layer behind it; echoes the pink." Black-eyed-susan as SUBJECT (tags "...|yellow|pollinator|native|...") ships "blue against orange, for contrast" (sage) and "purple against orange, for contrast" (allium). Replicated colorName(): #e8643c -> h=13.95/s=0.789/l=0.573 -> pink; #f4b400 -> h=44.26 -> orange; #d6507a -> h=341.19 -> pink.

TWO ADDITIONS TO CARRY INTO THE REPORT:
1. plantColors() (src/browse/plantFacets.ts:97-102) UNIONS the hex-derived name with the explicit tag, so lily-asiatic is filed under BOTH "pink" and "orange" - an orange lily surfaces in a "pink" colour filter. This is an extra user-visible symptom the finding missed, and it strengthens the proposed fix (prefer the explicit tag over the hex).
2. The bergenia/lily colour clause is doubly hollow: the two barely co-bloom (bergenia 4-5, lily 6-7) and the same reason says "blooms in early summer as this one finishes" - so the card asserts a colour echo between flowers essentially never open at the same time.

Severity medium holds: not plant-killing, but a systematic wrong-reason bug (two whole colour classes misfiled) producing self-contradicting cards on a marquee plant.

title: 'Carpet the ground beneath' / 'a canopy to plant beneath' is applied to 1-1.5 m herbaceous plants, sending sun-obligate succulents and alpines into shade
subject: baptisia
claim: baptisia -> ice-plant: 'Shares full sun and low water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; low at 0.1 m, to carpet the ground beneath.' (same clause for portulaca, crocus, scilla; mirrored on basket-of-gold -> switchgrass: 'stands well above at 1.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath')
why: layeringClause (compatibility.ts:1046-1078) gates this language on tallEnoughToShelter, but the tier boundary passes at 1.0 m, so a 1 m perennial qualifies as a canopy - the exact thing the code's own comment says must not happen ('nobody carpets the ground beneath a black-eyed Susan'). A mature Baptisia is a dense 1 m x 1 m shrub-like dome with a deep taproot (the taxonomy table even marks it noDivide, 'resents disturbance'): there is no usable, lit ground beneath it, and you cannot dig around it later to plant one. Telling a beginner to carpet that footprint with Delosperma or Portulaca - full-sun succulents that stretch, stay wet, and rot in shade - is advice that kills the companion. The mirror case is worse: Basket of Gold, a full-sun rock-garden alpine, is told to be planted BENEATH a 1.5 m switchgrass (also feather-reed-grass, perennial-sunflower, and the 2 m butterfly bush), where it will be shaded out.
fix: Raise tallEnoughToShelter so canopy/carpet language needs a genuinely overhead plant (roughly >=2 m, or a woody Shrub/Tree/Conifer category); below that, fall back to 'lower at X m, for the front of the border' / 'taller at X m, for the layer behind it'. Additionally, suppress the underplanting clause entirely when the shorter plant is full-sun and the taller one is dense.
scope: Every subject in the 1.0-2.0 m band paired with a sub-0.2 m companion, and every sub-0.25 m subject paired with a 1.5 m+ companion. Confirmed on 4 of baptisia's 8 cards and 4 of basket-of-gold's 8.
notes: Trim two weak examples. (1) crocus and scilla beneath Baptisia is actually defensible: spring bulbs finish and go dormant before the late-emerging clump fills out, and interplanting bulbs around late-emerging perennials is standard practice. Anchor the finding on the year-round sun-obligate mats instead: ice-plant (Delosperma), portulaca, creeping-phlox. (2) butterfly-bush at 2 m is a borderline-legitimate canopy (leggy, hard-pruned) and is the weakest mirror example; the grasses (switchgrass 1.5 m, feather-reed-grass 1.5 m) are the damning ones, because a grass tussock has no underplantable canopy at all. Also state the gate precisely: tier 2 starts above MIDDLE_TIER_M = 0.9 m, so the floor sits far below the intent the code comment encodes (a 0.6 m black-eyed Susan is in fact excluded; the leak is the 0.9-2 m band, 326 of 525 "beneath" reasons).

Two precision fixes for the report. (1) The code's own named example IS caught: black-eyed-susan is h = 0.8 (tier 1). The bug is that the tier boundary sits at 0.9 m, so a 1.0 m Baptisia - the same dense herbaceous full-sun habit - slips through by 0.1 m. Phrase it as "the guard fails its own stated principle at 1.0 m", not "the code does the exact thing its comment forbids". (2) The taxonomy comment for baptisia is "deep taproot, rarely needs division" (taxonomy.ts:163); "resents disturbance" is the gloss on peony/milkweed. Also worth noting the blast radius is larger than the pairs named: with ice-plant or portulaca as the subject, 5-6 of 8 companions carry "a canopy to plant beneath" (rosemary 1 m, echinops 1 m, little-bluestem 1 m, fountain-grass 1 m, spirea 1 m, boxwood 1.2 m).

Keep the finding, but reframe it as a wording/copy defect, not a plant-killer. Correct facts to carry into the report: the canopy/carpet clause fires when tierOf(max height) >= 2, i.e. above MIDDLE_TIER_M = 0.9 m (the Missouri back-of-border band), AND the height ratio exceeds CANOPY_RATIO = 6. The code's own black-eyed Susan example (h = 0.8) is correctly excluded, so do not claim the gate contradicts its own comment. Drop crocus and scilla from the victim list entirely: spring ephemerals under a late-emerging Baptisia is best practice, and the engine's bulb-cover factor already models it. Drop the "kills the companion" and "cannot dig around it later" framing. The accurate defect: for 0.9-3 m herbaceous subjects (Baptisia 1.0 m, agapanthus 1.0 m, switchgrass 1.5 m, feather-reed-grass 1.5 m, roses at 1.5 m) the crown occupies its own footprint, so there is no plantable lit ground "beneath"; the phrase is horticulturally meaningless there and nudges full-sun mats (ice-plant, portulaca, basket-of-gold) into a clump's shade where they will bloom poorly. The audit's canopy-wording lens already flags all 1222 instances at low severity. The proposed fix direction (require a genuinely overhead plant, roughly >= 2 m or a woody Shrub/Tree/Conifer, else fall back to "for the front of the border" / "for the layer behind it") is sound.

title: Colorado Blue Spruce is given full-sun border perennials (Garden Phlox, Hollyhock, Obedient Plant) with a reason that states no benefit at all
subject: blue-spruce
claim: "Shares full sun and moderate water." (identical reason for obedient-plant and hollyhock)
why: Picea pungens in the library is 15 m tall with a 6 m spread and keeps its skirt of branches to the ground. Nothing planted with it is in full sun: the zone under and beside a mature blue spruce is dry, root-filled, needle-covered shade, and the sun/water fields the engine matched on describe the spruce's OWN requirement, not the conditions it creates. Garden Phlox (Phlox paniculata) and Hollyhock (Alcea rosea) are the two worst possible choices for that spot: both are notorious for powdery mildew and hollyhock rust respectively, and both depend on full sun plus free air movement to stay clean. Crowded at the skirt of a dense evergreen they will be shaded, still-aired and diseased within a season. The same list also offers Climbing Rose and Clematis as "an understory layer" under the spruce, which is the same error (sun-demanding plants sited in dry conifer shade, and no support named for either climber). Compounding it, because blue-spruce has no bloom it scores no bloom/pollinator/colour factor, so the generated sentence collapses to "Shares full sun and moderate water." - a recommendation that names zero benefit, which is worse than no recommendation.
fix: Treat a Tree/Conifer subject above ~6 m as casting shade and drying its root zone: for those subjects, only recommend companions whose sun tolerance includes part-shade/full-shade and which tolerate dry root competition (e.g. epimedium, Christmas fern, hellebore, hosta), and drop any companion tagged mildew/rust-prone. Separately, suppress a recommendation whose reason reduces to sun+water only - if no positive factor scored, do not show the pair.
scope: All 8 blue-spruce pairings in this chunk; systemic for any Tree/Conifer subject over ~6 m
refutations: The reasons are quoted correctly, but the horticultural charge rests on a placement the engine never recommends and the product already guards against. (a) For phlox/hollyhock/obedient-plant the reason contains NO placement clause: layeringScore tapers 0.6 -> 0.4 as the height gap grows and phlox (0.40) / hollyhock (~0.50) fall below CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering = 0.5, so the engine deliberately declines to say "carpet the ground beneath" - the code comment names this exact case ("a shade tree as a bed companion for a groundcover") as what the taper exists to suppress. The finding supplies the "planted at the skirt of the spruce" scenario itself. (b) crowding.ts already encodes the spruce's real problem in the layer that owns it: CANOPY_MIN_CROWN_RATIO = 0.5, blue spruce is 6/15 = 0.40, so it is explicitly NOT a canopy ("There is no canopy to plant under, so they compete at ground level like a shrub"; the comment names spruce). A beginner who places phlox inside the 6 m footprint gets a crowding warning. (c) Placed as the app implies (outside the footprint), blue spruce + garden phlox is a textbook composition, not an error: zones 4-7 overlap, both full sun, both moderate water, an evergreen backdrop to a sunny perennial border. Mildew/rust follow from crowding and still air, which the crowding guard governs, not the companion list. (d) "Names zero benefit" is overstated: "Shares full sun and moderate water" is true and is the documented mandatory lead clause ("what makes the pair possible at all"), and the proposed fix's premise is factually wrong - factors DID score (layering 0.4, succession), they were withheld from the sentence by the clause floor, which is the file's stated ethic ("an invented reason is worse than no reason"). Suppressing conditions-only pairs would delete honest advice. The residue is a cosmetic "thin sentence" nit that the brief excludes as merely unexciting and the code documents as an accepted trade-off. No gardener is harmed by being told a spruce and a phlox want the same sun and the same watering.
notes: CONFIRMED in review-packets.json: subject blue-spruce (Picea pungens, Conifer, full-sun, moderate, z2-7, h=15 m, spread=6 m, no bloom) returns phlox (44.2), obedient-plant (44.2) and hollyhock (43.0) with the reason "Shares full sun and moderate water." and nothing more, plus ninebark "low at 2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it" and clematis / lilac / climbing-rose / witch-hazel "shorter at 3 m, an understory layer". Traced through the engine, the empty reason is mechanical: succession = ONE_SIDED_BLOOM 0.5 (floor 0.55), layering = 0.6-0.2*1 = 0.4 at r=15 (floor 0.5), winterClause returns null because the SUBJECT is the evergreen half, pollinator 0 for a foliage subject. It is rare, not background noise: only 15 of 2208 pairs catalog-wide are conditions-only, and 12 of those sit on conifer subjects (italian-cypress 4, blue-spruce 3, douglas-fir 2, eastern-white-pine, hemlock, juniper). Reframe the report around two verified defects: the actively-wrong clauses are the PLACEMENT ones (ninebark, lilac, climbing-rose, clematis told to go beneath a 15 m limbs-to-the-ground spruce, where sun-demanding lilac and ninebark will be leggy and shy-flowering, and Phlox paniculata / Alcea rosea at the skirt lose the full sun and free air movement that keep mildew and rust off them); the phlox / hollyhock / obedient-plant lines are vacuous, naming no benefit at all, which the brief counts on its own. Exposure is one-way: blue-spruce does not appear on phlox's, hollyhock's or obedient-plant's own companion lists, so it is the Colorado Blue Spruce card (plus the four other conifers with the same shape) that carries the bad advice.

Correct the mechanism before publishing: the sentence is not empty because nothing scored. Succession returns ONE_SIDED_BLOOM = 0.5 against CLAUSE_FLOOR.succession = 0.55; winterStructure scores a full 1.0 (the spruce is evergreen) but winterClause (line 1106) suppresses the text when the subject is the structural half; layering scores 0.40 for phlox and 0.495 for hollyhock against CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering = 0.5. So the engine credits the pair roughly 15 winter-structure points and 8.8 layering points FOR UNDERPLANTING A 15 m SPRUCE, then suppresses every clause that would have said so, leaving a recommendation that names zero benefit. Also worth naming in the report: ninebark, the top-ranked companion (46.7), is told in plain words to "carpet the ground beneath it" - a full-sun 2 m shrub under a dense conifer - which shows the tool really does mean "plant this under the tree", closing the obvious defence that the pairings are only "same bed, somewhere".

If any residue is kept, it is cosmetic and low: the sentence for a bloomless structural subject can reduce to conditions only. That is by design (clause floors + "an invented reason is worse than no reason"), not a botanical defect, and the pairing itself (conifer backdrop, sunny border in front) is sound. Do not report it as a bad pairing, and do not adopt the proposed fix's "no positive factor scored" premise, which misreads the engine.

title: Monkshood (Aconitum) recommended into two lean, sharp-drainage grass plantings, with the reason claiming it takes "low to moderate water"
subject: blue-fescue
claim: "Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late summer as this one finishes; taller at 1.2 m, for the layer behind it; blue against yellow, for contrast." (same pairing recurs for blue-oat-grass)
why: Aconitum is one of the least drought-tolerant common perennials: it needs cool, humus-rich soil kept evenly moist all season and collapses in hot, dry, lean ground. Festuca glauca and Helictotrichon sempervirens are the opposite culture - gritty, lean, sharply drained soil, and they rot in soil kept rich and moist. There is no single watering schedule or soil prep that serves both, yet the 1-rung water rule passes the pair and the generated sentence actively tells a beginner monkshood is happy on "low to moderate water", which is false. Second, and independently: Aconitum is the most acutely poisonous plant in this chunk (aconitine is lethal by ingestion and is absorbed through broken skin while handling roots), and the library carries no toxicity flag and the card gives no caution. Autumn Crocus (colchicum, recommended under boxwood at 54.6 - colchicine, also fatal) is the same gap. Recommending these to a non-expert who may have children or pets, with no warning, is not advice I would sign off.
fix: Either raise Aconitum's water to "high", which makes the pair fail the 1-rung filter, or add a soil-moisture/drainage axis so "needs it moist" cannot be matched to "needs sharp drainage". Separately add a `toxic` flag to the library (aconitum, colchicum, foxglove, etc.) and surface a short caution line in the companion card whenever a flagged plant is recommended.
scope: blue-fescue -> monkshood and blue-oat-grass -> monkshood in this chunk; the missing toxicity flag is library-wide
refutations: The pairing exists as described (blue-fescue -> monkshood 55.4, blue-oat-grass -> monkshood 55.4, quoted reason verbatim), but all three charges fail. (a) The reason does not assert Aconitum takes low water: conditionsClause (compatibility.ts:1002-1019) prints a UNION range across the two plants whenever a field differs; the identical "low to moderate water" clause appears in blue-fescue -> lilac, -> viburnum and -> wisteria, which the auditor accepts. Reading it as a claim about monkshood alone is a misread of a corpus-wide template, not a monkshood-specific defect. (b) "No single watering schedule or soil prep serves both" is not true: MOBOT gives Festuca glauca "dry to MEDIUM, well-drained" and Aconitum "MEDIUM to wet, well-drained"; RHS lists Aconitum napellus as "moist but well-drained, full sun or partial shade". The ranges overlap at medium moisture with good drainage, and both are cool-summer plants. An average, well-drained, mulched border is inside both plants' published tolerance - each at the edge of its preference, neither outside its range. This is exactly the 1-rung case the engine documents, prices with the water-differs x0.92 penalty, and consequently ranks 4th of 8. The proposed fix (raise Aconitum to "high") is contradicted by RHS's "moist but well-drained", so "moderate" is a defensible library value, not a data error. (c) The toxicity half is not a pairing finding: the library has no toxicity axis for ANY of its 1,611 plants, monkshood/foxglove/colchicum are plantable and displayable regardless of the compatibility engine, and the generated sentence does not praise or expose the toxic trait (unlike the invasiveness example the brief allows). Bundling a product-wide missing-safety-flag feature request into a pair verdict is what inflates this to "high". Worst realistic outcome for a beginner following this card: monkshood underperforms in a leaner spot than it likes. That is a suboptimal recommendation, not a pair that cannot thrive and not a hazard.
notes:
VERIFIED (pairings, verbatim from review-packets.json): blue-fescue -> monkshood 55.4 (rank 4/8) and blue-oat-grass -> monkshood 55.4 (rank 4/8), both with the quoted reason. ADD A THIRD, WORSE INSTANCE the finding missed: creeping-sedum -> monkshood at 71.1, rank 3 of 8 ("Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; stands well above at 1.2 m, a canopy to plant beneath") - i.e. a moisture-obligate perennial recommended into a succulent, drought-tolerant groundcover at a near-top score. Cite creeping-sedum as the headline pair; it is the most damaging.

VERIFIED (library, plantLibrary.ts): monkshood = sun "part-sun", water "moderate", zones 3-7, h 1.2; blue-fescue and blue-oat-grass = "full-sun"/"low", both tagged drought tolerant; creeping-sedum = "full-sun"/"low", succulent. Pair passes light (1 rung), water (1 rung, x0.92), zones and height.

STRONGEST CORROBORATION - the app contradicts itself: src/garden/intel/curated/monkshood.json states "Dislikes heat: A cool-climate plant that struggles in hot summers. In the South, give it afternoon or dappled shade and keep the roots cool and moist, or it may not last." The compatibility engine, reading only water: "moderate", prints the opposite advice on the same plant. That is an internal inconsistency, not just an outside opinion, and it is the sentence to quote in the report.

CORRECTION TO CARRY INTO THE REPORT - drop or rewrite the toxicity half. The library DOES flag both plants: curated safety level "serious" for monkshood and colchicum, rendered as the first bar of PlantDetail.tsx above the companions. The accurate version of that sub-point is: PlantCompanions.tsx CompanionCard surfaces no safety level and offers a direct "Add to garden" button with no path to the detail card, so the existing "serious" warning can be bypassed entirely when a plant is added from a companion row. The fix is to surface the existing intel.safety level on the companion card, NOT to add a new library field.

FIX (primary): monkshood's water should be "high" (MBG: "soils must not be allowed to dry out"; A. carmichaelii "medium to wet"), which correctly fails the 1-rung filter against every low-water xeric subject while keeping its six good part-shade/moderate pairings intact.

Confirmed verbatim in data/chunks/chunk-04.json: blue-fescue -> monkshood (55.4, 4th of 8) and blue-oat-grass -> monkshood (55.4, 4th of 8), both with the quoted reason. Reword two things: (a) the conditions clause is a pair-span clause from conditionsClause() (compatibility.ts:1002-1019), so say it "presents low water as inside the pair's workable band" rather than claiming it asserts monkshood takes low water; (b) drop "raise Aconitum's water to high" as the headline fix - "moderate" is a defensible value (MOBOT rates Aconitum medium water). The real defect is the missing drainage/soil-moisture axis: Aconitum's cool, humus-rich, never-dry requirement and Festuca glauca / Helictotrichon's lean, gritty, sharp-drainage requirement both sit inside the low/moderate rungs, so no single regime serves both and the x0.92 water-differs penalty is not enough to suppress it. The toxicity gap (no toxic/poison flag anywhere in src/garden; cautionClause at line 1146 fires only on `spreader`) is data-accurate but is a separate defect and should be split into its own finding rather than bundled with this pairing.

If anything is kept, it should be a LOW-severity, reason-free note and it should point at monkshood's OWN companion list, not blue fescue: monkshood's top five are creeping-sedum (71.1), rock-cress (65.8), thyme (65.4), sea-thrift (65) and lavender (59.1). Lavender's lean, alkaline, sharply drained requirement is a genuinely harder conflict with Aconitum's humus-rich, evenly moist soil than blue fescue's "dry to medium" is, and that list is the more visible surface. Drop the "the reason says monkshood takes low water" claim (the clause is a union range, used identically across the corpus), drop the "library water value is wrong" claim (RHS: moist but well-drained = moderate), and file the toxicity flag separately as a product-wide plant-card feature request, not as a compatibility finding.

title: Blue Oat Grass's colour is classified as "orange", so six of its eight reasons give colour advice about a colour the plant does not have
subject: blue-oat-grass
claim: "...purple against orange, for contrast." (also "blue against orange", "yellow sits close to orange on the wheel", "its white flowers settle the orange")
why: Helictotrichon sempervirens is grown for steel-blue foliage; its own tag is "blue", and its flower panicle is straw/wheat. The library hex is bloom: [[6,7,"#d8c89a"]] - a pale beige. Run through colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts, #d8c89a is hue 44.5 deg, saturation 0.44, lightness 0.73: it lands one degree under the `h < 45 -> "orange"` cut-off, and its saturation (0.44) is above the isNeutralBloom() threshold (s < 0.2), so the engine does not treat it as a neutral. Result: the app confidently tells the gardener to plant purple crocus "against orange, for contrast" while looking at a blue grass. Blue Fescue has the same defect one bucket over (#cdbf8a -> hue 47.5 -> "yellow", producing "pink against yellow, for contrast" for a plant whose tag is also "blue"), and Fountain Grass (#d8c0a0 -> "orange") is the same. Any beginner who follows this is being given a colour scheme built on a colour that is not in the garden.
fix: Two clean options: (a) widen isNeutralBloom() so low-chroma tans read as neutral (e.g. also neutral when s < 0.5 and l > 0.65 and hue is 30-60), which makes these grasses correctly print "a soft colour beside..."; or (b) better, since these grasses are grown for foliage, drop the token bloom entry (or add an explicit `foliageFeature` flag) so colourScore() returns null for them, as it already does for blue-spruce and boston-ivy.
scope: 6 of 8 blue-oat-grass reasons and 5 of 8 blue-fescue reasons in this chunk; every ornamental grass in the library carrying a straw/tan bloom hex
notes: Two corrections. (1) It is SEVEN of the eight blue-oat-grass reasons that carry the orange clause, not six; only creeping-phlox omits it (its hue lands "discordant", which colourClause drops). (2) The finding's proposed fix (a) does not work: colourClause's neutral branch still prints the SUBJECT's colour name (`a soft colour beside the ${colour.aName}` / `its white flowers settle the ${colour.aName}`), so widening isNeutralBloom would just produce "a soft colour beside the orange" - the existing viburnum reason ("its white flowers settle the orange") is already a neutral-relation case and proves it. The real remedy is either fixing colorName's low-chroma tan bucket (so #d8c89a is not named "orange" anywhere, including the browse colour facet, where these grasses are currently also filed under orange/yellow) or fix (b), dropping the token bloom entry - with the caveat that dropping bloom flips CompatPlant.foliage to true, which switches bloom succession to ONE_SIDED_BLOOM and makes the grass eligible as bulb cover (arguably an improvement, but not a no-op).

Confirmed verbatim in the generated data (chunks/chunk-04.json): blue-oat-grass -> crocus, score 54.3, "Shares full sun and low water; blooms in late winter, before this one opens; lower at 0.1 m, for the front of the border; purple against orange, for contrast." 6 of its 8 companion reasons carry the orange clause. Two additions worth carrying into the report: (1) Blue Fescue as subject is worse - 7 of 8 reasons name "yellow", including "echoes the yellow" for basket-of-gold; (2) the reciprocal direction produces an outright self-contradiction, e.g. `wisteria -> blue-oat-grass :: "... orange against blue, for contrast."`, where the engine calls the blue grass orange and contrasts it against blue in the same clause. Suggest re-categorising from "wrong-library-data" to a classifier/threshold defect: the hex is a fair rendering of the straw panicle; what is wrong is the `h < 45 -> "orange"` cut-off in colorName() plus an isNeutralBloom() threshold (s < 0.2 || l > 0.85) too tight to catch low-chroma tans. Proposed fix (b) - treating these foliage grasses as foliage so colourScore returns null - is the cleaner of the two.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Count: it is SEVEN of eight blue-oat-grass reasons that name orange, not six (only creeping-phlox has no colour clause). (2) Scope is wider than the finding states and should be reported as a class defect, not a single plant: blue-fescue (#cdbf8a -> "yellow": "pink against yellow, for contrast", "purple against yellow, for contrast", "echoes the yellow"), fountain-grass (#d8c0a0 -> "orange"), feather-reed-grass ("purple against orange, for contrast"), maiden-grass ("its white flowers settle the orange", "echoes the orange") and switchgrass ("orange sits close to pink on the wheel") all print colour advice about a straw/tan plume as if it were a saturated orange or yellow. Worth adding: for blue-oat-grass and blue-fescue the clause is doubly wrong, because the engine tells the gardener to set BLUE flowers (monkshood, grape-hyacinth, wisteria) "against orange, for contrast" next to a plant whose defining feature is blue foliage, so the real visual relation is blue-on-blue, an echo, not a contrast. Also note the app contradicts itself: plantColors() adds the "blue" tag, so Browse filters these grasses under BLUE while the companion card calls them orange. Harm is copy credibility and misleading colour-scheme advice; no plant is endangered, so keep it at medium and say so plainly.

title: "Carpet the ground beneath it" is printed for 1.8-2 m shrubs and grasses, and Boston Ivy (a flat wall vine) is treated as a canopy with an understory
subject: boston-ivy
claim: "...low at 1.8 m, to carpet the ground beneath it." (also boston-ivy -> maiden-grass, and blue-spruce -> ninebark, "low at 2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it")
why: Two defects in layeringClause() (src/garden/intel/compatibility.ts ~L1053). First, the Tree/Conifer/>=6 m branch (L1072) fires the "carpet the ground" wording on ratio alone, with no floor on the companion's absolute height - the code's own comment says a big ratio is not enough and it guards the OTHER branch with `tallEnoughToShelter`, but this branch has no equivalent check. So a 2 m x 2 m Ninebark and a 1.8 m Rhododendron are described to a beginner as ground carpet. Second, Boston Ivy (12 m, category Climber) trips the `a.height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M` test and is treated as a canopy tree: a self-clinging wall vine has no canopy and no "ground beneath it" to carpet, and its companion list is consequently filled with 18 m conifers (Douglas Fir, Eastern White Pine) whose only stated reason is "Takes full sun to part sun and moderate water; evergreen" - no benefit, and no warning that Boston Ivy will climb straight into a pine planted next to it.
fix: In layeringClause(), gate the "carpet the ground" wording on the companion actually being ground-level (e.g. b.height <= 0.5 m), not on ratio alone, and exclude category === "Climber" from the `a.height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M` structure branch so a wall vine never claims an understory (it already has a dedicated climber clause on the other side).
scope: 3 pairings in this chunk; systemic for any subject >= 6 m or of category Climber/Tree/Conifer
notes: Keep the pair examples but lead with the strongest: tulip-tree -> rose-of-sharon calls a 2.5 m Hibiscus syriacus a ground carpet; 20 pairs total describe a companion over 0.5 m as "carpet the ground beneath it" (of 284 such clauses). Drop the claim that Boston Ivy's 18 m conifer companions are a CONSEQUENCE of the canopy misclassification - they score identically to a 6 m arborvitae (38.2), so height contributes nothing; that is a separate thin-list/no-benefit issue. Also correct the code description: tallEnoughToShelter floors the TALLER plant, not the companion, and would be true anyway above 6 m; the real defect is that no branch floors the companion's absolute height. The Climber exclusion point stands on its own (the engine already treats a climber as "height without the footprint" when it is the candidate, but not when it is the subject).

Two wording fixes for the report. (1) tallEnoughToShelter is a floor on the TALLER plant, not on the companion, and the structure branch satisfies it implicitly (a >= 6 m is tier 3); the genuinely missing guard is a floor on the companion's absolute height, absent from both branches but only reachable in the structure branch, because only there can the taller plant exceed 12 m and still leave a 1.8-2.5 m shrub above the 6:1 canopy ratio. The proposed fix (gate the carpet wording on b.height being ground-level, and exclude category "Climber" from the structure branch) is the right shape. (2) Drop "consequently" from the Douglas Fir / Eastern White Pine sentence: those conifers are in Boston Ivy's list because they pass the filters and score on evergreen + matching conditions, not because of the layering bug (their 1.5 height ratio never reaches the canopy branch). The generic-reason quote itself is accurate. Report the wider scope: 15 "carpet the ground beneath it" pairs with a companion >= 1 m tall, and 17 climber-as-canopy pairs across boston-ivy, climbing-hydrangea, trumpet-vine and wisteria.

SURVIVES, rewritten and rescoped. Verified: layeringClause() L1072 emits "low at {h}, to carpet the ground beneath it" on ratio alone with no floor on the companion's height. Of 284 carpet clauses in the data, 269 are correct (companion 0.1-0.9 m); 15 fire on a companion >= 1 m, 9 of them absurdly. Lead with the worst, which the original finding missed: tulip-tree -> rose-of-sharon, "low at 2.5 m, to carpet the ground beneath it". Others: blue-spruce -> ninebark (2 m), linden -> butterfly-bush (2 m), weeping-willow -> joe-pye-weed (1.8 m) / hydrangea (1.5 m) / canna (1.5 m), boston-ivy -> rhododendron (1.8 m) and -> maiden-grass (1.8 m). Second, separate defect (keep): a >= 6 m Climber trips the a.height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M branch and is written up as a canopy with an understory - boston-ivy -> camellia reads "shorter at 2 m, an understory layer" under a flat self-clinging wall vine; climbing-hydrangea (8 m) and wisteria (8 m) do the same. The code already carries the explicit guard `if (b.category === "Climber")` at L1065 ("a climber carries height on a support, so it is never a canopy"), so only the climber-as-candidate case was handled. STRONGER THAN CLAIMED: this is not only wording - layeringScore() (L637) awards the 0.4-0.6 "canopy over underplanting" credit to ANY tier-3 plant including a Climber, so Boston Ivy's 22-point layering factor against a 1.8 m shrub is inflated and changes which companions rank onto its list. DROP from the write-up: the tallEnoughToShelter comparison (misreads the code), the claim that the 18 m conifers are a consequence of this branch (they get no layering clause; ratio 1.5), and the vine-into-tree warning (documented not-modelled). Fix stands: floor the carpet wording on the companion actually being ground level (e.g. b.height <= 0.5 m), and exclude category === "Climber" from the structure branch in both layeringClause() and the tier-3 canopy case in layeringScore().

title: Wisteria (genus-level, i.e. the invasive Asian species) offered as a companion to a 0.7 m clump grass, with no support, containment or invasiveness note
subject: blue-oat-grass
claim: "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; blue against orange, for contrast."
why: The library entry is `botanical: "Wisteria"` with no species, and what a US beginner buys under that name is almost always W. sinensis or W. floribunda - both listed as invasive across much of the eastern and southeastern US, both capable of girdling trees and pulling gutters and trellises off a house. The entry itself says 8 m tall, 4 m wide, 5 years to size. Offering it as a border companion to a 0.7 m alpine clump grass is not a garden combination at all, and the reason gives the beginner no support, no siting, no pruning and no containment note - the only stated benefit is a colour contrast that is doubly wrong (wisteria's #9b8fd6 is lavender-purple, not "blue", and blue oat grass is not "orange", see the separate colour finding). The same climber defect shows up as bluestar -> passionflower, where the reason promises "climbs to 4 m behind it, height without the footprint" - Passiflora is exactly the genus that DOES have a footprint, running by underground suckers metres from the crown and coming up through its neighbours.
fix: Resolve genus-only entries to a named, garden-safe species (use Wisteria frutescens, the US native, for the default archetype) or drop Wisteria from the archetype pool. Add an `aggressive`/`invasive` flag and either exclude flagged plants from companion recommendations or force a caution clause. And do not print "height without the footprint" for suckering climbers - gate that clause on a non-suckering flag.
scope: blue-oat-grass -> wisteria; same class of defect at bluestar -> passionflower
refutations:
The titled pair harms no one, and the charges against it do not hold up against the data.

EXPOSURE IS NEGLIGIBLE. Wisteria appears on exactly 3 companion lists in the entire corpus (blue-oat-grass rank 5/8, feather-reed-grass 8/8, jasmine 8/8). The audit's own data/invasive-recount.json already enumerates the genuine invasive-recommendation problem - pampas-grass (191 lists, praised 191, cautioned 0), northern-sea-oats (213), maiden-grass (68), butterfly-bush (42) - and wisteria is not in that file at all. Whatever harm "we recommend invasives without caution" does is already captured at ~60x the volume by findings the report holds. This is a 3-list footnote dressed as a medium finding.

THE INVASIVENESS CHARGE IS INFERENTIAL, NOT DATA-GROUNDED. The entry is genus-only (`botanical: "Wisteria"`, plantLibrary.ts:2597) and its variant group (lines 1582-1586, 2597-2601) contains TWO US NATIVES - W. macrostachya 'Blue Moon' and W. frutescens 'Amethyst Falls' - alongside two Japanese cultivars. The finding's premise ("what a US beginner buys under that name is almost always W. sinensis or W. floribunda") is a claim about shopper behaviour, not about what the library asserts. Every row in the recount names an unambiguous invasive binomial (Cortaderia selloana, Miscanthus sinensis, Buddleia davidii); this entry cannot be shown to be filed WRONG, only underspecified. Nor is it "praised for the very trait that makes it invasive" - the qualifier the brief attaches to this finding class. The reason praises bloom timing and colour, never vigour or coverage.

THE PAIR IS NOT HORTICULTURALLY IMPOSSIBLE. Both full sun; water differs by one rung (already penalised x0.92 and hedged honestly in copy as "low to moderate water"); zones intersect; height ratio 11.4:1 sits inside the documented 15:1 filter, an accepted trade-off the brief says not to report as news. A wisteria on an arbor with blue oat grass in the dry sunny bed at its foot is an ordinary planting. There is no allelopathy, pH, or exposure conflict to name. "Not a garden combination at all" is assertion, not horticulture.

THE STRONGEST EVIDENCE IS ATTACHED TO A DIFFERENT PAIR. MAX_FACTOR_CLAUSES = 3 (compatibility.ts:1153) truncates the titled pair's reason BEFORE the climber clause: blue-oat-grass -> wisteria prints only sun/water, bloom succession, and colour. The "height without the footprint" complaint (the finding's most substantive point) never fires on the pair in the headline; it is imported from bluestar -> passionflower.

THE MISSING-CAUTION COMPLAINT FAULTS THE FORMAT. No companion reason anywhere in the system carries cultural care advice - that is careTips.ts's job, and it already fires on aggressiveSpreader (careTips.ts:218). Invasiveness is also on the engine's documented not-modelled list.

THE PROPOSED FIX ASKS FOR SOMETHING THAT EXISTS. An `aggressiveSpreader` flag is already modelled (types.ts:111, plant.ts:161) and already drives a hard `spreader-smothers` blocker (compatibility.ts:564), a `spreader-needs-watching` penalty, and a caution clause ("a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging", :1147).

THE COLOUR HALF IS SELF-DECLARED DOUBLE-COUNTING ("see the separate colour finding") and is weak on merit: #9b8fd6 is hue ~250 degrees, i.e. blue-violet, and the library's own sibling entry is literally named "Blue Moon Wisteria."
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The proposed fix misstates the mechanism: the engine ALREADY has the flag. src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts carries `spreader: true`, which drives a hard `spreader-smothers` block plus a "give it room or an edging" caution. It is set on campsis, parthenocissus, clematis-sweet-autumn and miscanthus (comment: "M. sinensis self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US") but NOT on wisteria (line 381: `{ deerResistant: true }`) or passiflora (line 380). So the fix is a one-line data change, and it is internally inconsistent with the project's own treatment of trumpet vine and sweet autumn clematis. If wisteria were flagged, blue-oat-grass (0.7 m) would be hard-blocked by spreaderSmothers/isVulnerableTo, not merely cautioned. (2) The "no siting note" defect has a precise cause worth naming: layeringScore gives 8 m vs 0.7 m a canopy score of ~0.45, below the 0.5 CLAUSE_FLOOR, so the height clause is SUPPRESSED for exactly the most extreme mismatch - the same wisteria does say "climbs to 8 m behind it" to feather-reed-grass (1.5 m, score ~0.54). The worse the scale gap, the less the sentence says about scale. Drop "not a garden combination at all" and soften "doubly wrong" on colour.

Two corrections the report must carry, plus a sharper anchor.

(1) "No invasiveness/support note" is true of the REASON but false of the app, and the report must not imply the product never warns. `src/garden/intel/curated/wisteria.json` exists and is rendered on wisteria's own plant card (PlantDetail.tsx:362 renders `intel.goodToKnow` and `intel.notes`): safety level "serious" (toxic), goodToKnow "thick trunk-like stems pry apart gutters, crush a light trellis, or girdle and kill a tree. Use a heavy support", and a note titled "Native vs invasive species": "In the eastern US, Chinese and Japanese wisteria are aggressive invasives that strangle trees. Plant the native American wisteria (W. frutescens)." None of it reaches the companion surface: the companion card (PlantCompanions.tsx) shows only photo/name/category/spread/bloom bar/reason plus Save and a one-tap "Add to garden" - it never links to the companion's own detail. The correct framing is: the recommendation surface DROPS a caution the app already owns, and pairs the un-cautioned recommendation with an add button.

(2) The proposed fix should say "flag them on the seam that already exists", not "add a flag". The chain `trait.spreader` (taxonomy.ts) -> `intel.aggressiveSpreader` (plant.ts:161) -> `spreader` on CompatPlant (compatibility.ts:434) -> `spreader-needs-watching` penalty -> `cautionClause()` is already built; wisteria and passiflora are simply not flagged, so no caution ever prints.

(3) Sharper anchor: blue-oat-grass is wisteria's WEAKEST of three appearances (it gets no layering clause at all). The other two print an actively false benefit - feather-reed-grass -> wisteria and jasmine -> wisteria both read "climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint", for the very vine whose own curated note says it crushes trellises and girdles trees. Wisteria appears in only 3 of 276 archetype lists, so on volume alone the passionflower instance of the same defect (genus-level `Passiflora`, root-suckering, "height without the footprint", 12 lists) carries more weight; lead with the false-benefit clause and the genus-level default rather than with "not a garden combination at all", which is overstated (blue oat grass at the foot of a sunny wisteria pergola is a plausible planting).

Two scraps are worth salvaging, but neither belongs under this headline and neither is medium severity.

(1) The climber clause at compatibility.ts:1065 fires unconditionally for every Climber: `climbs to ${metres(b.height)} behind it, height without the footprint`. For a root-suckering climber (Passiflora incarnata, which runs by underground suckers metres from the crown) that is arguably a false stated benefit. If this is worth reporting, report it on its own merits keyed to the passionflower pairs and to the clause's lack of a non-suckering gate - do NOT smuggle it in under a wisteria title, where it does not even print.

(2) `wisteria` is a genus-only archetype whose variant group mixes two Japanese cultivars with two US natives. Resolving the default to a named species is a legitimate but minor LIBRARY DATA-HYGIENE note, not a pairing finding, and it should not be filed as irresponsible advice while the same report already carries pampas-grass (191 lists, praised, uncautioned) and maiden-grass (68) - which are the real, unambiguous, high-volume version of this concern.

title: Pampas Grass (Cortaderia selloana) is recommended to California Poppy and Butterfly Weed and praised for the seed heads that make it invasive
subject: california-poppy
claim: Shares full sun and low water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a Cal-IPC 'High' invasive, a CDFA-listed pest in California, a listed noxious weed in Oregon, Washington and Hawaii, and invasive across the Gulf and Southeast. Its library zones (7-11) are almost exactly its invasive range. A single plume carries hundreds of thousands of wind-dispersed seeds, so the reason praises the plant for the precise trait that makes it a weed, and offers it as the go-to companion for a California native (Eschscholzia) and for a US native prairie plant (Asclepias tuberosa, same clause, score 58.7) - i.e. to exactly the gardener most likely to be planting near open land. The library is internally inconsistent about this: taxonomy.ts:423 flags miscanthus as a spreader with the comment 'self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US', while taxonomy.ts:424 reads 'cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits'.
fix: Give cortaderia a caution trait in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts (at minimum spreader: true so the existing cautionClause fires; better, a regional invasive flag that suppresses it as a companion in zones 7-11 or attaches 'invasive in the West and Southeast - check your state list'). Also drop the 'seed heads for structure and the birds' clause for any plant carrying that flag.
scope: Every list pampas-grass appears in; in this chunk it is a top-8 companion for both california-poppy and butterfly-weed.
notes: Keep the finding, with these corrections. Verified facts to state: Cortaderia selloana is rated HIGH on the Cal-IPC Inventory at the species level (not just C. jubata); it is a Hawaii Noxious Weed (HAR 68) and a Class C noxious weed in Washington; and Cal-IPC records that each plume bears up to 100,000 seeds that are wind-dispersed and set WITHOUT fertilization (apomictic), so even a lone female garden clone seeds freely. Remove the false claim that it is CDFA-listed (Cal-IPC's profile shows CDFA rating: None) and drop Oregon (the Oregon-listed taxon is C. jubata). Correct "hundreds of thousands of seeds per plume" to ~100,000 per plume (~400,000 per plant). Soften "the go-to companion": in the data it ranks 8 of 8 for california-poppy (59.5) and 7 of 8 for butterfly-weed (58.7), so it sits behind the "Show all" fold - but it still appears in 191 companion lists with ZERO cautions (invasive-recount.json), and the generated praise clause, "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds", commends the exact organ that carries the wind-borne apomictic seed. The taxonomy.ts inconsistency is real and quotable: line 423 flags miscanthus as a spreader "invasive in much of the US" while line 424 reads cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits. The proposed fix stands.

Survives; keep at high, but tighten two things. (1) Rank, not "go-to": pampas is the LAST of the eight companions shown for california-poppy (59.5 against a 76 top score) and 7th of 8 for butterfly-weed. It is one of eight, three inline and the rest behind "Show all 8 companions" (PlantCompanions.tsx INLINE_FULL = 3) - so it is definitely shown, but do not call it the top or go-to companion. (2) Regulatory citations: Cal-IPC's California rating, Hawaii's noxious-weed listing and broad Gulf/Southeast invasiveness are the solid ones for Cortaderia SELLOANA; several of the Oregon/Washington/CDFA listings usually quoted attach to C. jubata. Cite the ones that hold for selloana and the point stands. (3) Scope is larger than the two named pairs and worth stating: the audit's own invasive-recount.json records pampas-grass on 191 companion lists, praised 191 times, cautioned 0 - the two named pairs are the sharpest examples (a California native and a US prairie native), not the whole blast radius. The proposed fix is mechanically correct: cautionClause() only reads `spreader`, so `cortaderia: {}` guarantees silence.

Two precision corrections to carry into the report. (1) Exposure: PlantCompanions.tsx uses PREVIEW_N = 3 and INLINE_FULL = 3, so on the poppy and butterfly-weed lists pampas-grass sits at rank 7-8, behind the "Show all 8 companions" expander (one tap away, not hidden). It is not always buried: it ranks #1 on aloe's list, i.e. inside the visible 3-item preview. The companions feature is also currently limited to 3 reveals per account. This narrows reach; it does not change the defect. (2) Butterfly-weed's layering clause is "taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it", not "stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath" (that is the poppy's); the seed-head praise clause is identical on both. (3) Fix caveat: `spreader: true` alone is probably insufficient - maiden-grass (Miscanthus) already carries `spreader: true` and still shows 0 cautioned / 68 praised in invasive-recount.json, because the caution clause only fires when the neighbour is judged vulnerable. The fix needs an explicit invasive/caution flag that always attaches a warning and suppresses the seed-head praise clause.

title: Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) is recommended and praised as 'a strong pollinator plant' with no invasiveness caution, in the zones where it is a noxious weed
subject: candytuft
claim: Shares full sun and low water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; a soft colour beside the white.
why: Buddleia davidii is a Class B noxious weed in Washington and a listed noxious weed in Oregon (only sterile cultivars may legally be sold there), and it self-sows into riparian corridors and disturbed land across the Pacific Northwest, mid-Atlantic and parts of New England - all inside its library range of zones 5-9. taxonomy.ts:145 gives it 'pollinator: high' and no spreader or caution flag, so it is not only offered without a warning, it is offered as a pollinator virtue. The pollinator claim is also half-true in a way that misleads a beginner: it is nectar-only, no North American butterfly larva feeds on it, so it is the classic 'nectar bar, not a host plant' shrub often planted instead of the milkweeds and asters that actually reproduce butterflies.
fix: Add a caution trait to buddleia/buddleja in taxonomy.ts so the reason carries 'self-seeds freely and is a listed weed in the Northwest - plant a sterile cultivar such as Lo & Behold or Miss Molly', or exclude the seedy species from the companion pool and keep only sterile cultivars as archetypes.
scope: butterfly-bush is a subject with 185 candidates and an archetype companion; every list it appears in inherits the missing caution.
notes: Re-anchor the pair on creeping-phlox -> butterfly-bush (score 79.7), whose reason literally says "both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months", and cite candytuft -> butterfly-bush (71.3, candytuft's top companion) as the uncautioned top-rank case. Retitle to the accurate charge: "Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) is recommended - and in 6 lists praised as a strong pollinator plant - with no invasiveness caution, inside the zones where it is a quarantine-listed noxious weed." Drop the "praised for the very trait that makes it invasive" framing (that fits pampas grass, not buddleia: the invasive trait is wind-blown seed and no clause praises it). Evidence: plantLibrary.ts:2464 files the seedy species itself as the group default while the seedless cultivars are already in the library as variants (lines 2470-2474), so the fix is a one-line archetype change plus a caution trait, not new data. In the proposed fix, do not name 'Lo & Behold Blue Chip' as the sterile exemplar - it was later found to set viable seed and was superseded by 'Blue Chip Jr.'; point at the current WSDA/ODA approved seedless list (the "Miss" series, Pugster) instead.

Restate as: "Buddleja davidii is recommended to 42 subjects (candytuft -> butterfly-bush, score 71.3) with zero invasiveness caution in the reason, and scored partly on taxonomy.ts:145 pollinator:'high' (5 of those reasons praise it as 'a strong pollinator plant' - allium, sage, creeping-phlox, linden; the candytuft reason itself praises colour and canopy, not pollinators)." Drop the claim that the app gives no warning: curated/butterfly-bush.json already carries the noxious-weed extraFact and the "no native caterpillars" goodToKnow, and PlantDetail renders them. The real defect is narrower and still worth fixing: the companion card (PlantCompanions.tsx:127-144) shows only the generated reason plus a direct "Add to garden" button and never links to the companion's own detail page, so the caution the product already owns never reaches the point of recommendation. Proposed fix should be "propagate the existing curated caution into the companion reason / card (or exclude the seedy species from the archetype pool in favour of a sterile cultivar)", not "add a caution that does not exist".

Verified: chunks/chunk-05.json shows butterfly-bush is candytuft's #1 companion (score 71.3) with the quoted reason; plantLibrary.ts:2464 makes the straight seedy species Buddleia davidii the group ARCHETYPE (the sterile cultivars Blue Chip / Pugster / Miss Molly sit in the same group but can never be recommended); taxonomy.ts:145-146 gives buddleia/buddleja pollinator "high" with no caution trait; invasive-recount.json records 42 companion lists, cautioned: 0. Restate the finding at population level (butterfly-bush recommended in 42 lists, 0 with any caution; in some of those the reason actively praises it as "a strong pollinator plant") rather than pinning the pollinator-praise claim to candytuft, whose reason has no pollinator clause. Drop the "beginners are misled about host plants" leg and the "add a caution to taxonomy.ts" fix: curated/butterfly-bush.json already states the noxious-weed regulation, the 40,000-seed figure, and the no-native-caterpillars fact, all free and shown on the plant card. The correct fix is to propagate that existing curated caution onto the companion card (which has its own one-tap "Add to garden" bypassing the plant detail), or to make an approved sterile cultivar the group archetype instead of the seedy species.

title: The colour classifier calls a white Calla Lily 'orange' and an orange Asiatic Lily 'pink', so the colour sentence shown to users is false
subject: calla-lily
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; blooms in early spring, before this one opens; a soft colour beside the orange.
why: colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts:83 decides 'white' with `l >= 0.82 && s < 0.25`, but HSL saturation is inflated for near-white hexes. Calla Lily's bloom is #f5f0e8 (plantLibrary.ts:2865, tagged 'white'): l = 0.94 but computed s = 0.39, so it misses the white bucket, falls through to hue 37 degrees, and is named ORANGE. Both of calla-lily's colour clauses (anemone, and ranunculus at the same score) therefore describe a pure white flower as orange. The same function's `h < 16 -> l > 0.55 ? pink : red` split misnames the Asiatic Lily (#e8643c, tagged 'orange', a vivid orange-red) as PINK, which is why calibrachoa -> lily-asiatic reads 'pink sits close to orange on the wheel' about two orange flowers. compatibility.ts correctly detects calla lily as a NEUTRAL bloom (isNeutralBloom uses l > 0.85) - only the name is wrong, so the bug is isolated to the classifier.
fix: In colorName(), decide white on chroma rather than HSL saturation (max-min < ~0.10, or `l >= 0.82 && (max-min) < 0.12`), and gate the hue<16 pink/red split on saturation so a saturated red-orange cannot be named pink. Note this same function feeds plantColors(), so the browse colour filter files Calla Lily under 'orange' too.
scope: Every plant with a cream or ivory bloom hex (calla-lily, and pampas-grass #f0e8d0 is named 'yellow' by the same path) and every mid-tone red-orange bloom (lily-asiatic).
notes: Two corrections/additions to carry into the report. (1) The colourClause() neutral branch is ALSO inverted, which the finding missed: colourScore() returns "neutral" when EITHER plant is neutral, but the templates ("its white flowers settle the X" / "a soft colour beside the X") assume the neutral bloom is the companion (b). When the subject is the white one, as with calla-lily, both halves invert: the saturated violet anemone (#6a5ac0) is called "a soft colour" and the white calla is called "the orange". So calla-lily -> anemone is wrong twice, not once. (2) The near-white bug is systematic, not isolated: dahlia-fleurel #f6f3ec -> orange, daylily-joan-senior #f4eedd -> orange, narcissus-mount-hood #f6f3ea -> orange, shasta-daisy-becky #fcfcfa -> yellow, tulip-white-triumphator / narcissus-thalia #f7f5ee -> yellow (these are non-archetypes, so they surface as SUBJECTS of a colour clause rather than as companions). Minor overstatement to trim: plantColors() still files these under "white" via the tags path, so the browse filter gains a spurious extra bucket rather than losing white.

Two small corrections/additions. (1) Line cite: colorName() begins at plantFacets.ts:83, but the white test is line 86 and the pink/red split is line 88. (2) The bug is broader than the two pairs named, which strengthens it: lily-asiatic is called "pink" in at least five subjects' reasons - bergenia -> lily-asiatic "echoes the pink", and bluestar / glory-of-the-snow / lobelia -> lily-asiatic "pink against blue, for contrast" - all about a vivid red-orange lily. And when calla-lily is the CANDIDATE (chunks/chunk-09.json) the same misclassification suppresses the better template: it emits "a soft colour beside the red" instead of "its white flowers settle the red", because the neutral clause gates on bName === "white".

Scope is larger than filed, and one sub-claim needs a correction. SCOPE: the same classifier bug misnames the subject's own colour in 108 of the 2,208 generated pairs. Beyond calla-lily, plainly-white archetype subjects whose reason names them a chromatic colour include gardenia and jasmine and moonflower and solomons-seal ("a soft colour beside the yellow"), climbing-hydrangea and goatsbeard ("the orange"), and foam-flower and nicotiana and ninebark ("the pink"). 225 library plants have a near-white bloom (chroma < 0.12, l > 0.85) that colorName() does not name white; 23 red/orange-tagged plants are named pink (every "Coral" cultivar, plus ranunculus #e0533c and knockout-rose). CORRECTION: the browse-filter consequence is over-inclusion, not misfiling. plantColors() unions the hex bucket with the plant's colour TAGS, so Calla Lily still shows under the "white" filter via its `white` tag; the bug adds a spurious "orange" hit. Same for Asiatic Lily (pink AND orange). SECOND-ORDER EFFECT worth mentioning in the fix: because the neutral template branches on `bName === "white"`, the misnaming also suppresses the better "its white flowers settle the X" clause for the ~225 near-white blooms, downgrading them to the generic "a soft colour beside the X" (true, but weaker). Fixing colorName() on chroma restores both.

title: 'A canopy to plant beneath' fires for dense shrubs: a beginner is told to plant sun-demanding Calibrachoa under a Lilac
subject: calibrachoa
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; opens in mid spring and overlaps into this one's season; stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: layeringClause (compatibility.ts:1063-1067) only tests height tier when the COMPANION is the taller plant, so any shrub over the tier-2 line with a >6:1 ratio is described as a canopy. Syringa vulgaris is a dense, suckering, multi-stemmed shrub 3 m x 2.5 m whose skirt reaches the ground and whose surface roots are greedy - there is no usable 'beneath'. Calibrachoa is a full-sun annual that needs six-plus hours of direct sun to keep flowering; planted under a lilac it goes leggy, stops blooming, and dries out. The same false instruction appears for candytuft -> smokebush ('stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath', Cotinus is 3 m x 3 m) and candytuft -> butterfly-bush, and in reverse for butterfly-bush -> creeping-phlox ('low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath'), all of them full-sun, sharp-drainage plants being sent into a shrub's dry shade. The a-is-taller branch already guards this correctly (line 1072 requires a Tree/Conifer or >= 6 m).
fix: Mirror the guard in the b-is-taller branch: only say 'a canopy to plant beneath' when b.category is Tree or Conifer, or b.height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M. For a broad shrub, fall back to 'taller at 3 m, for the layer behind it'.
scope: Every pairing where the taller partner is a shrub over ~1.5 m with a >6:1 height ratio; at least 4 pairings in this chunk (calibrachoa->lilac, calibrachoa->viburnum, candytuft->smokebush, candytuft->butterfly-bush, plus butterfly-bush->creeping-phlox in reverse).
notes: Correction 1 (load-bearing, the proposed fix is wrong as written): the a-is-taller branch is NOT correctly guarded. Only the "to carpet the ground beneath IT" variant requires Tree/Conifer or height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M; the final fallback still returns `low at ${metres(b.height)}, to carpet the ground beneath` whenever r > CANOPY_RATIO && tallEnoughToShelter, with no category check - which is exactly why the finding's own butterfly-bush -> creeping-phlox example (a = 2 m shrub) exists. The fix must add the Tree/Conifer/>= 6 m guard to BOTH branches, not mirror one onto the other. Correction 2 (tone): "there is no usable 'beneath'" a lilac is overstated; mature lilacs are commonly underplanted with dry-shade-tolerant plants. The error is calling a 3 m shrub a canopy and sending a full-sun annual into its dry, root-filled shade. Scale worth citing in the report: 241 "canopy to plant beneath" clauses, 210 of them with a non-tree "canopy", including 1.5 m grasses.

Correct one claim before publishing: the finding says "the a-is-taller branch already guards this correctly (line 1072 requires a Tree/Conifer or >= 6 m)". That is a misread. Line 1072 only chooses the wording for tall subjects; the fallthrough at lines 1077-1079 still emits "low at X m, to carpet the ground beneath" for any non-tree subject above the tier-2 line. The data proves the defect is symmetric: subject `lilac` (3 m Shrub) recommends `scaevola` ("low at 0.2 m, to carpet the ground beneath"), `verbena-annual` (0.25 m) and `lobelia` (0.2 m) with that exact clause - three more full-sun annuals sent into a lilac's dry shade. The fix must therefore guard BOTH branches (line 1066 and line 1077), gating "canopy to plant beneath" / "carpet the ground beneath" on category Tree/Conifer or height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M, falling back to "taller at 3 m, for the layer behind it" / "lower at 0.2 m, for the front of the border" for broad shrubs. Also worth noting the clause fires below shrub scale entirely: calibrachoa -> perennial-sunflower (1.5 m) reads "stands well above at 1.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath", so tierOf >= 2 is too low a bar for the word "canopy".

Two corrections. (1) The finding's diagnosis that "the a-is-taller branch already guards this correctly (line 1072 requires a Tree/Conifer or >= 6 m)" is wrong: that guard only covers the first path. The final fallback (compatibility.ts ~1077-1079) still emits "low at X, to carpet the ground beneath" for ANY non-tree subject with ratio > 6 and tier >= 2, which is how butterfly-bush -> creeping-phlox (rank #1), boxwood (1.2 m) -> sweet-alyssum, and cleome (a 1.2 m ANNUAL) -> rock-cress are produced (284 such clauses). The fix must guard BOTH branches, not mirror one onto the other. (2) Lead the example with candytuft -> butterfly-bush: it is candytuft's #1 companion (71.3) and calls a 2 m shrub "well above ... a canopy to plant beneath", which is more damning than the #7-ranked calibrachoa -> lilac. Verified reason strings: calibrachoa -> lilac "Shares full sun and moderate water; opens in mid spring and overlaps into this one's season; stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath."; candytuft -> butterfly-bush "Shares full sun and low water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; a soft colour beside the white."

title: Celosia is told to plant Pampas Grass, and the reason praises the seed plumes that make it invasive
subject: celosia
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a listed invasive over most of the range the library itself gives it (zones 7-11): Cal-IPC rates it High in California, it is a declared pest in Hawaii, and it has escaped in the Pacific Northwest, Texas and the coastal Southeast. The invasion vector is precisely the feature the reason sells: each female plume carries on the order of 100,000 wind-borne seeds that travel for kilometres. The sentence not only fails to caution, it actively markets the plumes and adds "for the birds", which is bird-mediated dispersal. It is also a 2.5 m clump of saw-edged leaves next to a 0.5 m bedding annual. A beginner in coastal California who follows this is planting a regionally listed weed. (The engine's docs say invasiveness is not modelled; this is a named pairing where that omission produces actively harmful advice, and it recurs for every Cortaderia companion slot.)
fix: Either drop Cortaderia selloana from the archetype/companion pool, or add an `invasive` trait in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts (currently `cortaderia: {}`) that (a) blocks it as a recommendable companion in its invaded zones and (b) suppresses the seed-head clause. Suggest Miscanthus-free alternatives such as switchgrass (already in the library, native, already recommended to Celosia) instead.
scope: Every pairing where pampas-grass is the companion; the same mechanism makes any invasive-but-untagged plant recommendable.
notes: Drop the "bird-mediated dispersal" framing. The accurate charge against the reason clause is two-fold: (1) it markets the seed plumes - the exact vector by which C. selloana escapes, roughly 100,000 wind-borne seeds per plume - with no invasiveness caution anywhere in the engine (taxonomy.ts:424 is `cortaderia: {}`; cautionClause() only fires off penalty keys, so an invasive can never be flagged); and (2) "for the birds" asserts a wildlife benefit pampas seed heads do not really provide, since the seed is wind-dispersed and of little food value to birds. Scale confirmed: 191 companion lists, 0 cautioned. Also worth keeping: at 2.5 m against a 0.5 m bedding annual, and with silica-serrated leaf margins that cut skin, it is a poor physical neighbour for Celosia regardless of the invasiveness issue.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Drop the sub-claim that "for the birds" is "bird-mediated dispersal" - Cortaderia selloana is wind-dispersed, and birds are not a meaningful vector. The clause is still false and harmful for a different reason: it markets the exact organ (the female plume, ~100,000 wind-borne seeds) that is the invasion vector, and pampas plumes are of poor wildlife value, so the stated benefit does not exist. (2) The same generated clause (compatibility.ts:1109, fired for any Grass because `seedheads` is true) praises maiden-grass / Miscanthus sinensis in 68 lists with 0 cautions, so the fix should cover the whole invasive-grass set, not just Cortaderia; the suggested substitute (switchgrass, native) is already rank 4 on this very list. Also worth adding: because Celosia is an Annual the zone gate is skipped, so a zone-5 user is offered a zone 7-11 grass here regardless.

Keep the finding at high, but drop the "bird-mediated dispersal" inference: Cortaderia selloana is wind-dispersed, not bird-dispersed. Re-frame the "for the birds" clause as a false wildlife-benefit claim (pampas is poor bird habitat and not a notable seed source) attached to praise of the seed plumes. Also demote the height/saw-edged-leaf remark to a footnote - height layering is an intended scored behaviour, not a defect. Strengthen the writeup with the corroborating counts from invasive-recount.json (lists 191, praised 191, cautioned 0) and note that switchgrass scores an identical 46.6 for celosia with an identical reason, so removing pampas from the archetype pool costs nothing.

title: Japanese Forest Grass is recommended for "seed heads that stand through winter, for structure and the birds" - it has none
subject: cinnamon-fern
claim: Shares part shade and moderate to high water; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; lower at 0.4 m, for the front of the border.
why: Hakonechloa macra is grown purely for foliage. Its flowers are sparse, greenish and inconspicuous, it sets little to no viable seed in cultivation, it carries no persistent seed head, and it has zero documented bird-seed value. The library agrees: the entry (plantLibrary.ts:2964) has no `bloom` array at all and is tagged `foliage|cascading`. The claim is manufactured by a blanket rule in compatibility.ts:433 - `seedheads: category === "Grass"` - which grants a standing-seed-head winter claim to every plant filed as Grass, including foliage grasses. The plant is in fact cut back in late winter and offers a collapsed tan mat, not structure. A beginner picking it for winter interest and bird food gets neither.
fix: In compatibility.ts:433, do not derive `seedheads` from the Grass category alone. Require the plant to have a `bloom`/plume record (or a curated `seedheads` trait) - e.g. `seedheads: (category === "Grass" && bloomMonths.some(Boolean)) || WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus)`. That alone removes the false claim for Hakonechloa and any other bloomless foliage grass.
scope: Every Grass-category companion with no flower/plume in the library; Hakonechloa macra (4 variants) is the clearest case.
notes: Confirmed in the data: the exact sentence is printed for cinnamon-fern -> japanese-forest-grass (chunk-06.json, score 41.3), and the same false clause is emitted for Hakonechloa under at least 9 sampled subjects (amaryllis, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger, cinnamon-fern). Scope note that strengthens the proposed fix: I checked every `cat: "Grass"` entry in plantLibrary.ts and the Hakonechloa group is the ONLY bloomless grass in the library, so requiring a bloom record before granting `seedheads` fixes exactly this plant and strips no legitimate seed-head grass. Reframe: the pair is a good, classic shade combination; only the winter clause is false. Severity medium (wrong reason, no plant-failure or safety risk).

Downgrade high -> medium. The pairing itself is horticulturally sound (cinnamon fern + Japanese forest grass is a textbook moist part-shade combination); the defect is one false clause in the generated reason plus 15 points of unearned winter-structure score. Blast radius is 9 pairings, all with japanese-forest-grass as the companion: amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger. Worth noting in the report that the engine contradicts itself on this plant: the same object literal sets `foliage: !bloomMonths.some(Boolean)` (true) and `seedheads` (true). Also correct the code quote to the full line: `seedheads: category === "Grass" || (genus !== null && WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus)),`.

Confirmed verbatim in the data. Two corrections to carry into the report: (a) downgrade high -> medium, because the pair is horticulturally good and the damage is a false claim plus rank inflation, not a plant that will fail; (b) scope it accurately - japanese-forest-grass is the ONLY bloomless Grass archetype in the generated set, so the bug's blast radius is one plant, but it corrupts all 9 of its recommendations (subjects: amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger). Worth quoting the engine's own comment at compatibility.ts:186-195 ("A claim we print gets a curated list") as the standard the Grass blanket at line 433 violates.

title: Cardinal Flower, a wet-soil native, is given two summer-dormant Mediterranean bulbs as companions
subject: cardinal-flower
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; blooms in early spring, before this one opens; lower at 0.25 m, for the front of the border; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: Lobelia cardinalis (correctly filed water: high) is a streambank and wet-meadow plant that must not dry out - the ground around it is kept constantly moist all summer. Hyacinthus orientalis is a Levantine/Mediterranean bulb that goes dormant in summer and needs a dry, sharply drained rest; kept wet through its dormancy the bulb rots, which is the single most common way gardeners lose it. Colchicum (also recommended, score 58.0) has the same summer-dormant corm and the same requirement. What drives this is not just the too-loose water rung: the 26-point bulb-cover bonus is actively rewarding "tuck bulbs under a leafy perennial", so the engine will keep steering dry-dormancy bulbs into the wettest plant in the border. A beginner plants the bulbs, they never come back, and nothing in the app explains why.
fix: Gate the bulb-cover bonus (and tighten the water rung) so a `water: high` perennial cannot take a summer-dormant Bulb as a companion; a `summerDormant` / `sharpDrainage` trait on Hyacinthus, Colchicum, Tulipa, Narcissus etc. in taxonomy.ts is the minimal seam. Cardinal Flower's honest bulb partner is a wet-tolerant one (e.g. Camassia), not a hyacinth.
scope: cardinal-flower -> hyacinth and -> colchicum; the same bulb-cover x high-water interaction will recur for every bog perennial in the library.
notes: Keep the pair (cardinal-flower -> hyacinth, score 57.8, 8th of 8) and the reason critique: the generated sentence "Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water" tells a beginner these two share a water regime when Lobelia cardinalis's culture (keep constantly moist all summer, tolerates flooding) is precisely what rots a dormant Hyacinthus bulb. Cause is the one-rung water tolerance (high vs moderate, only a x0.92 penalty), NOT the bulb-cover bonus - bulbCoverScore is null whenever the subject is not a spring ephemeral bulb, and hyacinth's own list is clean (baptisia, helenium, grasses, all low/moderate water). Drop the Colchicum sentence entirely: Colchicum autumnale is a damp-meadow plant, not a dry-summer Mediterranean corm. Honest fix seam is a water-rung tightening (or a summer-dormancy/sharp-drainage trait consulted in the hard filter) so a water:high perennial cannot take a dry-dormant Bulb; the bulb-cover gate in the proposed fix is aimed at the wrong factor.

Keep the pair; rewrite the mechanism and the fix. Correct mechanism: this pairing is produced purely by the one-rung water tolerance (moderate vs high, penalised only x0.92) plus succession + layering + pollinator points; bulbCover is null because the subject is not a Bulb (compatibility.ts:713-717, 664-668), and no bulb-cover clause appears in the reason. Drop the Colchicum example (C. autumnale is a damp-meadow corm, and the engine's isSpringEphemeral explicitly excludes autumn crocus, so it is not the same case). Drop the "gate the bulb-cover bonus" fix as inert for this pair; the honest fix is to forbid a water: high subject from taking a Bulb whose water is lower (or to add a summerDormant / sharpDrainage trait consulted by the hard filter). Scope in the data: subjects with water: high that get a Bulb companion = 20 pairs; hyacinth appears twice (cardinal-flower 57.8, hydrangea 52.8). Severity medium, not high: one bulb species, consequence is rotted bulbs and a wasted purchase, and it sits at rank 7-8 of an 8-item list rather than driving the whole list.

Report ONLY the pair, not the mechanism. Corrected framing: Cardinal Flower (Lobelia cardinalis, water: high - a streambank plant whose soil must never dry) is given Hyacinth (Hyacinthus orientalis, water: moderate) as its 8th and last shown companion, score 57.8. Hyacinth needs sharp drainage and a dry summer dormancy; a bed kept moist through July-September to satisfy the lobelia rots the bulb, so the beginner gets one season of bloom and no return. The coarse 3-rung water field hides this: "moderate" cannot express "must bake dry while dormant." Drop from the write-up: the bulb-cover mechanism (bulbCoverScore is null unless the subject is a spring-ephemeral bulb - it contributes nothing to a perennial's list, and hyacinth's own companion list contains no wet plants), the Colchicum example (C. autumnale is a damp-meadow plant, and autumn crocus is explicitly excluded from isSpringEphemeral), and the proposed fix (gating bulb-cover would not remove this pairing). The honest seam is either a summerDormant/sharpDrainage trait that blocks a water:high subject from taking a summer-dormant bulb, or noting that MAX_PER_CATEGORY = 3 is what pushes weak bulbs into the tail slots of a perennial-rich list.

title: Canterbury Bells is filed as an Annual; it is a biennial, and the mislabel also switches off the zone filter
subject: canterbury-bells
claim: plantLibrary.ts:3037 - { slug: "canterbury-bells", botanical: "Campanula medium", cat: "Annual", years: 1, bloom: [[5, 6, ...]], zones: "4-10" }
why: Campanula medium is the textbook hardy biennial: sown in year one it makes only a rosette, overwinters, and flowers in May-June of year two, then dies. Filed as `cat: "Annual", years: 1`, the app promises a beginner May-June flowers in the first season - they will get a leafy rosette and conclude they killed it, and the 3D year-scrub will animate a bloom that cannot happen. The mislabel also has a mechanical effect on this very audit's data: the engine SKIPS the USDA zone intersection whenever either plant is category Annual, so all 8 of Canterbury Bells' companions were matched with no zone check at all (hence Moonflower, zones 9-11, offered against a plant the library itself records as zones 4-10). A plant that must survive a winter in the ground should never be taking the annual exemption. Note the library already models this correctly elsewhere - Hollyhock (Alcea rosea, also a short-lived biennial) is filed `cat: "Perennial", years: 2`.
fix: Change the entry to `cat: "Perennial", years: 2` (matching the Hollyhock precedent) so the zone filter applies, and surface the biennial habit in the care copy ("sow one summer, flowers the next"). Longer term, a `biennial` lifecycle value would be more honest than borrowing either neighbour.
scope: The canterbury-bells entry itself, plus all 8 of its companion pairings, which were scored with the zone hard filter disabled.
refutations:
The botanical kernel is true (Campanula medium is a biennial) but every consequence the finding hangs on it is false or already handled, and it changes no pairing.

(a) THE ZONE-SKIP LEG IS ARITHMETICALLY WRONG. MIN_ZONE_OVERLAP = 1 (compatibility.ts:102) and Canterbury Bells is zones 4-10. Moonflower 9-11 intersects it at zones 9-10, a valid 2-zone overlap: it PASSES the zone filter. I re-ran the check by hand against the audit data for all 8 companions (helenium 3-8, false-sunflower 3-9, joe-pye-weed 4-9, sunflower [Annual, no zones], passionflower 6-9, moonflower 9-11, rose-of-sharon 5-9, flowering-cherry 5-8) and all 7 reverse subjects (dahlia 8-10, morning-glory [no zones], etc.). Not one of them would be rejected if the zone test were applied. The annual exemption admits nothing here; "hence Moonflower" is a false causal story.

(b) THE 3D-SCRUB LEG IS WRONG. src/garden/growth.ts (maturityOf / dimensionsOf) uses yearsToMaturity to scale SIZE only; bloom is never gated by maturity or lifecycle. Hollyhock (years: 2) and wisteria bloom in the scrub too. The proposed fix (Perennial, years 2) would not change the animation at all.
