(c) THE "BEGINNER IS MISLED" LEG IS ALREADY MITIGATED, IN THE SAME CARD. src/garden/intel/curated/canterbury-bells.json carries a sourced note, topic "Annual or biennial?": "Sold as an annual, but the species is really biennial: it flowers year two, then dies. Only bred strains like 'Champion' bloom their first year," plus an extraFact "as a biennial it dies after flowering, and self-sown seed is its only way to return." src/browse/PlantDetail.tsx:163 renders intel.notes in the very card that lists the companions. The proposed fix's second half is already shipped.

(d) THE CATEGORY IS A DOCUMENTED APPROXIMATION, NOT AN ERROR OF FACT. compatibility.ts:467-472 defines the Annual category as "grown as an annual" (hardiness range describes where it OVERWINTERS), which is exactly how Campanula medium is sold in the US trade; there is no Biennial category, and deepIntel/canterbury-bells.json:135 already records the call as a known nuance. The proposed Perennial is arguably a worse lie for a monocarpic plant that dies after flowering, and would tell a beginner it comes back.

Net: no pairing changes, no gardener is harmed, the honest habit is already on the card. Under a MATTERS lens this does not survive.
notes: Keep the finding, but as a pure library-data error with no companion-list consequence. Corrected wording: Canterbury Bells (Campanula medium, plantLibrary.ts:3037, plus variants 3038-3041) is filed cat: "Annual", years: 1 with a May-June bloom. It is a hardy biennial: sown one summer it makes a rosette, overwinters, and flowers the following May-June, then dies. The card and the 3D year-scrub therefore promise a beginner a first-season bloom that cannot happen, and the label is an outlier against the library's own biennial convention (hollyhock, foxglove, sweet-william and mullein are all cat: "Perennial", years: 2). Fix: cat: "Perennial", years: 2, and say "sow one summer, flowers the next" in the care copy. Delete the zone-filter/Moonflower argument: although the Annual category does skip the zone intersection (compatibility.ts:525), Canterbury Bells' 4-10 range overlaps every one of its 8 companions (Moonflower 9-11 overlaps at zones 9-10, and MIN_ZONE_OVERLAP is 1), so the mislabel admitted no bad pairing - correcting it would only add a narrow-zone penalty to the Moonflower score.

Keep the finding, but rewrite the consequences. Correct version: Campanula medium is a hardy biennial (rosette year one, flowers year two, then dies) yet is filed cat: "Annual", years: 1 at plantLibrary.ts:3037 (and in all 4 colour variants, 3038-3041), against the library's own convention for biennials - hollyhock (2980), foxglove (2136), sweet-william (3008) are all cat: "Perennial", years: 2. The repo's own research file already flags this (deepIntel/canterbury-bells.json:135: "The plain 'Annual' category is only defensible for the bred first-year-flowering strains such as 'Champion'"). Two real effects: (a) the user-facing category/tag reads "annual", telling a beginner to treat a plant that must overwinter as one-season bedding; (b) compatibility.ts:525 skips the USDA zone test whenever either plant is an Annual, so all 8 of this plant's companions were matched with the zone check off. DROP the Moonflower example: Canterbury Bells 4-10 and Moonflower 9-11 overlap at zones 9-10, so the pair still passes with the category fixed - the only change is that it would gain the breadth-2 narrow-zone penalty (x0.85) and the "they only overlap in zones 9 to 10" caution it is currently missing. No companion is actually rejected by the fix. Also DROP the year-scrub claim (the scrub shows a mature year for every plant, foxglove included) and drop "surface the biennial habit in the care copy" from the fix - curated/canterbury-bells.json:13 already says it. The fix that matters is cat: "Perennial", years: 2.

If any residue is kept in the report, it must be demoted to a low-severity catalog nit with the causal story corrected: (1) the annual exemption admits ZERO bad zone pairs for this plant (its 4-10 range intersects all 8 companions, incl. moonflower at 9-10, with MIN_ZONE_OVERLAP=1), so no pairing is affected; (2) the growth model never gates bloom on lifecycle, so the 3D scrub is unchanged either way; (3) the biennial habit is already disclosed in the same plant card via curated intel ("Annual or biennial?"); (4) "Perennial, years 2" is not the right fix for a monocarpic biennial. Do not cite Moonflower as a consequence.

title: Cornflower is told to plant Pampas Grass, and the reason praises the exact organ that makes it invasive
subject: cornflower
claim: "Shares full sun and low water; blooms in late summer as this one finishes; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it." (score 60.3, 5th of 8)
why: Cortaderia selloana is a Cal-IPC "High" invasive, a listed/regulated weed in California, the Pacific NW, Texas and Hawaii, and banned outright in New Zealand. A single female plume carries on the order of 100,000 wind-borne seeds, and dry thatch inside the clump is a documented fire hazard; the leaf blades are razor-edged and routinely cut hands. Its library zones (7-11) are precisely the invaded range. The generated reason recommends it FOR the plumes standing through winter "for the birds" - i.e. for the seed-dispersal organ itself - with no caution. The library is inconsistent about this: taxonomy.ts flags `miscanthus: { spreader: true }` with the comment "invasive in much of the US", while `cortaderia: {}` is annotated "confidence: high - no applicable traits", so pampas takes no spreader penalty at all.
fix: In src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts set `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` (matching the miscanthus precedent) so it takes the x0.8 penalty, and either exclude Cortaderia from the companion pool or attach an invasive-range caution to any recommendation of it. At minimum, suppress the "seed heads ... for the birds" clause for it.
scope: Every subject in zones 7-11 that can be paired with pampas-grass; the same missing spreader flag applies to the pampas-grass-pink/gold/dwarf variants.
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Trim the regulatory over-claim: for C. selloana specifically, Cal-IPC "High" is a non-regulatory inventory (the CDFA-listed noxious weed is C. jubata), and Texas lists it on invasive advisory lists rather than a state prohibited list. Use the audit's own safer wording: "Invasive in CA, HI, TX and the Southeast; a listed noxious weed in several counties." The New Zealand National Pest Plant Accord ban (sale, propagation, distribution) is accurate as stated. (2) The proposed fix is blunt: setting cortaderia: { spreader: true } produces the clause "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging" (compatibility.ts:1146), which describes a creeping clump, not wind-dispersed seed escaping into wildland, so it misleads in a different direction. The real fix is a distinct invasive/self-seed flag with its own caution clause, plus suppression of the "for the birds" praise - pampas seed is not a documented bird food (the plume is nesting fluff at best), so that clause is separately dubious. Also worth noting the scale: invasive-recount.json shows pampas praised on all 191 companion lists it appears in, cautioned on zero, so this pair is one instance of a systemic gap.

Three corrections for the report. (1) Reframe "no caution" as an INTERNAL CONTRADICTION rather than silence: src/garden/intel/curated/pampas-grass.json already carries free-tier facts "Invasive; check local lists ... Cal-IPC rates it 'High'" and "Leaf blades cut skin", while taxonomy.ts calls the same genus "no applicable traits" and the engine praises the plume. The caution only reaches the reader if they open pampas's own card; the recommendation on cornflower's card carries none. (2) It is systemic, not a one-off: data/invasive-recount.json shows pampas-grass lists=191, praised=191, cautioned=0, praise phrase "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds" (butterfly-bush and maiden-grass sit in the same table, also cautioned 0). Cornflower is the named instance; say so. (3) Weaken the proposed fix: `spreader` in this engine means "runs and crowds in the bed" and emits the caution "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging" - botanically wrong for a clumping, wind-seed-dispersed grass. Setting cortaderia spreader:true buys the score penalty but ships a misleading caution; the correct fix is a distinct invasive flag (own caution text / pool exclusion) plus suppressing the seed-head clause. Also drop the unsupported "listed/regulated weed in Texas" wording; CA / HI / Pacific NW / NZ are the defensible ones.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Scope: this is not a one-off pair. invasive-recount.json shows pampas-grass on 191 companion lists, 0 cautioned, 191 praised, with the recorded praise phrase being exactly "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds". Cornflower -> pampas-grass (score 60.3, 5th of 8) is the named instance, but the fix must be plant-level. (2) The proposed fix is only half a fix: `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` applies the x0.8 penalty (60.3 -> ~48), which lowers the rank but leaves pampas still recommended on thin lists and leaves the "seed heads ... for the birds" clause intact. The part that actually closes the harm is suppressing that praise clause for Cortaderia and/or excluding it from the companion pool (or attaching an invasive-range caution). Note also the existing precedent in the same file: miscanthus already carries `spreader: true` explicitly because it "self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US", so the engine already has the concept - it simply misclassified the more prolific seeder as having "no applicable traits".

title: Climbing Hydrangea is white, but three of its eight reasons tell the user about "the orange"
subject: climbing-hydrangea
claim: serviceberry: "...its white flowers settle the orange." Also solomons-seal and turtlehead: "...a soft colour beside the orange."
why: Climbing Hydrangea's bloom hex in plantLibrary.ts:2613 is #f3f0ea (a cream white; the plant is tagged "white"). colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts:83 tests `l >= 0.82 && s < 0.25` for white. For #f3f0ea the HSL lightness is 0.935 but HSL saturation computes to ~0.27 (near-white hues get a numerically large s because the denominator 2-max-min collapses), so the white branch fails, it falls through to `h < 45` and is named ORANGE. The user is therefore told that white Serviceberry flowers "settle the orange" of a white-flowered vine - a sentence that is both false and self-evidently nonsense to anyone who has seen the plant - and that pink Turtlehead and white Solomon's Seal are "a soft colour beside the orange".
fix: In colorName(), decide white on chroma, not HSL saturation: return "white" when the channel spread (max-min)/255 is <= ~0.06 and l >= 0.82, before the hue tests. #f3f0ea has a spread of 9/255 = 0.035 and would then correctly read white, which also switches the clause to a legitimate white-on-white or contrast statement.
scope: Any plant whose bloom hex is a warm near-white (checked: jasmine #fbfbf5 and pampas #f0e8d0 also miss the white branch), plus every companion reason generated against them.
notes: Confirmed verbatim in the shipped data (chunks/chunk-07.json). Two clarifications worth carrying into the report: (1) the pairings themselves are horticulturally fine (serviceberry, Solomon's seal, turtlehead are all part-shade, moderate-to-high water, zone 4-8 with the vine) - the defect is purely the colour clause, but it sits inside otherwise-correct sentences and so undermines trust in them. (2) The root cause is not confined to climbing hydrangea: the same near-white/cream mis-bucketing produces "the orange" clauses for maiden-grass (#e6d8b0), fountain-grass (#d8c0a0), northern-sea-oats, goatsbeard and calla-lily. The proposed chroma-based white test (channel spread <= ~0.06 at l >= 0.82) fixes #f3f0ea, but the tan grass plumes have a wider spread and need their own neutral/cream handling rather than "orange".

Verified end to end. Two amplifications worth carrying into the report: (1) the same colorName fault hits other subjects, not just this one - `calla-lily` (companions anemone, ranunculus) and `goatsbeard` (companions pansy, azalea) also ship "the orange" clauses despite being tagged white; (2) it also corrupts the COMPANION side: Solomon's Seal (#fbfbf2 -> h=60, s=0.53) and Jasmine (#fbfbf5) are both named "yellow", so white-flowered companions never earn the legitimate "its white flowers settle the X" clause. The proposed chroma-based fix resolves all of these (#f3f0ea spread 0.035, #fbfbf5 spread 0.024, #fbfbf2 spread 0.035). Note the pairings themselves are horticulturally sound part-shade woodland matches - the defect is confined to the colour clause, which is the lowest-weighted factor (5 pts) but is nonetheless printed to the user as a plainly false statement.

Verified: plantLibrary.ts climbing-hydrangea bloom #f3f0ea (tagged "white"); colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts:83 returns "orange" (h=40, s=0.273, l=0.935). Confirmed in generated data at C:/Users/etano/bloomseye-reports/compatibility-audit/data/chunks/chunk-07.json: serviceberry "...its white flowers settle the orange.", turtlehead and solomons-seal "...a soft colour beside the orange." (3 of 8 companions). Two corrections to carry into the report: (1) Severity is medium, not high - the misnaming is copy-only. colourScore's isNeutralBloom() (s < 0.2 || l > 0.85) still classifies the bloom as neutral and scores it 0.85, so the companion set and ranking are unaffected; no beginner is pushed into a bad planting, only a false sentence. (2) It is NOT specific to climbing-hydrangea and should be reported as one systemic colorName() bug: re-running colorName over the library, 259 plants tagged "white" are misnamed, e.g. shasta-daisy-becky/-alaska/-snowcap, phlox-david, yarrow-white, bee-balm-snow-white, salvia-snow-hill, catmint-snowflake, narcissus-thalia/-actaea, crocus-jeanne-darc, hyacinth-carnegie, allium-mount-everest -> "yellow"; peony-festiva-maxima, peony-duchesse-de-nemours, narcissus-mount-hood, dahlia-fleurel, daylily-joan-senior -> "orange". flags.json confirms user-visible fallout beyond this pair (bleeding-heart-alba -> climbing-hydrangea reads "a soft colour beside the yellow" for a white bleeding heart; solomons-seal likewise named "yellow"). The proposed chroma-based fix (decide white on (max-min) spread <= ~0.06 with l >= 0.82, before the hue tests) is correct and fixes all 259 in one function.

title: Coral Bells is filed with no bloom at all, so all eight of its pairings degrade to a content-free sentence
subject: coral-bells
claim: rhododendron / camellia / bergenia / hellebore / autumn-fern all read essentially "Shares part shade and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter." (scores 33-43, the lowest block in the chunk)
why: plantLibrary.ts:2059 gives Heuchera no `bloom` array at all. Heuchera does bloom: airy wands of small bells from roughly May to July, and they are a well-documented hummingbird and small-bee nectar source (Heuchera sanguinea is a classic hummingbird plant). Because bloom is empty, the engine can score neither succession (30 pts, the heaviest factor) nor pollinator overlap, so every Coral Bells reason collapses to a restatement of the hard filter it already passed ("shares part shade and moderate water") plus one fact about the OTHER plant. Four of the eight say nothing whatsoever about why these two go together - which is exactly the beginner question the card exists to answer. It also means Heuchera can never be offered to anything else as a late-spring succession or pollinator partner.
fix: Add a bloom span to the coral-bells archetype, e.g. `bloom: [[5, 7, "#e8899a"]]` (keeping `tags: ["foliage", "shade"]`, since it is still grown for leaves). The eight reasons then gain real succession and pollinator clauses.
scope: All 8 coral-bells pairings, plus every pairing in which Heuchera would have been a candidate companion.
refutations: Overstated and harmless. (a) The reasons are not content-free: all eight state shared conditions plus a real benefit, and four of eight also carry a height-layering clause ("taller at 1.8 m, for the layer behind it"). "Shares part shade and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter" is true, useful, and is precisely the beginner's question. Nothing false is said. (b) No pairing is wrong: Heuchera with rhododendron/camellia/hellebore/bergenia/ferns/northern sea oats is a textbook shade bed, so no gardener is harmed. (c) The "wrong data" charge is a deliberate convention, not an error: intel/taxonomy.ts:175 reads `heuchera: { noChop: true }, // coral bells (foliage)` and plant.ts:130 comments "excludes hosta / heuchera / astilbe"; Hosta is filed bloomless the same way. The archetype is generic Heuchera grown for leaves. (d) The proposed fix would not deliver what it promises: FORAGE_RANK (compatibility.ts:719) is `{ high: 1, some: 0.5, none: 0, unknown: 0 }` and plant.ts:113-117 derives pollinator value as curated-trait > "pollinator" tag > (foliage ? "none" : "unknown"). Adding a bloom span moves Coral Bells from "none" to "unknown", which still ranks 0, so pollinatorScore stays 0 and NO pollinator clause ever appears. Two of the four "empty" reasons are ferns (bloom ""), so succession cannot fire for them either: the fix would touch at most 2 of 8 sentences while making the archetype render flowers its 15 sibling cultivars lack. (e) The "never offered to anything else" claim is a ranking artifact: archetype-standing.json shows 66 of 276 archetypes with recommended: 0, including daylily, which has a full bloom span.
notes: Corrections to carry into the report. (1) The pollinator half of the diagnosis is wrong: pollinator value is not derived from bloom. src/garden/intel/plant.ts:113-127 sets it from the curated genus trait, else a "pollinator" tag, else none/unknown; pollinatorScore ranks both none and unknown at 0. taxonomy.ts:175 has heuchera: { noChop: true } with no pollinator trait, so adding bloom alone moves Heuchera from "none" to "unknown" - still zero, still no pollinator clause. The fix must also add a curated trait (pollinator: "some") or a pollinator tag. (2) "The eight reasons then gain real succession and pollinator clauses" is false: two of the eight companions are ferns (christmas-fern, autumn-fern), which are bloomless, so successionScore stays null for them - at most six of the eight improve. (3) Unflagged side effect: setting bloom flips `foliage` to false in plantIntel, and coverHasSpringPresence (compatibility.ts:691) grants full bulb-cover credit via `cover.evergreen || cover.foliage` (the comment literally names heuchera as the archetypal cover). With bloom 5-7 and no evergreen flag, Heuchera would drop to LATE_COVER_SCORE for early ephemerals (crocus, snowdrop, scilla) whose bloom ends before May - so the fix needs an evergreen/semi-evergreen flag or the cover path adjusted, or it trades a reason gain for a bulb-cover regression. (4) The gap affects all ~14 Heuchera cultivars, not just the archetype, since each cultivar entry is likewise bloomless. (5) Tone down "four of the eight say nothing whatsoever": those reasons do state one real benefit (winter structure); the correct charge is that they are thin and generic and omit the pair's actual selling point (e.g. hellebore blooms Feb-Apr and would hand off to Heuchera's May-July flowers).

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Succession is NOT unscored: compatibility.ts:606 returns `ONE_SIDED_BLOOM = 0.5` whenever exactly one side is foliage, so coral-bells already banks a flat 15 of 30 points against every flowering companion. The correct statement is that it can never earn a REAL succession score, and the reason writer suppresses the succession CLAUSE entirely -- which is what the eight empty reasons show. (2) The proposed fix is incomplete. Missing bloom does zero the pollinator factor, but through plant.ts:116 (`else if (foliage) pollinatorValue = "none"`). Adding a bloom array alone moves heuchera only from "none" to "unknown" -- taxonomy.ts:175 is `heuchera: { noChop: true }` with no `pollinator` trait and no "pollinator" tag -- and FORAGE_RANK scores BOTH "none" and "unknown" as 0, so pollinatorScore still returns 0 and no pollinator clause appears. The fix must ALSO add `pollinator: "some"` (Heuchera sanguinea is a documented hummingbird/small-bee nectar source) to the heuchera taxonomy entry. Minor: a real May-July bloom will LOWER succession below the current flat 0.5 for companions blooming in the same window, so a few of the eight may reshuffle; "the eight reasons then gain" is not uniformly true. Also worth noting hosta (plantLibrary.ts:2074) has the identical missing-bloom problem, if the report wants to widen the fix.

If any version of this is kept, it must be reframed as a low-priority richness/polish note (Heuchera's flowers are unmodelled by an intentional foliage-plant convention), and the proposed fix must be corrected: adding a bloom array alone yields pollinatorValue "unknown" (FORAGE_RANK 0), so it produces no pollinator clause; a curated `pollinator` trait or a "pollinator" tag would be required, and the two fern pairings would still gain nothing.

title: Japanese Forest Grass is credited with seed heads that "stand through winter, for structure and the birds" - it has neither
subject: coleus
claim: "Shares part shade and moderate water; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds." (score 33.2)
why: Hakonechloa macra is a foliage grass. Its library entry (plantLibrary.ts:2964) has no bloom at all and is tagged foliage/cascading; in the garden its panicles are wispy and inconspicuous, the clump goes tan and flops to mush by midwinter in its own zones 5-9, and it feeds no birds. The claim comes from winterStructureScore's blanket `category === "Grass"` route into `seedheads`. That is the very error the code's own comment warns against for perennials ("it would have this engine tell a gardener that English daisy ... holds its seed heads through winter. A claim we print gets a curated list.") - the curated genus list was built for the composites but grasses were left as a blanket category. Here the false clause is also the ONLY benefit the sentence offers: strip it and the reason says nothing.
fix: In compatibility.ts, require grasses to have real seed interest before setting `seedheads`: gate the Grass route on a plume/seed-heads tag or a non-empty bloom span (feather-reed, fountain, switchgrass, northern sea oats and pampas all qualify; hakonechloa and the other foliage grasses do not).
scope: Every pairing with japanese-forest-grass (and its 3 variants) and any other bloom-less foliage grass in the library.
notes: Two wording/scope corrections for the report. (1) Do not say Hakonechloa "has no bloom at all" as a botanical fact - it does flower (inconspicuous yellow-green panicles in late summer, hidden in the mound); the accurate, unassailable claim is that it has no seed heads that STAND through winter and no bird value, and that its LIBRARY entry carries no bloom span, so the engine prints seed heads for a plant it does not even model as flowering. (2) The false clause is not a one-off: the identical sentence is generated for japanese-forest-grass on 9 subject lists (amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger), which strengthens the finding. Caveat on the proposed fix: gating the Grass route on a non-empty bloom span alone would still let Blue Fescue (Festuca glauca, bloom June, a semi-evergreen tuft with no winter-standing seed heads) make the same claim; the "seed heads" tag / curated list is the safer gate.

Scope is confirmed and slightly broader than stated, in the finding's favour: the false seed-head clause appears in ALL 9 of Japanese forest grass's companion appearances in the dataset (amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger). It is also the only archetype grass with an empty bloom span - the other nine grasses (blue fescue, blue oat, feather-reed, fountain, little bluestem, maiden, northern sea oats, pampas, switchgrass) all have bloom, and the genuine plume/seed grasses carry a `plume` or `seed heads` tag - so the proposed gate (require a plume/seed-heads tag or non-empty bloom before setting `seedheads` on a Grass) fixes this case without stripping the clause from any grass that has earned it.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Precision on the botany: Hakonechloa does produce inconspicuous yellow-green panicles in late summer, so "it has neither" is slightly overstated; the false parts are that the seed heads STAND through winter and feed BIRDS (it sets little seed, is division-propagated, and the tan clump collapses). Anchor the claim to the library instead: the entry has no `bloom` field and is tagged `foliage|green|shade|cascading`, versus northern-sea-oats which carries `bloom: [[7,9,...]]` and an explicit `seed heads` tag. (2) Scope, verified: the clause fires 9 times for japanese-forest-grass at archetype level (subjects amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger), and in coleus and lady-fern it is the sole benefit clause; because the chunks sample only the 276 archetype subjects while all 1,611 plants get lists, the true number of cards showing it is a multiple of that. Hakonechloa is the ONLY bloomless grass archetype in the pool (every other grass carries a bloom span), so the proposed bloom/tag gate is surgical and changes nothing else. Small residue: blue-fescue (bloom 6-7, 2 pairings) would still pass a bloom-based gate while being a grass whose flower spikes are routinely sheared off.

title: Climbing Hydrangea tells the user to "carpet the ground beneath it" with a 1.2 m azalea and with monkshood
subject: climbing-hydrangea
claim: azalea: "...low at 1.2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it." Same clause for monkshood (1.2 m) and northern-sea-oats (1 m).
why: A 1.2 m x 1.2 m evergreen azalea is not a groundcover and cannot carpet anything; a beginner who reads that will plant azaleas as a carpet tight against the wall the vine is on and end up with three shrubs crammed into the vine's root run. The cause is in layeringClause(): a Climber is explicitly excluded from being a canopy when it is the CANDIDATE ("A climber carries height on a support, so it is never a canopy"), but when the climber is the SUBJECT the code only checks `a.height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M` (8 m >= 6), so the same 8 m vine flat on a wall is treated as an overhead tree canopy with shaded ground beneath it. The engine contradicts its own stated rule. It is worse for the monkshood pairing: Aconitum is one of the most poisonous plants in cultivation (aconitine is absorbed through skin; gardeners are told to wear gloves), and "carpet the ground with it" is the last thing to say about it.
fix: Apply the existing climber exemption in both directions: in layeringClause, exclude `a.category === "Climber"` from the STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M canopy branch, and additionally gate the "carpet the ground" wording on the shorter plant's absolute height (say <= 0.3 m) and on it not being a Shrub.
scope: 3 of 8 climbing-hydrangea pairings; every subject that is a tall Climber (>= 6 m).
notes: Keep the finding, but (a) drop or heavily trim the monkshood-toxicity argument: the clause describes layering, not handling, Aconitum is a mainstream perennial sold to beginners, and skin absorption from intact top growth is a marginal risk (ingestion of roots is the real one). The load-bearing defect is the layering wording plus the engine contradicting its own climber rule. (b) The bug is systemic, not confined to climbing hydrangea: the same climber-as-subject path also yields boston-ivy -> rhododendron "low at 1.8 m, to carpet the ground beneath it", boston-ivy -> maiden-grass (1.8 m), and wisteria -> helenium "low at 1 m, to carpet the ground beneath it", plus 8 "an understory layer" clauses under vines (trumpet-vine -> lilac 3 m, wisteria -> pampas-grass 2.5 m). 17 affected clauses across 4 climber subjects (boston-ivy, climbing-hydrangea, trumpet-vine, wisteria). Cite one of the extra pairs so the report shows it is a class, not a one-off.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Widen the scope: the same STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M branch also tells boston-ivy (12 m) to "carpet the ground beneath it" with a 1.8 m RHODODENDRON (and 1.8 m maiden grass), and wisteria (8 m) with 1 m helenium. Separately, climbers under 6 m (clematis, climbing-rose, jasmine, morning-glory, mandevilla, black-eyed-susan-vine, all 2.5-4 m) already reach the OTHER carpet arm (line 1077, tallEnoughToShelter) and emit "to carpet the ground beneath" - so exempting Climber from the STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M branch alone does not fix the family. The absolute-height floor on the shorter plant (and the not-a-Shrub gate) is the load-bearing half of the fix and must apply to BOTH carpet arms. Across the whole dataset 284 pairs carry a "carpet the ground" clause, of which the companion is 1 m or taller in 15 - those 15 are the wrong ones. (2) Soften the monkshood angle: the clause never tells anyone to handle Aconitum, and toxicity is simply not a modelled factor, so leading with "aconitine is absorbed through skin" invites a fair "you are reporting an unmodelled factor" pushback. The wording is wrong for monkshood for exactly the reason it is wrong for the azalea - a 1.2 m plant is not a carpet. Keep toxicity as a footnote.

Reframe as a generic wording bug, not a Climbing Hydrangea / climber bug: layeringClause applies "low at Xm, to carpet the ground beneath it" whenever the height ratio exceeds CANOPY_RATIO (6), with no floor on the shorter plant's absolute height. Confirmed instances in the data: climbing-hydrangea -> azalea (1.2 m), monkshood (1.2 m), northern-sea-oats (1 m); blue-spruce -> ninebark (2 m); linden -> butterfly-bush (2 m); saucer-magnolia -> baptisia (1 m); dogwood-tree -> northern-sea-oats (1 m). The only effective fix is gating the "carpet" wording on the shorter plant's absolute height (roughly <= 0.3 m, the engine's own FRONT_TIER band) and on it not being a Shrub; excluding Climber from the STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M branch changes nothing, since the fallback branch emits the same sentence. Drop the monkshood toxicity argument - it is out of the engine's declared scope and the pairing itself is sound.

title: Climbing Rose is paired with Eastern White Pine, an 18 m forest conifer, as "the layer behind it"
subject: climbing-rose
claim: "Shares full sun and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; taller at 18 m, for the layer behind it." (score 47.2)
why: These two cannot share a spot. Pinus strobus matures at 18 m with a 9 m spread; a climbing rose needs 6+ hours of direct sun, free air movement to stay clear of blackspot, and its own support structure. Planted "behind" the rose the pine will, within a decade, shade it into non-flowering, out-compete a shallow-rooted rose for water, and bury it in acidic needle duff. "Evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter" is also a strange thing to say about a forest tree that IS the skyline, not bed structure. The engine allows it because its only size test is the 15:1 height ratio (18/3 = 6, passes) and it reads taller purely as "behind"; spread and canopy shade are never considered. The same coarse rule produces cleome -> crabapple (6 m, 6 m spread) for a sun-hungry annual.
fix: Do not let a plant above the canopy threshold (roughly >= 10 m, or Conifer/Tree with a spread greater than the subject's height) score as a height-layering partner for a sub-3 m full-sun subject; either drop the pairing or change the clause from "for the layer behind it" to an explicit "plant it well clear - at maturity it will shade this out".
scope: climbing-rose -> eastern-white-pine; the same canopy-shade blindness affects cleome -> crabapple in this chunk.
notes: Verified against data and engine. Sharper detail worth carrying into the report: the ratio is EXACTLY 6.0, and the canopy wording in layeringClause (compatibility.ts:1053-1068) is gated on `r > CANOPY_RATIO` with CANOPY_RATIO = 6 (strict inequality), so this pair misses "stands well above at 18 m, a canopy to plant beneath" by a hair and falls through to the border-gradation sentence "taller at 18 m, for the layer behind it" - which contradicts the engine's own comment that a plant over 3 m "is not a back-of-border plant, it is structure". Drop the two bad sub-claims from the write-up: pine needles do NOT meaningfully acidify soil (long-debunked myth), and roses are not shallow-rooted (0.6-1 m+). State the harm as: shade below the rose's 6 h full-sun minimum, surface-root competition across the pine's 9 m spread, and poor air movement raising blackspot pressure. Supporting example cleome -> crabapple (63.9, "taller at 6 m, for the layer behind it") is also real in the data.

Correct the mechanism before publishing. The finding says the engine "reads taller purely as 'behind'" and never considers canopy. That is wrong, and the truth is more damning. The engine HAS canopy handling on both paths: layeringScore (compatibility.ts:637-653) sees tierOf(18)=3 and correctly routes this through its canopy-over-underplanting branch, damping layering to 0.53. And layeringClause (line 1049-1067) has a purpose-built canopy sentence: `const CANOPY_RATIO = 6; if (r > CANOPY_RATIO && tallEnoughToShelter) return "stands well above at 18 m, a canopy to plant beneath";`. The pair misses that safeguard on an EXACT TIE: 18/3 = 6.0 and the test is a strict `>`, so it falls through to "taller at 18 m, for the layer behind it". The output is therefore internally inconsistent - scored as a canopy, described as back-of-border. Minimal fix is `r >= CANOPY_RATIO`. Note that fix alone only corrects the sentence to "a canopy to plant beneath", which is still poor advice for a sun-hungry, blackspot-prone climbing rose under an 18 m acidic-duff conifer, so the finding's stronger proposal (bar >=10 m canopy trees as height-layering partners for a sub-3 m full-sun subject) remains justified. Severity stays medium: it is the lowest-ranked of 8 and sits behind a "show all" expander, but it is shown and it is actively misleading.

Keep the pair, but rewrite the mechanism and drop two supports. (1) WRONG: "the engine reads taller purely as behind; canopy shade is never considered". layeringClause DOES have a canopy branch (CANOPY_RATIO = 6, STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M = 6, compatibility.ts ~L1047-1067). This pair sits exactly on the boundary: 18/3 = 6.0, which is not > 6, so it falls through to "taller at 18 m, for the layer behind it". A 2.9 m rose would have printed "stands well above at 18 m, a canopy to plant beneath". It is a boundary-value miss, not an absent concept. (2) CUT the cleome -> crabapple parallel: a 6 m x 6 m crabapple as a backdrop to a bed of annuals is a textbook sound pairing, and citing it as a failure undermines the report. (3) CUT "bury it in acidic needle duff": fallen pine needles do not meaningfully acidify soil (a known garden myth). Argue shade and root competition only. ADD the stronger supporting fact: the misjudgment is consistent across the system (crowding.ts treats the rose as legitimately underplanting the pine), so the rose's full-sun requirement is never tested against the shade its recommended companion casts. Severity dropped to low: exactly one subject is affected (the pine's other 3 pairings, with river-birch, boston-ivy and bald-cypress, are all appropriate), the pairing is ranked last of 8 behind a premium reveal plus a "Show all" expand, and the planner renders the 9 m footprint to scale before the user commits.

title: Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) is recommended to Creeping Phlox and praised for the exact trait that makes it invasive, with no caution
subject: creeping-phlox
claim: Shares full sun and low water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months.
why: Buddleia davidii is a listed noxious weed in Washington (Class B) and Oregon, where sale of non-sterile forms is restricted, and it is naturalised on riverbanks and disturbed ground across the Pacific Northwest, the mid-Atlantic and much of Europe. A single panicle sets tens of thousands of wind-borne seeds. The reason sells it to a beginner on its nectar draw and gives no hint of a problem. The pollinator claim is also overstated: buddleia is a nectar bar for adult butterflies and hosts no North American larvae, so it feeds butterflies while displacing the natives their caterpillars need. The library entry (plantLibrary.ts:2464) carries tags [purple, pollinator, fragrant] and no invasiveness flag, so nothing downstream can warn. Same pattern in this chunk: crape-myrtle -> maiden-grass (Miscanthus sinensis, plantLibrary.ts:2621), invasive through the Southeast and mid-Atlantic and recommended with 'its seed heads stand through winter' - i.e. praised for its seed production, in zones 6-10 where it seeds freely.
fix: Add an `invasive` flag to the library and either exclude flagged plants from the archetype companion pool or force a caution clause into the reason. Set it at minimum on butterfly-bush and maiden-grass; if excluding is too blunt, swap the recommendation to a sterile cultivar (Buddleia 'Blue Chip'/Lo & Behold) and a clumping Miscanthus alternative.
scope: 2 pairings in this chunk directly; every list where butterfly-bush or maiden-grass surfaces as an archetype companion
notes: Fix the title's framing. "Praised for the exact trait that makes it invasive" is not true of this reason: B. davidii's invasiveness comes from prolific wind-borne seed, and the reason praises sun/water match, bloom succession, height layering and pollinator draw - none of which is the seeding trait. That phrasing is exactly right for the engine's grass reasons (crape-myrtle -> maiden-grass: "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds", confirmed verbatim; and pampas-grass, 191 lists) but not for buddleia. Retitle to something like: "Butterfly Bush (straight Buddleia davidii) is recommended to Creeping Phlox with no caution, sold on a pollinator benefit that is ecologically misleading." Body should keep both halves of the real problem: (a) it is a state-listed noxious weed whose non-sterile sale is restricted in WA and OR, recommended to beginners across 42 lists with 0 cautions; (b) "both are strong pollinator plants" overstates - it is an adult-nectar bar that hosts no NA larvae, so it feeds butterflies while displacing the natives their caterpillars need. Also correct one factual slip: maiden-grass is zones "5-9" in the library (plantLibrary.ts:2621), not 6-10. Supporting detail worth one clause: the reason also tells a beginner to plant a full-sun, drought-tolerant mat "beneath" a 2 m canopy, which is separately poor advice for Phlox subulata.

Correct the reasoning before publishing: do NOT say "no invasiveness flag, so nothing downstream can warn." The repo already holds sourced invasiveness data in src/garden/intel/curated/butterfly-bush.json ("Regulated noxious weed... illegal to sell in Oregon and Washington except approved sterile cultivars; one flower cluster can set more than 40,000 seeds", Penn State/Cal-IPC) and the exact larval-host point ("feeds adult butterflies but hosts no native caterpillars"), plus the same for curated/maiden-grass.json. The accurate statement is: the compatibility engine never consults the curated intel it already has. cautionClause() (compatibility.ts:1146-1150) fires only on the `spreader` penalty (vegetative runners), so seed-borne invasiveness cannot produce a clause; the warning exists only on the plant's own detail card, while the recommendation sits on the SUBJECT's card with a one-tap "Add to garden" button under the reason (PlantCompanions.tsx:137-144), bypassing it entirely. Restate the fix accordingly: reuse the existing curated records rather than inventing a new library flag - the audit's own invasive-recount.json already names the tier-1 set (pampas-grass, maiden-grass, butterfly-bush, periwinkle, siberian-squill). Scope note: pampas-grass is a larger instance of the identical bug (191 lists, 191 praised, 0 cautioned, praised for "its seed heads stand through winter"); the named creeping-phlox -> butterfly-bush pair is real but is not the worst case, and the report should say so.

Fix the framing for this specific pair. 'Praised for the exact trait that makes it invasive' is literally true of maiden-grass and pampas-grass (seed-head persistence = seed production) but NOT of butterfly-bush: its reason praises nectar draw, bloom timing and height, never seed. Buddleia's invasiveness is wind-borne seed, which the reason does not touch. Similarly, 'both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months' is not a false benefit - buddleia is a real adult-nectar plant and the bloom windows (Apr-May vs Jun-Sep) do differ. The accurate charge for creeping-phlox -> butterfly-bush is: a regulated noxious weed (WA Class B, OR quarantine) recommended to beginners with zero caution, sold on the nectar draw that is exactly what gets it planted, while it hosts no North American larvae. Keep the seed-praise wording for the grass examples only. Worth adding to the fix: the safe swap is already in the library - the butterfly-bush variant group's default could be pointed at the low-fertility 'Lo & Behold Blue Chip' / 'Pugster Blue' instead of the straight species.

title: Flowering Crabapple -> Zinnia and -> Dill: full-sun annuals told to 'carpet the ground beneath' a 6 m dense-canopy tree
subject: crabapple
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months; low at 0.8 m, to carpet the ground beneath it.
why: The reason instructs the user to plant zinnias underneath a Malus with a 6 m spread. The light hard filter compares the two plants' own sun ratings (both 'full-sun') and never asks what light the underplanting actually receives once it is under a crabapple's canopy: that is dry shade over a mat of shallow Malus feeder roots. Zinnia elegans is the worst possible candidate for it - the standard zinnia failure mode is powdery mildew and alternaria in shade and still, humid air, so a beginner gets thin, mildewed, barely-flowering plants and reads it as their own fault. Dill (same subject, 'low at 0.9 m, to carpet the ground beneath it') is a taprooted full-sun annual that leans and bolts weakly in shade and resents root competition. `layeringClause` (compatibility.ts:1072-1075) emits 'carpet the ground beneath it' for any Tree/Conifer subject above the canopy ratio, without checking that the companion tolerates shade.
fix: In `layeringClause`, only emit the 'carpet the ground beneath it' / canopy-underplanting wording when the shorter plant is rated part-shade or shade tolerant; for a full-sun companion under a Tree subject, either drop the layering credit or reword to 'plant it at the tree's sunny edge, not under the canopy'.
scope: crabapple -> zinnia and crabapple -> dill in this chunk; every Tree/Conifer subject with a full-sun companion above CANOPY_RATIO
notes: Two refinements. (1) The defect is best framed as a wrong REASON, not an intrinsically impossible pairing: zinnias/dill beside a crabapple, outside the drip line, are fine; it is the "carpet the ground beneath it" instruction that fails. (2) The bug is systemic, not a one-off, and the proposed fix is too narrow. The sibling branch of the same ternary emits "shorter at 1.2 m, an understory layer" for Cleome under the same crabapple, which is the same under-canopy instruction below the ratio threshold, so both branches need the shade-tolerance gate. Across the corpus, 11 rendered pairings put a full-sun companion under a Tree/Conifer subject with the "carpet the ground beneath it" wording: Weeping Willow -> Joe Pye Weed / Perennial Sunflower / Canna Lily, Littleleaf Linden -> Butterfly Bush, Tulip Tree -> Rose of Sharon, Saucer Magnolia -> False Indigo, Arborvitae -> Bee Balm, Juniper -> Butterfly Weed, Colorado Blue Spruce -> Ninebark, plus the two crabapple cases. Minor: dill's library category is Herb, not Annual (it is an annual herb, so the finding's substance is unaffected). Cited line range 1072-1075 is actually 1073-1075.

One small correction to carry into the report: dill is filed as cat "Herb" with zones 3-11, not category Annual (only zinnia is an Annual and thus zone-filter-exempt). Botanically Anethum graveolens is an annual, so the horticultural argument is unaffected, but the report should not imply the library files dill as an annual. Otherwise the finding is exact: full-sun annuals/herbs are being told to "carpet the ground beneath" a 6 m x 6 m Malus, and layeringClause's Tree branch never asks whether the companion tolerates shade.

Two wording corrections for the report. (a) Call the crabapple canopy "leafy and low-branched" rather than "dense-canopy": Malus habit ranges from open-vase to dense, so "dense" overstates it; the shade beneath a 6 m x 6 m low-branched crabapple is enough to carry the point on its own. (b) For dill, lead with the light misdirection (a full-sun annual instructed under a canopy), not "resents root competition" - root competition is on the engine's documented not-modelled list, so that half of the argument is out of scope and weakens an otherwise solid finding. Also add the scope evidence: the clause emits 19 times across the corpus, 13 with a full-sun companion (juniper -> butterfly-weed, saucer-magnolia -> baptisia, linden -> butterfly-bush, weeping-willow -> perennial-sunflower / joe-pye-weed / canna, blue-spruce -> ninebark), which makes the layeringClause fix a systemic one rather than a two-pair patch. Harm per instance is a mildewed, barely-flowering annual bed that the beginner reads as her own failure: real, but not plant death or ecological damage, so medium (not high) is correct.

title: Cuphea (scarlet Cigar Plant) is described as pink in its reasons: 'echoes the pink', 'its white flowers settle the pink'
subject: cuphea
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months; its white flowers settle the pink.
why: Cuphea ignea is a scarlet, tubular hummingbird flower - the library itself names it 'Cigar Plant' and tags it 'red' (plantLibrary.ts:3288) - but its bloom hex #e0533c lands at H 8 deg, S 0.73, L 0.557, and `colorName()` (src/browse/plantFacets.ts:88) reads `h < 16 -> l > 0.55 ? 'pink' : 'red'`. A saturated vermilion clears the pink threshold by 0.007 of lightness, so every colour clause on this plant names a colour it does not have. The user reads 'its white flowers settle the pink' and 'echoes the pink' (cuphea -> crabapple) about a red-flowered plant sitting in front of them, which is the kind of visibly false detail that makes a beginner distrust the whole card. This is a threshold bug, not a hue bug: the pink/red split should be gated on saturation as well as lightness.
fix: In `colorName()`, require low-to-moderate saturation for the pink bucket in the red arc, e.g. `if (h < 16 || h >= 330) return (l > 0.55 && s < 0.6) ? 'pink' : 'red';` - that keeps true pinks (crabapple #e87da0, S 0.66 but L 0.70) and stops calling saturated vermilions pink.
scope: cuphea -> serviceberry and cuphea -> crabapple here; any bloom hex in the red arc with L just over 0.55
notes: Finding stands as written, BUT replace the proposed fix. `(l > 0.55 && s < 0.6) ? "pink" : "red"` applies the saturation gate to the whole h<16||h>=330 branch, and most real pinks in the library are highly saturated. Crabapple #e87da0 is actually S 0.699 (not 0.66 as the finding says), i.e. ABOVE the proposed 0.6 threshold, so the fix turns red the one example it claims to protect. Applied to the library it flips roughly two-thirds of the 301 currently-pink plants in that arc to "red", including Peony 'Sarah Bernhardt' (#ef9ec0, S 0.72), Tulip 'Pink Impression' (#ec6f94, S 0.77), Phlox 'Bright Eyes' (S 0.71), Astilbe 'Sprite', Aster 'Wood's Pink'. Correct minimal fix: raise the lightness bar only in the orange-red sub-arc and leave the magenta side untouched - `if (h < 16) return l > 0.65 ? "pink" : "red"; if (h >= 330) return l > 0.55 ? "pink" : "red";` This makes cuphea (L 0.557), Dahlia 'Bishop's Children' (#e0503a, L 0.55) and Asiatic Lily (#e8643c, L 0.57) red, while pale corals (Peony 'Pink Hawaiian Coral' L 0.74, Daylily 'Strawberry Candy' L 0.74) and every magenta-side pink keep their name. Also worth noting: the same mis-bucketing gives cuphea a "pink" browse facet via plantColors() alongside its "red" tag.

Finding survives as written, but the PROPOSED FIX is wrong and must be corrected before it ships. Crabapple #e87da0 is S 0.699 (recomputed with the file's own HSL formula), not the S 0.66 the finding claims, so the suggested gate `l > 0.55 && s < 0.6` would flip crabapple - a genuine pink - to "red", breaking the exact case the finding cites as protected. A correct gate must be hue-aware: split the orange-red arc from the magenta arc, e.g. for `h < 16` only allow "pink" on a true tint (`l > 0.7`), and leave `h >= 330` on the existing `l > 0.55` rule. Calibration: this is a systematic threshold bug, not a one-off - 13 other single-span library plants tagged "red" also read as "pink" (knockout-rose #e23b5e, marguerite-daisy-red #d8424e, dahlia-bishops-children #e0503a, cherokee-chief #d6486a, verbena-annual-coral #f0705a, among others). Severity stays medium: horticulturally harmless, but a visibly false statement in advice a beginner reads with the red plant in front of them.

Confirmed: cuphea (#e0533c, tagged "red") is classed "pink" by colorName() because L=0.557 clears the 0.55 threshold; the reasons for cuphea -> serviceberry ("its white flowers settle the pink") and cuphea -> flowering-crabapple ("echoes the pink") therefore name a colour the plant does not have, and the crabapple clause asserts a harmony (scarlet echoing pink) that is actually a clash. Not unique to cuphea: the same threshold mislabels other red-tagged subjects, e.g. dahlia-bishops-children (#e0503a), lupine-my-castle (#cc5a6e), daylily-strawberry-candy (#ef8a8e). Do NOT ship the proposed fix as written: crabapple #e87da0 is S 0.699 / L 0.70, so an `s < 0.6` gate on the pink bucket would turn true pinks (crabapple, peony, most tulips) into "red". A safer correction is to narrow the low-hue arm (e.g. only h >= 330 may become pink; 0-16 deg stays red regardless of lightness), validated against the library's bloom hexes.

title: Creeping Sedum -> Monkshood: a moisture-loving woodland-edge perennial sold as the canopy over a dry-rockery succulent, and it is the most poisonous plant in the library
subject: creeping-sedum
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; stands well above at 1.2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both offer pollinator forage.
why: Creeping sedum is a succulent whose entire cultural requirement is lean, sharply drained, hot, dry soil - it rots in anything richer or wetter. Aconitum is the opposite plant: cool, humus-rich, reliably moist soil, and it sulks and scorches in the dry, baking full sun of a sedum planting, which is why it is always listed for part shade and moist borders. The one-rung sun ladder (full-sun vs part-sun) and one-rung water ladder (low vs moderate) hide a pairing that fails at both ends: on one watering schedule, either the aconitum wilts or the sedum rots. Separately, this is the third-ranked companion (score 71.1) for a beginner-facing groundcover and Aconitum carries the highest toxicity of anything in the catalog - aconitine is absorbed through the skin from cut stems and roots - and the card says nothing about it while telling the user to plant a mat right underneath, which is where you kneel and weed.
fix: Tighten the cultural filter for succulents: a plant with a 'succulent' tag should not accept a companion whose moisture need is above 'low'. And add a `toxic` flag surfaced as a one-line caution on the card for Aconitum, Colchicum and their kin.
scope: creeping-sedum -> monkshood in this chunk
notes: Rewrite as a SOIL-MOISTURE-REGIME finding, not a poison finding. Keep: creeping sedum (full-sun, low water, sharply drained lean gritty soil, rots in rich/wet ground) is offered Aconitum (moderate water, but per the app's own deepIntel "rich, humus-rich, consistently moist" soil that "should not be allowed to dry out") as its #3 companion (score 71.1), with a reason that tells a beginner to plant the sedum mat directly beneath it as ground cover. One watering/soil regime cannot serve both: the bed that keeps aconitum from wilting is the bed that rots the sedum. The one-rung water ladder plus the mild x0.92 water-differs penalty is not enough to catch it. Drop the "scorches in full sun" line (Aconitum takes full sun in cool climates; the library gives it zones 3-7). Drop the toxicity/no-caution paragraph and the toxic-flag fix: monkshood and colchicum already carry curated `safety.level: "serious"` blocks that PlantDetail.tsx renders directly above the companions section. The surviving fix is the first half only: a plant tagged `succulent` (or with the deepIntel "lean, sharply drained" soil profile) should not accept a companion whose moisture need is above `low`, i.e. make the water ladder a hard filter, not a x0.92 nudge, when one side is a succulent. An optional secondary suggestion: show the companion's safety level on the companion chip, since the recommendation is read before the destination card.

Keep the pairing finding: creeping-sedum -> monkshood is real (rank 3 of 8, score 71.1, water-differs penalty applied), and the reason actively asserts compatibility ("Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water") for a succulent that needs lean, baking, sharply drained ground under a plant the app's own curated facts call a cool-climate, moisture-and-shade plant that "struggles in hot summers". On one watering regime the sedum rots or the aconitum fails. Best evidence: the engine scores only the coarse library row and never sees the curated culture notes that contradict the pair. Two corrections to make before publishing. First, drop the "add a toxic flag" fix and the "the card says nothing about it" framing: src/garden/intel/curated/monkshood.json already has safety.level "serious" and src/browse/PlantDetail.tsx:353-358 already renders a Safety/Serious bar. The accurate ask is that src/browse/PlantCompanions.tsx surface the companion's existing safety.level (and/or gate the inline "Add to garden") because the companion mini-card shows name, size, bloom strip and reason only, and lets a beginner add monkshood straight to a bed without ever seeing the warning that already exists. Second, soften "the most poisonous plant in the library" to "one of the most poisonous garden plants" (the repo's own sourced wording); the library also contains Convallaria majalis.

Retitle to the defensible core: "Creeping Sedum -> Monkshood: the reason sells drought-intolerant Aconitum as taking 'low water', and tells the user to underplant it with a succulent mat." Drop the toxicity/aconitine argument entirely (off-scope for a pairing audit; make it a separate catalog-wide toxic-flag item if wanted). Evidence to keep: monkshood (plantLibrary.ts:2987, sun part-sun / water moderate / zones 3-7 / h 1.2) is rank 3 of 8 on creeping-sedum (plantLibrary.ts:3011, full-sun / low / h 0.1, tags succulent + drought tolerant) at score 71.1; compatibility.ts has no succulent or drought-tolerant handling and only penalises a one-rung water gap x0.92; creeping-sedum is monkshood's TOP-scoring subject catalog-wide, and monkshood is also offered to blue-oat-grass and blue-fescue (both full-sun/low/drought-tolerant) while its other six subjects are all correct moist part-shade plants. Preferred fix is the data correction, not a new engine rule: Aconitum's water should be "high" (medium-to-wet, intolerant of drought), which puts it two rungs from "low" and blocks all three dry-site pairings through the existing hard filter.

title: Pampas Grass (Cortaderia selloana) is recommended to Dianthus, Daylily and Dusty Miller and praised for the seed plumes that make it invasive
subject: dianthus
claim: Shares full sun and low water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a Cal-IPC 'High' rated invasive, a California noxious weed, a quarantine/noxious species in Oregon and Hawaii, and weedy in Texas coastal areas: a single female plume carries on the order of 100,000 wind-borne seeds for miles into open ground. The engine's only cited benefit ('its seed heads stand through winter') is literally the dispersal organ that drives the invasion, and it is offered with no caution. The root cause is in the data: taxonomy.ts:424 has `cortaderia: {}` ("confidence: high - no applicable traits"), so the engine scores a Cal-IPC High invasive as a neutral drought-tolerant grass with no spreader flag and no spreader caution clause. The app already knows better elsewhere - src/garden/intel/deepIntel/pampas-grass.json cites the Cal-IPC profile - so the compatibility engine is contradicting the plant's own intel file. Two secondary problems in the same sentence: pampas leaves are serrated and can slice a hand, which makes it a poor neighbour to lean over a 0.3 m front-of-border Dianthus, and 'a canopy to plant beneath' is false for a dense 1.5 m-wide grass clump that nothing grows under.
fix: Remove pampas-grass from the archetype/companion pool (the same way retired plants are excluded), or at minimum give `cortaderia` an invasive/spreader trait in taxonomy.ts so it takes the spreader penalty and always emits a caution clause, and suppress the 'seed heads stand through winter' clause for it.
scope: 3 of the 10 subjects in this chunk (dianthus, daylily, dusty-miller); it will surface on every full-sun / low-water archetype list in zones 7-11
notes:
Correct one factual detail before publishing: "a California noxious weed" is imprecise. Cal-IPC "High" is a nonprofit inventory rating, not a CDFA legal noxious-weed listing (CDFA does not list C. selloana). Reword to: "Cal-IPC High-rated invasive; quarantine/noxious-listed in Oregon and Hawaii; naturalized on the Texas coast and through the Southeast." Everything else stands.

Two strengtheners worth adding: (1) the repo contradicts itself - crowding.ts:39 and crowding.test.ts:42 assert pampas is dense to the ground and NOT a canopy former, while compatibility.ts:1066 emits "stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath" for it; (2) scale from the audit's own invasive-recount.json: 191 companion lists, 191 praised, 0 cautioned. (3) Precision on the pairs: the canopy clause fires for dianthus and dusty-miller; daylily gets "taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it" (plus a water-differs penalty), so the headline should not attribute the canopy wording to daylily.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Scope is larger than the three named pairs: data/invasive-recount.json records pampas-grass on 191 companion lists, all 191 praised, 0 cautioned. Cite that, and note maiden-grass (Miscanthus sinensis, 68 lists, 68 praised, 0 cautioned) is the same failure mode. (2) The proposed fix's mechanism is off. `aggressiveSpreader` in this engine means vegetative smothering: its caution text is "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging" (compatibility.ts:1147) and it also feeds the `spreader-smothers` hard filter, so flagging cortaderia with it would emit the wrong warning (pampas is a clump-former, not a runner) and silently blocklist pairs. Also, the "seed heads stand through winter" clause comes from `seedheads` (granted to every ornamental grass by category in winterStructureScore, compatibility.ts:756), not from `cortaderia: {}`. The right levers are: remove pampas-grass (and maiden-grass) from the archetype/companion pool, or add a genuine invasiveness trait with its own caution clause and suppress the seed-head praise for it.

Finding stands at HIGH. Three corrections to carry into the report:

1. SCOPE IS UNDERSOLD BY THE TITLE. The three named subjects are a sample, not the extent. Per invasive-recount.json, pampas-grass is recommended on 191 companion lists library-wide with 0 cautions and 191 praises. Frame it as "pampas-grass is praised, never cautioned, on 191 companion lists (Dianthus, Daylily and Dusty Miller among them)".

2. ONE QUOTED DETAIL IS MISATTRIBUTED. The "a canopy to plant beneath" clause does NOT appear in the Daylily reason, which instead ends "taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it". The canopy clause fires for Dianthus and Dusty Miller. It is still a real and widespread defect (13 of the 30 sampled pampas pairings tell the user to plant beneath a 1.5 m-wide dense clump), and it is the more alarming one given the app's own intel calls the blades a cutting hazard near paths and play areas. Just attribute it correctly.

3. THE PROPOSED FIX NEEDS ONE MORE STEP THAN "ADD A SPREADER FLAG", AND THE FINDING IS RIGHT TO SAY SO. The winter-seedhead praise is not gated on taxonomy at all: compatibility.ts:433 is `seedheads: category === "Grass" || WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus)`, so EVERY Grass is auto-praised for seed heads. Flagging `cortaderia` as a spreader would add a penalty and a caution clause but would NOT suppress the praise clause. Both changes are required, exactly as the finding's proposed fix states.

WORTH ADDING (strengthens the case against a locality-caveat-only fix): deepIntel's own localityCaveat says pampas is "low-risk to plant in cold-winter interior regions where it barely seeds". But the library files pampas-grass at zones 7-11, and the engine's zone-intersection filter therefore only ever recommends it into zones 7-11: precisely the coastal California, Gulf, Southeast and Hawaii range where it escapes. The zone filter confines the recommendation to the invasive range rather than away from it, so "we already have a locality caveat" is not a defence. Removal from the archetype pool, or a hard unconditional caution, is the right call.

title: Trumpet Vine is offered as a companion for Eastern White Pine and Douglas Fir with no spreader caution, because the engine treats a conifer as too 'robust' to warn about
subject: eastern-white-pine
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; both are native plants.
why: Campsis radicans climbs by aerial rootlets to 8-12 m (the library itself gives it h=8) and root-suckers many metres from the crown; extension services describe it as extremely difficult to eradicate. Planted beside an 18 m Pinus strobus or Pseudotsuga it will do exactly what it is built to do: go up the trunk into the canopy and colonise the root zone. The engine DOES flag it (taxonomy.ts:385 `campsis: { ..., spreader: true }`), but `spreaderNeedsWatching()` only fires when the neighbour is 'soft' - a Conifer is in ROBUST_CATEGORIES, so the x0.8 penalty AND the 'a vigorous spreader, so give it room' caution clause are both suppressed in precisely the situation where the vine is most damaging. The user is left with a sentence that states no benefit at all except a native flag. That native claim is itself misleading: the `native` flag is region-blind, and Campsis radicans (southeastern US) plus Pseudotsuga menziesii (Pacific Northwest / Rockies) are natives of opposite ends of the continent, so 'both are native plants' reads to a beginner as an endorsed native planting that no regional native-plant guide would make. Same sentence, same problem, for douglas-fir -> trumpet-vine and douglas-fir/eastern-white-pine -> queen-of-the-prairie (Filipendula rubra, an eastern wet-prairie native).
fix: Never suppress the spreader caution when the neighbour is a Tree or Conifer - a woody host is what a climbing spreader exploits. Simplest targeted change: in `spreaderNeedsWatching`, treat a `Climber` spreader beside a Tree/Conifer as a hard blocker (or at least keep the caution clause), and stop emitting 'both are native plants' when the two natives have no shared native range.
scope: both conifer subjects in this chunk (douglas-fir, eastern-white-pine); the caution suppression affects every spreader paired with a Tree/Conifer/Shrub/Grass
notes: Scope the native-range criticism to Douglas fir only: Campsis radicans and Pinus strobus genuinely overlap natively in the eastern US, so "both are native plants" is fair for eastern-white-pine; it is misleading only for douglas-fir -> trumpet-vine and douglas-fir -> queen-of-the-prairie (Pseudotsuga is PNW/Rockies, both companions are eastern natives). The core defect is unchanged and worth stating plainly: no UI surface anywhere shows a spreader flag (grep for "spreader" across src/**/*.tsx is empty), so the suppressed caution clause is the user's only possible warning, and the two conifers get a sentence with no benefit and no warning for the most aggressive vine in the catalog.

Survives, with two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The native-range critique is sound for douglas-fir -> trumpet-vine (Pseudotsuga menziesii is a PNW/Rockies native, Campsis radicans a SE US native) but NOT for the headline pair: Pinus strobus and Campsis radicans ranges genuinely overlap in the eastern US/Appalachians, so "both are native plants" is defensible there. The eastern-white-pine fault is purely the suppressed spreader caution, and the finding should lead with that rather than the native flag. (2) eastern-white-pine -> queen-of-the-prairie is NOT a native-range mismatch: Filipendula rubra is an eastern/midwestern US native, same continent-half as white pine. Its only shared defect is that `filipendula` is also spreader: true (taxonomy.ts:269) and so also loses its caution beside a robust neighbour - but a rhizomatous prairie perennial beside a mature pine is a far milder problem than a canopy-climbing vine, so "same problem" overstates it; drop or downgrade that example. Strengthening evidence worth adding: the caution clause for Campsis fires in ZERO of the 15 lists it appears in, because the other soft() branch (neighbour is itself a spreader) knocks out the remaining herbaceous subjects (bee-balm, lemon-balm, maiden-grass). The proposed fix stands: do not let a robust/woody neighbour suppress the caution for a Climber spreader.

Finding is real and UNDERSTATED, but the headline pair should be re-anchored. (1) eastern-white-pine -> trumpet-vine (score 46.9, rank 6 of 8) is the WEAKEST instance of the class: an 18 m Pinus strobus is the one host that genuinely can shrug off a Campsis, which is the strongest ground the engine's "robust" defence has. Leading with it invites exactly the rebuttal that the tree is fine. (2) The indefensible instances are the shrubs: `viburnum (2 m) -> trumpet-vine (8 m)` score 69.5 and `lilac (3 m) -> trumpet-vine` score 62.9, whose reason reads "...climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint." That is not merely a missing caution but a FALSE BENEFIT: root-suckering many metres from the crown is precisely Campsis radicans' footprint and is the entire problem. The engine praises the plant for the very trait that makes it a menace, on a shrub it will bury, at a HIGHER score than the pine pairing. Contrast `ajuga -> goatsbeard`, a mild ground cover, which DOES get "this one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time" - the engine owns the sentence and withholds it from the plant that most needs it. (3) The class is broader than climbers: ajuga, pachysandra, lily-of-valley, vinca and snow-in-summer beside shrubs suppress the same caution (100 pairs total). (4) The proposed fix is the wrong shape: hard-blocking Climber-beside-Tree/Conifer is too narrow (misses the perennial spreaders beside shrubs, which are the bulk) and wrong-targeted. Correct fix: stop spreaderNeedsWatching() consulting ROBUST_CATEGORIES entirely - "will the neighbour survive?" (isDelicate / isVulnerableTo, where the robust test is right) is a different question from "must the gardener be warned this thing runs?" Keep the robust test in the hard-blocker path, drop it from the caution/penalty path. Separately gate the "height without the footprint" template behind !p.spreader. (5) The region-blind "both are native plants" claim (Campsis radicans, southeastern US; Pseudotsuga menziesii, Pacific Northwest) is real but secondary - a supporting sentence, not the headline.

title: 18 m conifers recommend full-sun roses, lilacs, hollyhocks and sweet peas as 'an understory layer' - dry shade under a dense evergreen kills all four
subject: eastern-white-pine
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; shorter at 3 m, an understory layer.
why: The layering clause (compatibility.ts:1072-1075) says any Tree/Conifer subject 'has an understory', so a full-sun plant gets told to go under an 18 m Pinus strobus. Under a mature white pine or Douglas fir the ground is dry, deeply shaded, matted with shallow feeder roots and covered in acidic needle duff: a climbing rose (needs 6+ hours of direct sun) will not flower and will mildew; a lilac (full sun, and it prefers a neutral-to-slightly-alkaline pH) is a textbook failure under a pine. The engine says 'shares full sun' in the same sentence in which it puts the plant in shade - the reason contradicts itself. The bottom of the same list is worse: douglas-fir -> hollyhock and douglas-fir -> sweet-pea (both score 45.9) read in full as 'Shares full sun and moderate water.' - no benefit stated at all, just two coarse fields matching. And daylily -> arborvitae says 'stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath' about a columnar Thuja that is branched to the ground with a 1.5 m spread: there is no beneath.
fix: For a Tree/Conifer subject over roughly 6 m, only offer companions whose light rung is part-shade or shadier (dry-shade tolerant), and never phrase a full-sun companion as an 'understory layer' - phrase it as 'nearby, clear of its shade'. Also drop a pairing whose reason reduces to the conditions clause alone (no factor scored) rather than shipping 'Shares full sun and moderate water.' as advice.
scope: douglas-fir and eastern-white-pine lists (8 of 16 pairings are sun-lovers placed 'beneath'); the same clause also mis-fires on daylily -> arborvitae and on grass clumps
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The title is wrong about hollyhock and sweet pea: they never receive the "understory layer" phrasing. Their layering score is 0.47, just below CLAUSE_FLOOR.layering = 0.5, so they get a bare "Shares full sun and moderate water." - a reason with no benefit at all. That is a real but SEPARATE defect (15 such conditions-only pairs exist catalog-wide, e.g. blue-spruce -> hollyhock, italian-cypress -> russian-sage, juniper -> california-poppy). Retitle around the plants that actually get the clause: roses, lilacs and clematis. (2) Drop or soften the pH leg: "pine needles acidify the soil" is a debunked garden myth (the duff layer is acidic, mineral soil pH barely moves), and neither Pinus strobus nor Pseudotsuga menziesii is textbook shallow-rooted. The case stands on shade + root competition + dry soil alone; leaving the myth in gives a horticulturist an easy way to dismiss the whole finding. Also: the defect is broader than the title suggests - 7 subjects over 10 m emit a full-sun understory/carpet clause: blue-spruce (ninebark, clematis, lilac, climbing-rose), douglas-fir, eastern-white-pine, italian-cypress (butterfly-bush, clematis, climbing-rose, jasmine), linden, tulip-tree, weeping-willow.

Title needs one correction before it ships: hollyhock and sweet pea are NOT phrased as "an understory layer". Their 10:1 height ratio to the 18 m conifer produces no layering clause at all, so their reason is only the bare conditions sentence "Shares full sun and moderate water." (the body of the finding already states this correctly; the title conflates the two failure modes). Suggested title: "18 m conifers recommend full-sun roses and lilacs as 'an understory layer', and full-sun hollyhocks/sweet peas with no stated benefit at all". Note also that sweet-pea appears under douglas-fir (Annual, so the zone filter is skipped) while eastern-white-pine's bare-conditions pair is hollyhock. The daylily -> arborvitae sub-point verifies too: Thuja occidentalis is stored at h 6 / spread 1.5 and tagged columnar|screen|hedge, so "a canopy to plant beneath" is false about it.

Two corrections must be carried into the report.

1. THE TITLE IS WRONG IN DETAIL. Hollyhock and sweet-pea get NO layering clause - their reasons are bare conditions clauses ("Shares full sun and moderate water.", nothing else). Folding them into an "understory layer" headline overstates. These are TWO distinct defects and should be reported as such:
   (a) full-sun woody plants and climbers phrased as an "understory layer"/"carpet beneath" under 12-20 m dense conifers (eastern-white-pine -> climbing-rose / lilac / clematis; douglas-fir -> lilac / clematis; blue-spruce -> climbing-rose / lilac; italian-cypress -> climbing-rose / jasmine / butterfly-bush). This is the harmful one: a climbing rose or lilac under a mature Pinus strobus gets deep shade, dry acidic needle duff and a dense surface root mat - it will not flower and will mildew. The reason contradicts itself in one sentence ("Shares full sun ... an understory layer").
   (b) 12 reasons that reduce to the conditions clause alone and state no benefit whatsoever (douglas-fir -> hollyhock, -> sweet-pea; eastern-white-pine -> hollyhock; blue-spruce -> phlox / obedient-plant / hollyhock; italian-cypress -> gaura / mullein / russian-sage / cosmos; juniper -> california-poppy; autumn-fern -> dogwood-tree). Lower severity than (a), but indefensible on a premium feature.

2. DROP the daylily -> arborvitae sub-claim. Daylilies ringing the base of an arborvitae are a ubiquitous, successful foundation planting and daylilies take part shade; "a canopy to plant beneath" is loose phrasing, but no gardener is harmed by it. It fails the harm test and weakens the finding by association. (Note it is also the h=6 boundary case, exactly at STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M.)

The proposed fix is sound but should be scoped to the light rung, not the height alone: for a Tree/Conifer subject above ~6 m, only phrase a companion as understory/beneath when its light rung is part-shade or shadier (witch-hazel under white pine, correctly clause'd today, is genuinely good advice and must survive the fix); a full-sun companion should be phrased as "nearby, clear of its shade". Separately, suppress any pairing whose reason carries no scored factor.

title: Echeveria is told to sit 'beneath' Tropical Hibiscus, Cleome and Cosmos - a succulent under a thirsty 1.5 m canopy on one watering schedule will rot
subject: echeveria
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; stands well above at 1.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath; echoes the pink.
why: Echeveria is a 0.1 m rosette succulent that needs sharp drainage, unobstructed sun, and soil that dries out completely between waterings - crown and root rot from staying wet is the single most common way beginners kill it. Hibiscus rosa-sinensis is a heavy drinker that wants consistently moist, rich soil and wilts if it dries; Cleome (0.5 m wide, 1.2 m tall) is the same. Watering the hibiscus adequately waters the echeveria to death, and a 1.2-1.5 m neighbour directly overhead removes the full sun and the air movement the rosette depends on, while water shed off the foliage sits in the rosette's crown. The engine's whole response to this is the water-differs multiplier (x0.92), which is far too weak: for a succulent, 'one rung apart' is not a nuance, it is the failure mode. Cleome scores 54.2 and Cosmos 60.5 with the identical 'a canopy to plant beneath' instruction.
fix: Treat succulents (the `succulent` tag, or water='low' + succulent category) as a hard water filter - same water rung only, no straddling - and never emit 'a canopy to plant beneath' when the SHORTER plant is a succulent or is tagged full-sun; it should read 'plant it in front, in the open, where it stays dry'.
scope: 3 of echeveria's 8 companions (tropical-hibiscus, cleome, cosmos); applies to every succulent archetype in the library
notes: Keep the finding, but narrow it to echeveria <-> tropical-hibiscus and add the reciprocal. Corrected framing: "Echeveria and Tropical Hibiscus recommend each other in both directions, each telling the beginner to plant the succulent under the shrub (echeveria's card: 'stands well above at 1.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath'; hibiscus's card, #2 companion at score 52: 'low at 0.1 m, to carpet the ground beneath'). Watering H. rosa-sinensis to its needs - evenly moist, frequent irrigation - rots a 0.1 m Echeveria rosette, and the 1 m-wide evergreen canopy takes away the unobstructed sun and airflow it depends on while shedding drip into its crown. The engine's only response is the x0.92 water-differs multiplier; for a succulent, one water rung is not a nuance, it is the failure mode." Drop Cosmos from the finding entirely (water=low, no penalty, airy habit - no conflict), and demote Cleome to a passing mention at most: Cleome hassleriana is heat- and drought-tolerant once established, not a heavy drinker. Do not claim the hibiscus water value is miscoded; "moderate" is a defensible listing. The residual, separate observation (worth at most a low-severity note, not this finding) is that the CANOPY_RATIO clause fires for anything over 6:1 above tier 2, so echeveria is told to sit beneath cosmos, rosemary, switchgrass and perennial sunflower as well - "a canopy to plant beneath" is the wrong sentence for a 1 m airy annual regardless of water.

Verified verbatim: echeveria -> tropical-hibiscus, score 52, reason "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; stands well above at 1.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath; echoes the pink." (chunks/chunk-09.json), penalty water-differs. Library values confirmed at plantLibrary.ts:3258 (echeveria, water "low", tags include "succulent") and :3246 (tropical-hibiscus, water "moderate", h 1.5, w 1.0). Two refinements to carry into the report: (1) Drop Cosmos from the water/rot half of the claim - Cosmos is water "low" (plantLibrary.ts:2344), the same rung as echeveria, so it is only guilty of the "a canopy to plant beneath" wording, not of drowning the succulent. The thirsty-canopy charge is correctly aimed at Tropical Hibiscus (moderate) and Cleome (moderate). (2) Worth naming that the pair clears the scale filter by exactly zero margin: heightRatio is 1.5/0.1 = 15.0 against MAX_HEIGHT_RATIO = 15 (the test is strictly greater-than), and it simultaneously earns FULL layering credit (1.0, tier 0 to tier 2), so the engine is not merely tolerating this pair, it is rewarding the height gap that causes the harm.
