Also note tropical hibiscus's own water value is arguably understated: H. rosa-sinensis wants consistently moist soil and is closer to "high", which would have hard-failed the pair at two rungs - so the library data compounds the engine gap.

Retitle to name only the moderate-water partners (Tropical Hibiscus, Cleome, Perennial Sunflower); Cosmos is filed LOW water and drought-tolerant, so the rot argument does not apply to it - cite Cosmos (score 60.5, the top companion) only for the shading/"plant beneath" instruction. Cleome is likewise moderate and reasonably drought-tolerant once established, so "a heavy drinker, the same as hibiscus" is loose; the clean water conflict is hibiscus (consistently-moist heavy feeder) and perennial sunflower.

Add the real mechanical root cause, which is stronger than the finding's water-multiplier argument: in compatibility.ts the SCORE and the SENTENCE disagree. layeringScore() puts echeveria (tier 0) against hibiscus (tier 2) on the border-gradation branch (Math.abs(ta-tb)/2 = 1.0), i.e. it credits the textbook "short plant at the FRONT" relation. layeringClause() then uses a different test (r > CANOPY_RATIO 6 && tierOf(taller) >= 2; ratio here is 15:1) and emits "a canopy to plant beneath". The engine never modelled a canopy for this pair; the template invented one. The clause should be gated on the same tier-3 (>3 m) canopy branch the scorer uses, and never emitted when the shorter plant is full-sun or succulent-tagged.

Scope: this is systemic, which raises rather than lowers its importance. 241 generated pairs carry "a canopy to plant beneath"; 221 of them have a "canopy" under 3 m; 33 have a succulent-tagged subject, including hens-and-chicks -> spirea / lily-asiatic / amaranth (all moderate water), ice-plant -> spirea, creeping-sedum -> monkshood, and portulaca -> boxwood, each with the identical instruction.

Exposure caveat worth stating once in the report, not per-finding: the companion panel is gated off today (canUseCompatibility false, 3-per-account reveal trial), so today's blast radius is the reveal trial, not every user. It does not reduce the correctness of the finding.

title: Grass plumes that are beige are described as 'orange', selling a colour contrast the plant does not have (Delphinium -> Fountain Grass)
subject: delphinium
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late summer as this one finishes; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; orange against blue, for contrast.
why: The colour clause names a hue from the bloom hex without regard to saturation or lightness, so desaturated tans come out as saturated hue names. Pennisetum alopecuroides is stored as #d8c0a0 - a pale beige - and its real inflorescence is a buff to purplish-tan bottlebrush; nothing about it reads orange, and 'orange against blue, for contrast' promises a complementary punch a beginner will not get. Same clause, same problem, on delphinium -> little-bluestem (#d8a87a) and (in the Dahlia list) dahlia -> ranunculus, where a plant the card tags 'orange' (#e0533c) is described to the user as 'pink sits close to red on the wheel', and the pink Dahlia is repeatedly called 'the red'. The reason and the plant's own colour tag disagree in front of the user.
fix: Gate the colour clause on chroma: if either bloom colour is low-saturation or near-neutral (beige/cream/tan plumes, most grasses), skip the colour factor rather than naming a hue, and prefer the plant's own colour tag over a hex bucket when the two disagree.
scope: every grass with a tan/buff plume hex (fountain-grass, switchgrass #dcae9e, little-bluestem #d8a87a) and any plant whose hex bucket disagrees with its colour tag (dahlia, ranunculus)
notes: Drop or soften the little-bluestem leg. Schizachyrium scoparium (#d8a87a) really does turn coppery-orange in autumn - that russet is the reason it is planted - so "orange against blue, for contrast" is advice a horticulturist would sign for that pair. It is not an instance of "beige called orange" and weakens the finding if left in. The finding stands on (a) delphinium -> fountain-grass, where a buff Pennisetum alopecuroides plume is sold as an orange complement to blue, and (b) the dahlia list, where the hue bucket and the plant's own colour tag openly disagree in front of the user (ranunculus, tagged and chipped "orange", is called "pink"; dahlia, tagged and chipped "pink", is called "the red" three times). Worth adding to the fix: the colour clause should also require actual bloom overlap, since here it promises a contrast between a June-July blue and an August-October plume.

Fix one overstatement before publishing: the finding says the clause names a hue "without regard to saturation or lightness". Neutrality gates DO exist - colorName returns white at l>=0.82 && s<0.25 and null at s<0.12, and colourScore's isNeutralBloom() skips at s<0.2 || l>0.85. They are just too permissive: #d8c0a0 sits at s=0.42, l=0.74, sails through, and hue 34deg lands in the orange bucket. So the fix is RAISING the neutral threshold (and preferring the plant's own colour tag when tag and hex bucket conflict), not adding a gate that does not exist. Also, for fountain grass the card facet is derived from the same hex, so card and reason both say "orange" - both wrong, but they agree; the visible tag-vs-reason contradiction is the ranunculus case (tag "orange", reason "pink") and the pink Dahlia called "the red". Worth adding: little bluestem's orange is autumn FOLIAGE (foliage: #8a9a9f in the library, a field the colour factor never reads); its actual seed heads are silvery-white fluff, so "orange" is doubly wrong there. Scope is 15 grass-companion reasons, not just the two named.

SURVIVES as written; medium is correct (false user-facing copy in the paid feature, not a plant-kills-plant or invasive-recommendation issue). Two amendments for the report:

1. BLAST RADIUS IS BIGGER THAN STATED. 15 grass pairs carry an actively false hue clause, not 2. Beyond delphinium -> fountain-grass / little-bluestem: larkspur, lupine, blue-fescue -> fountain-grass; browallia, torenia, meadow-rue -> northern-sea-oats; scaevola, verbena-annual -> feather-reed-grass; wisteria -> blue-oat-grass; geranium-annual -> switchgrass ("pink sits close to red" - so tan plumes also mis-bucket as PINK, not only orange); and portulaca -> little-bluestem ("echoes the orange", telling the user a silver-white seed head echoes an orange bloom). The single clearest illustration for the report: blue-fescue -> fountain-grass reads "orange sits close to yellow on the wheel" - two beige grasses sold to a beginner as an orange-and-yellow harmony.

2. THE PROPOSED FIX'S SECOND HALF WOULD INTRODUCE A NEW BUG. "Prefer the plant's own colour tag over a hex bucket when the two disagree" is unsafe, because the tag is frequently a FOLIAGE colour, not a bloom colour: little bluestem is tagged "blue" (its stems), fountain grass "green", blue-fescue "blue". Preferring the tag would emit "echoes the blue" for delphinium -> little-bluestem, which is equally false. Recommend the chroma gate ALONE (skip the colour factor when either bloom is low-chroma / near-neutral - raise isNeutralBloom's threshold so buff plumes at s~0.4-0.55, l~0.65-0.75 route to the neutral path the way pampas and maiden grass already do). Optionally also suppress the "for contrast" clause when the two plants' bloom windows do not overlap, since delphinium (Jun-Jul) and every one of these grasses (Aug-Oct) never share a moment in which a contrast could be seen.

title: Elephant Ear (a bog plant) is paired with Calibrachoa, Scaevola, Sweet Alyssum and Gardenia, whose one documented failure mode is exactly its watering regime
subject: elephant-ear
claim: "Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; lower at 0.2 m, for the front of the border." (score 35.8; same shape for scaevola 35.8, sweet-alyssum 35.8, gardenia 34.7)
why: Colocasia is a marginal aquatic. The repo's own curated intel says so: elephant-ear.json - "most types grow happily in boggy soil, at pond and rain-garden margins, and in a few inches of standing water", water "High ... 2 to 3 inches per week or more", and the reviewer note adds "High is correct and if anything conservative". Now the top three companions: calibrachoa.json - "UMN advises against planting it in garden beds", "Overwatering and soggy roots are the main danger", "poor resistance to Phytophthora root rot and stem rot ... the main way to lose the plant is overwatering". scaevola.json - "it resents standing water", "Sharp drainage is the key requirement, since wet soil brings on root rot". gardenia.json - shallow-rooted, "poor drainage / soggy soil drives root problems and chlorosis", and it "dislikes root disturbance and competition" (Colocasia is a 1 m-wide tuberous clump). The coarse moderate-vs-high band reads these as one rung apart and applies only a x0.92 nudge, but on one watering schedule the beginner either keeps Colocasia wet (the annuals and the gardenia rot) or keeps them dry (Colocasia stalls and scorches). There is no schedule that serves both. Note also that the whole elephant-ear list scores 31-36: the engine has found no real companion for this plant and still prints eight.
fix: Treat a high-water plant that tolerates standing water as its own class (a wet/bog flag on the plant, or make water-differs a hard filter, not a x0.92 penalty, when one side is high and the other is a documented sharp-drainage plant). The deepIntel drainage/root-rot data already exists in the repo and the compatibility engine simply does not read it. At minimum, add a score floor so a subject with nothing above ~45 shows fewer than eight companions rather than filling the card.
scope: 4 of elephant-ear's 8 companions; the same root-rot-vs-bog conflict will recur for any high-water subject (canna, cardinal flower, Joe Pye) paired with container/Mediterranean annuals
refutations: Refuted. (1) Colocasia is not an obligate bog plant - the repo's own deepIntel soil claim says it wants "organically rich soil kept constantly moist ... TOLERATES occasionally wet to boggy conditions"; standing water is a tolerance, not a requirement (Proven Winners: "moist to wet soil, but can tolerate some drought stress", "great in landscapes and containers"). (2) The central claim "there is no schedule that serves both" is false: calibrachoa and scaevola are killed by poor DRAINAGE / waterlogged root zones, not by frequent watering. In rich, well-drained soil the frequent watering they need in heat is the same regime that satisfies Colocasia. The finding conflates water volume with drainage. (3) The exact trio it calls impossible is a published commercial recipe: Proven Winners' "Memories" (14 in pot, part sun to sun) = 1 Elephant Ears + 2 Superbells Calibrachoa + 2 Whirlwind Blue Scaevola; their 2025 grower guide pairs Colocasia 'Coffee Cups' with Supertunia petunias (same family, same rot profile as calibrachoa). Elephant-ear-over-annual-spillers is canonical trade practice, not a documented poor combination. (4) The reason text discloses the range ("moderate to high water") rather than falsely claiming identical needs, and asserts no benefit the pair lacks. (5) The calibrachoa-in-a-bed caveat (UMN) is systemic - calibrachoa is recommended to 7 subjects, 4 of them moderate-water - so it is not attributable to elephant ear. (6) The "engine found no real companion yet prints eight" sub-claim is a misread of the scoring: the lowest top-scores are ALL bloomless foliage subjects (elephant-ear 35.8, boston-fern 42.7, caladium 42.7, coleus/hosta/coral-bells 43.2; median top = 64.3) because bloom succession is 30 of the 100 points. Hosta at 43.2 obviously has real companions; low absolute score is a weighting artifact. Harm test: the worst realistic outcome is a calibrachoa sulking in heavy clay, which the app's own calibrachoa card already warns about ("Skip heavy garden beds and clay", "Overwatering is the main killer"). No gardener is hurt by this pairing.
notes: Narrow the finding to: elephant-ear -> scaevola (primary), with calibrachoa as a secondary case. Scaevola aemula needs sharp drainage and resents standing water (repo deepIntel), yet is recommended at the feet of a plant the repo itself files as happy in a few inches of standing water, with the reason "Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water" implying their needs align. Calibrachoa is a weaker case and is as much a habitat mismatch as a water one (UMN advises against bedding it out at all); note that frequent watering is fine for it - only soggy, poorly drained soil is not. DROP gardenia entirely: it is filed at water HIGH (same rung as elephant ear), gets the reason "Shares part sun and high water", carries no water-differs penalty, is a moisture-loving streambank shrub, and its real make-or-break is acidic pH. DROP sweet alyssum (moderate-water bedding annual, no documented conflict at ordinary bed moisture). DROP the "no real companion, still prints eight" claim: joe-pye-weed, autumn fern and Japanese forest grass on the same list are appropriate. The proposed fix (a wet/bog class, or making water-differs a hard filter when one side is a documented sharp-drainage plant) remains reasonable, but it must not fire on high-water/high-water pairs like gardenia.

CORRECTIONS TO APPLY BEFORE PUBLISHING.

1. DROP GARDENIA from the finding. It is not a water-band mismatch: gardenia is filed water: high, the same rung as elephant ear, so it carries no water-differs x0.92 penalty (its listed penalty is over-recommended). Its own deepIntel vsCatalog endorses "high". Both plants are part-sun, zone 8-11, and want rich, moist, organic soil; they are a standard Deep South combination. Including it weakens an otherwise airtight finding. Retitle to the three that hold: Calibrachoa, Scaevola, Sweet Alyssum.

2. The remaining three are fully verified and are the top three companions in the list (35.8, 35.8, 35.8 of a max 35.8), so this is not a tail-end artifact: the engine's BEST advice for this plant is its worst.

3. Strengthen with two facts I confirmed that the finding does not use:
   - Sweet Alyssum's reason is "low at 0.12 m, to carpet the ground beneath" - it literally directs a full-sun 12 cm annual to be carpeted UNDER a 1.2 m x 1 m leaf canopy, so that pair fails on light as well as water. Its own intel adds "waterlogged soil invites root rot" and "Lean, well-drained soil gives the best bloom".
   - Scope: elephant-ear is the single worst case in the catalog. Of the 7 high-water subjects that recommend any of these three sharp-drainage container annuals (canna, cardinal-flower, elephant-ear, gardenia, hardy-hibiscus, hydrangea, winterberry), only elephant-ear draws all three, and it draws them into its top three slots.

4. The "no score floor" mechanism is confirmed in code: compatibility.ts MAX_COMPANIONS = 8, MIN_COMPANIONS = 6, and pick() applies no minimum score, so a subject whose best candidate scores 35.8 still fills all eight slots. The proposed fix (a score floor, plus reading the existing deepIntel drainage/root-rot data) is well-founded.

Two factual corrections to carry forward if any part of this is reused. (a) The finding is wrong that all four pairs are hit by the coarse moderate-vs-high water band: gardenia is filed water: "high" in src/garden/plantLibrary.ts:2526, so elephant-ear -> gardenia carries NO water-differs penalty and its reason reads "Shares part sun and high water." (b) The one thing genuinely worth a separate look is that gardenia's own water value of "high" is questionable - the repo's deepIntel quotes NC State calling gardenia "moderately drought-tolerant once established" and warns "poor drainage / soggy soil drives root problems and chlorosis," which reads as moderate, not high. That is a library-data question about gardenia, not a bad-pairing finding about elephant ear.

title: Feather Reed Grass -> Wisteria: a genus-level entry that includes the invasive Asian wisterias, sold on the very trait that makes it dangerous
subject: feather-reed-grass
claim: "Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint; blue against orange, for contrast." (score 61.1)
why: The library's default 'wisteria' is filed with botanical name "Wisteria" (bare genus, plantLibrary.ts:2597) and its variant group carries Wisteria floribunda 'Alba' and 'Rosea' under it. The repo's own wisteria.json says: "Chinese wisteria (W. sinensis) and Japanese wisteria (W. floribunda) are aggressive invasives across much of the eastern US. They escape gardens, shade out and strangle native trees ... spread by both abundant seed and short rooting stems (stolons/runners) that sprout wherever nodes touch soil. Because cut vines resprout, eradication typically ne[eds repeated treatment]". The generated reason then praises it as "height without the footprint" - which is precisely, flatly false for a wisteria: it runs at the root, layers wherever a stem touches soil, needs a structural (not trellis) support, and needs hard pruning twice a year (wisteria.json). A beginner who reads "height without the footprint" and plants a wisteria behind a 1.5 m grass has been told the opposite of the truth. All parts are also toxic to people and pets, seeds and pods worst.
fix: Point the 'wisteria' default at the native Wisteria frutescens 'Amethyst Falls' (the repo's own intel already recommends exactly this substitution), and suppress the "height without the footprint" clause for Climber companions flagged as aggressive spreaders; a vigorous woody vine is never a footprint-free companion.
scope: Every list wisteria appears in as a companion (archetype, so any full-sun / moderate-water subject in zones 5-9)
notes: Survives, with three corrections to carry into the report. (a) The variant group is MIXED, not Asian-only: alongside W. floribunda 'Alba' and 'Rosea' it also holds W. macrostachya 'Blue Moon' and W. frutescens 'Amethyst Falls', both well-behaved. The defect is in the DEFAULT/archetype (bare-genus "Wisteria", 8 x 4 m, the Asian-trained geometry per the repo's own deepIntel vsCatalog note), so the fix is to repoint the group default at 'Amethyst Falls', exactly as proposed. (b) Soften "precisely, flatly false": in the plan-view sense a trained vine does occupy little bed area. The defensible claim is that wisteria is the single worst plant in the library to attach a no-footprint clause to (root suckers, rooting stolons, a 4 m spread recorded in the app's own catalog, and a support that must be structural), and that compatibility.ts:1065 fires the clause for every Climber with no spread or vigour check. (c) Blast radius is small and should be stated: wisteria is a companion on only 3 lists (feather-reed-grass 61.1, blue-oat-grass 54.4, jasmine 46.9), two carrying the footprint clause. Severity stays high because the recommendation surface (src/browse/PlantCompanions.tsx) shows zero caution and offers a one-tap "Add to garden", the harm is severe and effectively irreversible, and 'Karl Foerster' feather reed grass is one of the most-planted grasses a beginner will open. Also worth noting for the report: the audit's own invasive-recount.json lens misses wisteria entirely.

Two pieces of corroboration to fold into the report. (1) The precise data defect is src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts:381, `wisteria: { deerResistant: true }` - no `spreader: true`, so the engine's own x0.8 spreader guardrail never fires on it. The same file DOES flag campsis (line 385) and miscanthus (line 423, whose comment reads "M. sinensis self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US") as spreaders, so wisteria is a straightforward omission from a guardrail that already exists. (2) src/browse/PlantCompanions.tsx renders only the companion's name, category and reason - there is no safety or invasive badge on a companion chip - so wisteria's own curated `safety.level: "serious"` and its native-vs-invasive note never reach the gardener reading the feather-reed-grass card. Not a one-off either: wisteria is offered as a companion to 7 subjects (blue-oat-grass, delphinium-magic-fountains-dark-blue, feather-reed-grass, feather-reed-grass-gold, feather-reed-grass-white, jasmine, little-gem). Soften "precisely, flatly false" to "seriously misleading".

Survives. Two refinements for the report: (a) the offending clause is not wisteria-specific, it is the shared Climber template at compatibility.ts:1065, so the fix must suppress or qualify it for vigorous woody vines generally (wisteria also reaches blue-oat-grass at rank 5 of 8 and jasmine with the same clause); (b) the harm path is concrete because the companion card itself exposes "Add to garden" with no safety or invasiveness line (PlantCompanions.tsx), so the caution in the wisteria plant detail is never seen on this route. The proposed fix is well-supported by the repo's own precedent: taxonomy.ts already carries a spreader flag applied to miscanthus for invasiveness, and wisteria simply lacks it. Exposure caveat worth one line: companions are reveal-gated (3 per account) and this card ranks last in the list, so the pairing fires less often than a top-3 companion would.

title: Floribunda Rose -> Arborvitae (and Saucer Magnolia): the reason literally tells the beginner to plant a full-sun rose under a 6 m dense conifer
subject: floribunda-rose
claim: "Shares full sun and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath." (score 47; saucer-magnolia gets the same "stands well above at 7 m, a canopy to plant beneath")
why: Read the clause in the direction the code actually means it (compatibility.ts:1066: when the companion is taller than the subject and the ratio exceeds CANOPY_RATIO, the companion is the canopy and THE SUBJECT is the underplanting). So the advice is: put the rose under the arborvitae. That is a rose-killer on three counts: a floribunda needs 6+ hours of direct sun and would get near none under Thuja occidentalis, which holds dense foliage to the ground; Thuja has a greedy shallow root mat that creates dry, starved shade; and the still, unventilated air under an evergreen is the classic blackspot incubator for roses. The hard light filter passed the pair because both plants PREFER full sun - it never asks whether the companion will cast shade on the subject. The clause is self-contradictory: it matches on full sun, then advises a canopy that removes it.
fix: Suppress the "a canopy to plant beneath" phrasing (and the winter-structure/tree pairing generally) when the SUBJECT's light need is full-sun; a canopy companion is only honest for a subject that tolerates part-shade or less. Same clause is also wrong in this chunk for english-daisy -> sunflower ("stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath") and english-daisy -> joe-pye-weed (1.8 m).
scope: Any full-sun subject with a Tree or Conifer companion above the 6:1 ratio; at least 4 pairings in this chunk
notes: Finding is under-scoped rather than over-scoped; two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The same "arborvitae ... a canopy to plant beneath" clause fires for four more full-sun subjects: peony -> arborvitae, lupine -> arborvitae (47), daylily -> arborvitae and iris-bearded -> arborvitae (46.7). Peony under a conifer is the textbook cause of a peony that never blooms. (2) The engine's tallEnoughToShelter guard is effectively inert: tierOf(1 m) is already >= 2, so the canopy clause also fires for 1 m perennials, e.g. crocus -> echinops ("stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath"). So the proposed fix (suppress the canopy phrasing when the subject needs full sun) is necessary but not sufficient - the height gate needs raising too (a canopy should mean a tree/large shrub, not a 1 m globe thistle). Also worth naming: Thuja occidentalis as filed (spread 1.5 m, columnar screen/hedge) is not a canopy tree in any sense, so the clause is botanically false even before the sun conflict.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) Scope the blame precisely: the PAIR is not itself a botanical error (a floribunda in front of an arborvitae screen is a normal, good combination). The defect is entirely the generated REASON, which contradicts its own "shares full sun" opener, and which is factually wrong about a narrow columnar conifer that has no canopy to plant beneath. (2) The bug is broader than trees, which strengthens it: tierOf(1 m) returns 2 (compatibility.ts:617-622, MIDDLE_TIER_M = 0.9), so the tallEnoughToShelter guard - whose stated purpose at lines 1057-1060 is to stop exactly this - lets the clause fire for ANY companion over 0.9 m at a 6:1 ratio. english-daisy's list calls a 1 m HELENIUM "a canopy to plant beneath", plus cleome (1.2 m), false-sunflower (1.2 m), feather-reed-grass (1.5 m) and cosmos (1 m). The fix should therefore be two-part: (a) suppress the canopy phrasing when the subject's light need is full-sun, and (b) require the companion to be genuine woody structure (STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M / Tree or Conifer category), not merely tierOf >= 2, before any "canopy" language is used.

Confirmed and broader than reported: this is a class of 18 shipped pairings where a FULL-SUN subject is told a >=4 m woody companion is "a canopy to plant beneath". Several are worse than the rose: peony -> arborvitae (rank 8) and iris-bearded -> arborvitae (rank 8) both simply refuse to bloom in shade (bearded iris rhizomes rot); zinnia -> crabapple (rank 2), bee-balm -> crabapple (rank 2), shasta-daisy/dill/gaura/potentilla -> crabapple, lupine -> arborvitae, daylily -> arborvitae, spirea/larkspur -> redbud, and rose-of-sharon -> tulip-tree (a 20 m tree). Two corrections to the finding as written: (a) DROP the root-competition and blackspot legs of the reasoning - both are on the engine's documented not-modelled list; the self-contradicting light clause is the whole case and is squarely in scope. (b) The proposed fix is too broad. Gating on "subject is full-sun" would suppress 171 of the 241 canopy clauses, most of them harmless (a 0.2 m calibrachoa in front of a 1.5 m grass; english-daisy -> sunflower/joe-pye-weed, which the finding cites, are in this harmless bucket - a 1.8-2 m herbaceous perennial does not shade out a front-of-border daisy, and calling a 1 m helenium "a canopy" is merely silly phrasing, not dangerous advice). The correct gate is on the COMPANION being a genuine overhead shade caster (category Tree/Conifer, or height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M) while the subject's light need is full-sun, which isolates exactly the 18 harmful cases and leaves the benign front-of-border phrasing alone.

title: Two of the deadliest plants in the catalog (Colchicum, Monkshood) are recommended with cheerful benefit-only reasons and no caution, and 'Autumn Crocus' is listed directly under 'Crocus'
subject: echinops
claim: echinops -> colchicum: "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in early fall as this one finishes; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months." And foamy-bells -> monkshood: "...blooms in late summer, extending the bed's season; taller at 1.2 m, for the layer behind it; both offer pollinator forage, in different months."
why: The repo's own colchicum.json rates it "Poison severity ... High and ingestion of any part can be fatal to humans and animals; it is a medical emergency", colchicine has "no clinically available specific antidote", one case series reported 25 percent mortality, and it explicitly warns: "This is the truly dangerous 'autumn crocus'; do not confuse it with non-toxic spring Crocus" - naming correct identification as "itself the primary safety control". The Echinops card then presents Crocus at position 4 and Autumn Crocus at position 5, adjacent, both as low purple bulbs, with nothing distinguishing them. Aconitum is the other one: skin-absorbable aconitine, the plant every horticulture course tells you to glove up for. Neither reason carries a word of caution. The engine is not being asked to model toxicity in the score - it is being asked not to hand a beginner (the north-star user is someone's mother, possibly with grandchildren or a cat) a fatal plant with a sentence about pollinators and nothing else.
fix: Carry the toxicity flag that already exists in deepIntel through to the companion card: append a short caution clause to the reason ("all parts are toxic; wear gloves") for any companion flagged high-toxicity, and never rank Colchicum adjacent to Crocus in the same list without the 'not a true crocus' note.
scope: Every list colchicum and monkshood appear in as archetype companions; colchicum is a top-8 companion for 3 of the 10 subjects in this chunk alone
refutations: The finding's core premise is factually wrong about the product. Colchicum and Monkshood are NOT handed to a beginner "with nothing else": src/browse/PlantDetail.tsx renders the curated safety bar (ShieldAlert, level "Serious") as the FIRST item of the very same left rail that PlantCompanions closes, and src/garden/intel/curated/colchicum.json's safety text is "Deadly. Every part, especially the corm, holds colchicine, a potent toxin with no antidote. Eating any of it can kill people, dogs, cats and horses; a medical emergency." (ASPCA, PMC). Monkshood's is equivalent. "Nothing distinguishing" Crocus from Autumn Crocus is also wrong: curated/crocus.json's own safety line is "Do not confuse it with deadly autumn crocus (Colchicum)" and its good-to-know is the ID key (three stamens vs six, spring-with-leaves vs leafless-in-fall); the two mini-cards further differ by name, photo and bloom strip at opposite ends of the year, and the reasons themselves say "blooms in late winter" vs "blooms in early fall". Horticulturally, the pairings are sound and entirely mainstream: Colchicum is sold in every fall bulb catalogue, Aconitum holds an RHS AGM, and recommending them is not irresponsible advice by any professional standard (the same logic would bar foxglove, daffodil, lily-of-the-valley, yew, hellebore and delphinium, all already in this library). Toxicity is a plant attribute, not a pair attribute; the app already surfaces it where extension plant profiles put it. No gardener is harmed by planting Colchicum beside a globe thistle, and the ingestion hazard is unchanged by which screen surfaced the plant. This is not a pairing-data or reason-correctness defect.
notes:
VERIFIED, but three corrections must be carried into the report, one of which is load-bearing:

1. THE PROPOSED FIX IS NOT IMPLEMENTABLE AS WRITTEN and the report will be refutable if it ships as-is. There is no "high-toxicity" flag to carry through. CuratedSafety.level (src/garden/intel/curatedStore.ts:21-26) is BINARY: "serious" | "mild". And 65 of the 276 curated archetypes are level "serious" - including Daffodil, Hosta, Daylily, Hydrangea, Allium, Chives, Azalea, Rhododendron, Clematis, Hellebore, Juniper, Red Maple. Appending a caution clause to every "serious" companion would stamp a warning on ~24 percent of all recommendations and dilute it into noise. Restate the fix as: (a) make the companion mini card tap through to PlantDetail, which ALREADY renders the graded safety text, and/or (b) show a small ShieldAlert glyph on the mini card for serious-safety companions, and route "Add to garden" for those through the detail card first.

2. CREDIT THE SAFETY BAR, OR THE FINDING IS ATTACKABLE. The product is NOT silent about toxicity: PlantDetail.tsx:353-358 renders a ShieldAlert "Safety / Serious" bar in danger tokens, and colchicum.json's text is excellent ("Deadly... no antidote... a medical emergency"), as is monkshood's ("One of the most poisonous garden plants... Always wear gloves"). The defect is precisely located: PlantCompanions.tsx is a DEAD-END surface - it has the Add-to-garden button but no link to the safety bar. Frame it that way and it cannot be waved off.

3. Minor overstatement to soften: "nothing distinguishing them" (Crocus vs Autumn Crocus) - the mini cards do carry visibly different BloomBars (Feb-Mar vs Sep-Oct). Nothing NAMES the difference, and the curated "Not a crocus, not saffron" fact never reaches this surface, which is the real point. Also note they sit at positions 4 and 5 on the Echinops card, so they surface only after the user taps "Show all 8 companions" (INLINE_FULL = 3), not above the fold.

Exposure figures worth adding: colchicum is recommended to 36 of 276 subjects, monkshood to 9; crocus and colchicum are adjacent in 4 lists (echinops, liatris, mullein, red-hot-poker).

Two precision corrections, plus one fact that sharpens the fix. (1) The companion list renders only INLINE_FULL = 3 cards inline (PlantCompanions.tsx), so Crocus (4) and Autumn Crocus (5) surface once the user taps "Show all 8 companions" - still adjacent, still one tap away, with the reason folded until tapped. (2) "Nothing distinguishing them" is slightly strong: the two mini-cards do show different photos and different bloom strips (late winter vs fall); what they share is an identical "Bulb" type line and zero textual or safety cue. (3) The toxicity flag is not merely present in deepIntel - it is a first-class typed field (CuratedSafety.level = "serious") already rendered as a red Safety bar in PlantDetail.tsx:353-358 for the plant's own card (colchicum: "Deadly...a medical emergency"; monkshood: "One of the most poisonous garden plants"). The gap is precisely the companion mini-card, which shows name, type, spread, bloom bar, reason, Save and "Add to garden" - so a beginner can add Colchicum to a garden from the Echinops card without ever seeing the warning the app already holds. Crocus's own curated record even carries the exact disambiguation line ("Do not confuse it with deadly autumn crocus (Colchicum)"), which never reaches the companion list where the confusion is actually manufactured.

One narrow, low-severity residue is worth keeping if anything is: the companion mini-card (src/browse/PlantCompanions.tsx, CompanionCard) offers "Add to garden" inline, so a user can place a serious-toxicity plant without ever opening its own detail card and its "Safety: Serious" bar. A small safety dot/badge on the mini-card for curated safety.level === "serious" is a cheap UX polish. It should be filed as low-severity product polish, not as a high-severity irresponsible-advice finding, and it is not a defect in the compatibility engine, the pairing, or the reason text.

title: Japanese Forest Grass is credited with "seed heads [that] stand through winter, for structure and the birds" - it has no flowers in the library and dies back completely
subject: elephant-ear
claim: "Takes part sun to part shade and moderate to high water; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; lower at 0.4 m, for the front of the border; they only overlap in zones 8 to 9." (score 31.3)
why: Hakonechloa macra is stored with NO bloom array at all (plantLibrary.ts:2964: foliage-only, tags foliage/green/shade/cascading), so the engine's own model says it never flowers - yet the reason asserts it holds seed heads through winter and feeds birds. The repo's japanese-forest-grass.json is blunt about it: "unlike many ornamental grasses it does not hold up for winter interest and dies back completely", "many cultivars do not set viable seed", and the standard care is "cut the clump back to the ground in late winter". The cause is compatibility.ts:433, `seedheads: category === "Grass" || WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus)` - a blanket grant to every grass. The irony is that the code comment right above WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA (line 191-195) refuses exactly this kind of loose claim for daisy-family perennials because "it would have this engine tell a gardener that English daisy ... holds its seed heads through winter", then does the same thing to every foliage grass. The plant also scores a bogus 15-point winter-structure bonus it has not earned.
fix: Change the grass branch to `category === "Grass" && !foliage` (i.e. it must have a recorded bloom/plume), which keeps Calamagrostis and Chasmanthium and correctly drops Hakonechloa and the other foliage-only grasses.
scope: Every pairing where a bloom-less grass (all 4 japanese-forest-grass variants, and any other foliage-only grass) is the companion
notes: Two corrections to carry into the report. (a) SCALE IS LARGER THAN STATED: Japanese Forest Grass is recommended as a companion to 9 subjects (amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, lady-fern, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, wild-ginger) and all 9 of 9 reasons carry the false winter-seed-head/bird clause - it is 100% of this plant's appearances, not one bad pairing. Lead with that. (b) THE PROPOSED FIX IS CORRECT BUT ITS RATIONALE IS SLIGHTLY OFF: the four Hakonechloa entries are the ONLY foliage-only grasses in plantLibrary.ts (every other grass - Calamagrostis, Chasmanthium, Miscanthus, Panicum, Pennisetum, Schizachyrium, Cortaderia, Festuca, Helictotrichon - carries a bloom array), so `category === "Grass" && !foliage` drops exactly the Hakonechloa group and touches nothing else; drop the phrase "and the other foliage-only grasses," there are none. (c) Strongest evidence to quote in the report is the self-contradiction: the same plant's own curated fact card says "It dies back fully with no winter interest" (source: Wisconsin) while the companion reason says "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds." (d) Avoid implying H. macra never flowers botanically - it flowers, but the inflorescences are insignificant and it holds no winter structure; keep the finding's existing "no bloom array in the library" phrasing.

Scope is wider than the one pair: Hakonechloa is an archetype, so the false "seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds" clause plus the unearned 15-point winter-structure bonus fire on EVERY subject it is recommended to that is not itself structural (elephant-ear is one instance). The misdirection is concrete for a beginner: the repo's own care advice is to cut the clump to the ground in late winter because it offers no winter interest, while the card tells them its seed heads feed birds. Fix verified safe: only the 4 Hakonechloa rows among 33 Grass entries lack a bloom array.

Downgrade to low. Keep it in the report as a wrong-reason: the printed clause is false and directly contradicts the plant's own free-tier fact card ("It dies back fully with no winter interest"), and the unearned winterStructure credit lifts the grass into 9 companion lists on a trait it does not have. But it is one plant, 9 pairings, always rank 6-8 of 8 (never inline), and the pair itself is horticulturally sound - so no gardener is materially harmed. Worth noting that the proposed fix is verified collateral-free: japanese-forest-grass is the ONLY bloomless Grass in the archetype companion pool, so `category === "Grass" && !foliage` drops exactly it and keeps every other grass's seed-head credit.

title: Colchicum is recommended as the low carpeting layer under three different subjects - backwards, since it is bare ground for the whole summer and is the plant that needs covering
subject: false-sunflower
claim: "Shares full sun and moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; both offer pollinator forage." (also echinops -> colchicum and feather-reed-grass -> colchicum, same "low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath" clause)
why: The stored height 0.15 m is the leafless autumn flower stem. Colchicum's actual habit (colchicum.json): "strap leaves emerge in spring, yellow and die back by early summer as the plant goes dormant, then in late summer to autumn the flowers rise directly from the ground with no foliage present" - the spring leaf clump is coarse, up to about 0.25 m (some sources 0.35 m) and wider than the stored 0.1 m spread, and then the plant is simply ABSENT from early summer until the flowers appear. It carpets nothing. Standard practice is the exact inverse of what the app prints: you plant Colchicum THROUGH a low groundcover so the fall flowers have something to lean on and the summer gap is hidden. Compounding it under Feather Reed Grass specifically: Calamagrostis is a cool-season grass that is up and dense in early spring, which is the one window when Colchicum needs "full sun reaching the foliage" to rebuild the corm (colchicum.json), so the corm weakens year on year.
fix: Exclude dormancy-gap bulbs (Colchicum and other leafless-flowering, summer-dormant corms) from the "carpet the ground beneath" / ground-layer clause; they belong on the receiving end of the bulb-cover factor, not the covering end. Widening the stored spread from 0.1 m to ~0.2 m would also stop the 3D plan under-drawing the spring foliage.
scope: 3 of the 10 subjects in this chunk get colchicum in the top 8 with this clause; applies to every full-sun subject over ~1 m
refutations:
The finding's two load-bearing claims are both contradicted by the repo's own sourced data, and the residue is a stock-template wording nit with no plausible user harm.

1. "The stored 0.15 m is the leafless flower stem / the data is wrong." The repo's own tier-1 harvest, C:/Users/etano/bloomseye-studio/src/garden/intel/deepIntel/colchicum.json, adjudicates exactly this and rules the opposite: claim c-identity, vsCatalog = "agrees-with-nuance", "Stored height 0.15 m sits at the low end of the sourced 0.15 to 0.20 m range and agrees" (extensions list OVERALL height and spread near 0.15-0.20 m, not just the flower stem). Only the spread (0.1 m vs ~0.15-0.20 m) is called "a touch small", and that is already logged as a known gap in the same file. So there is no library-data error to inherit.

2. "Feather Reed Grass specifically weakens the corm by shading the spring foliage." The same intel file's own protocol says Colchicum "is a strong choice for high-browse-pressure beds and naturalizing in grass" (i.e. in turf, a far more competitive spring root/leaf mat than the edge of a clumping Calamagrostis), and lists the site as "full sun to part shade". A corm that persists and naturalizes in mown grass is not going to be starved by being planted at the foot of a clumping ornamental grass. The compounding harm is speculative and unsourced.

3. "Colchicum is uniquely, backwards, cast as the carpeting layer." The clause is a generic layering template (compatibility.ts layeringClause, fired whenever heightRatio > 6 and the taller plant is tier >= 2). It fires on 284 pairs across many low candidates, including crocus (10), windflower (16), scilla (8) and grape hyacinth (7) - all spring bulbs that likewise vanish for the summer. It means "this is the low/ground layer, plant it at the foot in a drift", not "this is an evergreen groundcover"; and for Colchicum, whose signature use is a naturalized drift of autumn flowers at ground level, the phrase is apt at exactly the moment the reason is talking about (the fall succession clause). Nothing in the sentence promises summer ground cover.

4. The dormancy-gap asymmetry the finding wants fixed is a DOCUMENTED, deliberate engine decision, not an oversight: the isSpringEphemeral doc comment names it - "Summer bulbs (dahlia, canna) and autumn crocus fail the month test" - because the bulb-cover factor exists to mask RIPENING SPRING FOLIAGE, which Colchicum sheds in a season when nothing needs hiding. My lens is whether it matters, and here it does not: the actual pairings (Heliopsis, Echinops, Calamagrostis + Colchicum) are combinations real garden practice endorses, a beginner following them loses no plant and gets the lilac autumn flowers the app shows them, and the worst case is mild aesthetic surprise at a summer gap under a 1-1.5 m perennial whose own foliage occupies that ground anyway.

Note also the title is factually off in scale (23 subjects carry the clause for Colchicum, not three) - which does not help it, but underlines that this is a template-level copy question, not a botanical error in a named pair.
notes: Two corrections, both enlarging the finding. (1) SCOPE: the "to carpet the ground beneath" clause fires for Colchicum under 23 subjects, not 3 - amaranth, beautyberry, boxwood, butterfly-bush, canna, cardinal-flower, cleome, delphinium, echinops, false-sunflower, feather-reed-grass, forsythia, gladiolus, hardy-hibiscus, helenium, hollyhock, hydrangea, joe-pye-weed, lily-asiatic, mullein, red-hot-poker, spirea, sunflower. Colchicum is the single most frequent recipient of that clause in the entire catalog (23), ahead of creeping-phlox (17), sweet-alyssum (17) and thyme (15), which are real mat-formers where the clause is correct. It is recommended 188 times overall (archetype-standing.json). (2) The clause also misfires, less severely, on other non-carpeting bulbs (windflower 16, crocus 10, scilla 8, grape-hyacinth 7), so the fix should gate the ground-layer clause on habit, not height alone; Colchicum is the worst case because it is the only one that is both absent all summer AND the plant that needs the covering. Additional engine detail worth carrying: isSpringEphemeral gates on bloom month and its own comment says "autumn crocus fail the month test", so Colchicum can never receive the 26-point bulb-cover factor that its dying spring foliage is the textbook case for. Soften the feather-reed-grass line to "risks weakening the corm" rather than asserting it.

Scale correction: it is not three subjects. Colchicum is recommended to 36 subjects, and 23 of them print the "low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath" clause (amaranth, beautyberry, boxwood, butterfly-bush, canna, cardinal-flower, cleome, delphinium, echinops, false-sunflower, feather-reed-grass, forsythia, gladiolus, hardy-hibiscus, helenium, hollyhock, hydrangea, joe-pye-weed, lily-asiatic, mullein, red-hot-poker, spirea, sunflower). Wording correction: describe 0.15 m as the low end of the sourced 0.15 to 0.20 m listed height rather than "the leafless autumn flower stem" (deepIntel gives flower stems as 0.15 to 0.25 m and the plant's listed height/spread as 0.15 to 0.20 m). Additional code support for the fix: isSpringEphemeral (compatibility.ts:664) requires last bloom month <= EPHEMERAL_LAST_BLOOM_MONTH, so colchicum (bloom 9-10) can never be scored as the bulb that NEEDS covering, only as a cover, which is the exact inversion the finding names.

If anything is salvaged for the report, it is only a low-value copy nit, stripped of both substantive claims: the stock phrase "to carpet the ground beneath" reads as ground COVER to a beginner, and 23 of the 284 firings of that clause are on Colchicum, which is absent from early summer until it flowers. Any rewrite must not repeat the finding's data claim (the repo's own deepIntel says the stored 0.15 m height is correct) or the Calamagrostis corm-starvation claim (the same file says Colchicum naturalizes in grass). The one genuinely supportable data tweak is unrelated to the pairing: stored spread 0.1 m should be ~0.15-0.20 m, already recorded as a known gap in colchicum.json.

title: Freesia is told to plant Pampas Grass, praised for the seed heads that make it a listed invasive, and to plant "beneath" a saw-edged clump
subject: freesia
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late summer, extending the bed's season; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath; they only overlap in zones 9 to 10.
why: Cortaderia selloana is one of the worst ornamental invasives in exactly the band this pairing lives in: freesia (9-10) x pampas (7-11) = zones 9-10, i.e. coastal California, the Gulf/Southeast and Hawaii. Cal-IPC rates it High; a single female plume releases on the order of 100,000 wind-borne seeds. The reason praises "its seed heads ... for structure and the birds" - the seed heads ARE the dispersal mechanism, and birds do not feed on them, so the one trait the app celebrates is the one that invades. The taxonomy already knows how to say this: src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts line 423 reads `miscanthus: { spreader: true }, // M. sinensis self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US`, yet line 424 reads `cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits`, so no spreader penalty and no caution ever fires. Second error in the same sentence: pampas is a dense basal clump with serrated, skin-slicing leaves - there is no space under it and nothing can be planted beneath it, so "a canopy to plant beneath" is both false and a way to get a beginner cut.
fix: src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts: `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` (matching the miscanthus precedent), and suppress the seed-head praise clause for spreader-flagged plants.
scope: Every list Pampas Grass appears in as a companion; Cortaderia is an archetype so it can be recommended catalog-wide.
notes: Two corrections. (a) "No caution ever fires" is overstated: src/garden/intel/curated/pampas-grass.json does carry free-tier facts "Invasive; check local lists" (Cal-IPC High) and "Leaf blades cut skin", rendered by PlantDetail.tsx - but only on pampas grass's OWN card. On freesia's card, PlantCompanions.tsx renders the companion as a mini card with the reason plus an "Add to garden" button, so a beginner can plant it without ever reaching the warning. The correct charge is that the recommendation surface carries no caution and actively contradicts the app's own sourced guidance (leave the plumes standing vs cut and bag them before they shatter). (b) Soften "birds do not feed on them" to "no documented bird-forage value" - the repo's own deepIntel records pollinator/forage value as nil for a wind-pollinated grass and explicitly logs the absence of visitation data, which is enough; a flat negative on bird use is more than the sources support. Also, the proposed fix is stronger than stated: with cortaderia: { spreader: true }, isVulnerableTo(freesia, pampas) is true (0.4 <= 0.6 x 2.5), so spreaderSmothers fires and the pair is HARD-BLOCKED, not merely penalised x0.8.

One overstatement to fix before publishing: "no caution ever fires" is too strong. src/garden/intel/curated/pampas-grass.json carries free-tier extraFacts "Invasive; check local lists" (Cal-IPC) and "Leaf blades cut skin", and PlantDetail.tsx (line 164) renders extraFacts, so a user who taps through to Pampas Grass's own detail card does see a caution. What never fires is any caution in the COMPANION context: cortaderia is not spreader-flagged, so no x0.8 penalty applies (confirmed by the empty spreader entry in the pairing's penalties array), and the recommendation shown on freesia's card actively praises the seed heads with no counterweight. Also worth stating precisely: the "for structure and the birds" clause is not pampas-specific at all - the engine sets seedheads=true for every plant of category Grass, so this template attaches to a wind-dispersed invasive by default. The canopy claim is independently wrong on its own terms (a dense 1.5 m-wide clump of serrated blades has no plantable understory).

Finding stands as written; severity high is if anything conservative (pampas appears on 191 companion lists, praised 191 times, cautioned 0). One correction to the PROPOSED FIX, not the finding: `spreader` in this engine means within-bed vegetative aggression ("Aggressive spreader that can overrun delicate neighbours") and is load-bearing for the `spreader-smothers` HARD blocker (compatibility.ts:503-512) and the clause "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging". Setting `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` would (a) emit a botanically off clause - pampas is a clumper that spreads by wind-borne seed into wild land, not by running into your bed - and (b) silently DELETE a large share of its 191 pairings via the blocker (freesia at 0.4 m is well under the overtop fraction of 2.5 m) rather than cautioning them. The honest fix is a distinct `invasive` flag that suppresses the seed-head praise clause and emits a regional caution. Separately, the height-layering clause "a canopy to plant beneath" should never fire for a dense basal Grass clump, invasive or not.

title: Gardenia (a white-flowered shrub) is colour-classified as YELLOW, so all 8 of its reasons offer a colour benefit that does not exist
subject: gardenia
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; low at 0.12 m, to carpet the ground beneath; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its white flowers settle the yellow.
why: Gardenia's bloom hex in plantLibrary.ts line 2526 is `#fbfbf5` (an off-white cream) and its own tags say "white". But colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts computes saturation at lightness 0.97, where s = 0.0235 / 0.055 = 0.43, so the white test (`l >= 0.82 && s < 0.25`) fails and hue 60 falls through to "yellow". Result: every gardenia companion reason talks about "the yellow" ("its white flowers settle the yellow", "a soft colour beside the yellow") for a shrub whose flowers are pure white. The Sweet Alyssum line is the plainest nonsense: white alyssum is sold to the user as settling a yellow that isn't there, i.e. white settling white. A beginner picking companions on colour is being given a false premise on all 8 pairings.
fix: Either set gardenia's bloom hex to a neutral white (`#fbfbfb`, as sweet-alyssum uses) or, better, make colorName() return "white" for any hue when l >= 0.9, since saturation is numerically unstable at near-white lightness.
scope: All 8 gardenia pairings, plus any other plant carrying a cream/off-white bloom hex (the same math misfires for any hex with l > 0.9 and a slight hue cast).
notes: Two corrections. (1) Severity is medium, not high: the misnaming does not affect scoring or ranking - colourScore hits the isNeutralBloom branch (l > 0.85) and returns relation "neutral", score 0.85, regardless of the name, and the colour weight is only 5. No pairing is chosen or ordered differently; only the user-facing sentence is wrong. (2) The bug is not gardenia-only, so the "just fix gardenia's hex" option would leave it live elsewhere. Scanning all subject packets for a white-tagged plant with a "the yellow" clause: gardenia 8/8, pampas-grass 8/8, jasmine 6/8, moonflower 2/8, solomons-seal 1/8. Any near-white cream trips it (#f4f2ea -> s=0.31, h=48 -> "yellow"). The defect belongs to colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts, and the correct fix is the second one proposed: treat near-white lightness as white (align the threshold with the engine's own isNeutralBloom, l > 0.85), since saturation is numerically unstable there.

Two precisions for the report. (1) The colour SCORE is correct, only the label is wrong: isNeutralBloom (compatibility.ts:823, s < 0.2 || l > 0.85) does catch gardenia as a neutral mediator, so the 0.85 neutral relation is right; the defect is purely colorName()'s bucket name leaking into the copy, so the fix belongs in colorName() (or the hex), not in the scoring. (2) All seven gardenia cultivars carry the same near-cream hexes (#fbfbf5 / #fcfcf6 / #fcfcf8), which classify "yellow" too, so the same false clause would appear wherever they are the subject; Browse's colour facet also lists gardenia under both white (from the tag) and yellow (from the hex). Severity lowered to medium: the copy states a benefit that does not exist and misleads a beginner choosing by colour, but no pair is sent to a spot it cannot grow and no hazardous plant is promoted.

Survives, and is broader than the finding states - but severity should be medium, not high, and the framing and fix need two corrections.

(1) SCORE vs TEXT. The finding's title says the reasons "offer a colour benefit that does not exist", which implies the scoring is skewed. Precisely: isNeutralBloom() (s < 0.2 OR l > 0.85) already catches these blooms, so colourScore correctly returns relation "neutral" at 0.85 and the numeric score and ranking are unaffected. The defect is confined to the printed clause. That is why this is a user-facing copy falsehood (credibility, trust in the advice) rather than horticultural harm - no plant is put in the wrong place and no plant dies. Medium.

(2) SCOPE. It is not gardenia-only. Across the 276 archetype packets, white-flowered subjects emitting a "the yellow" clause: gardenia 8/8, pampas-grass 8/8, jasmine 6/8, moonflower 2/8, solomons-seal 1/8. jasmine (#fbfbf5), moonflower (#fbfbf5) and solomons-seal (#fbfbf2) are pure-white flowers misread as yellow by the same saturation instability. Non-archetype white cultivars are affected as subjects too: #f4f2ea (rose-of-sharon-white-chiffon, camellia-alba-plena, weigela-white) and #f2f0e8 (camellia-setsugekka) classify as yellow, and #f2ece2 (rose-of-sharon-red-heart) classifies as orange.

(3) FIX. Recolouring gardenia's hex to #fbfbfb fixes one plant and leaves jasmine, moonflower and Solomon's seal still lying. The correct fix is the classifier: in colorName(), return "white" for any hue at very high lightness (e.g. l >= 0.9, or gate on chroma d = max-min rather than HSL saturation, which is unstable near white). That also brings colorName in line with isNeutralBloom, which already treats l > 0.85 as neutral - the two functions currently disagree about the same bloom.

Best single instance to cite remains gardenia -> sweet-alyssum: "its white flowers settle the yellow" is white settling white.

title: Summer-dormant bulbs (Freesia, Crown Imperial) are paired with companions whose whole point is summer irrigation, which rots the bulbs
subject: freesia
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back; ... they only overlap in zone 9.
why: Freesia is a winter-growing Cape corm: standard culture is to withhold water completely once the foliage yellows and keep the corms bone dry (or lift them) through summer. Calamagrostis x acutiflora is a cool-season grass grown precisely for even summer moisture. In zone 9, the only shared zone, that bed is watered all summer and the dormant freesia corms rot - this is the #1 recommendation in freesia's list. The single "moderate" water field cannot see the difference between water in growth and water in dormancy, so the pairing looks perfect and is exactly backwards. The same failure hits Crown Imperial (Fritillaria imperialis, whose bulbs are notorious for rotting without sharp drainage and a dry summer rest) paired with Delphinium (56.5) and Hollyhock (54.8), the two hungriest, wettest border perennials in its list.
fix: Add a dry-summer-dormancy flag to bulbs like Freesia, Fritillaria imperialis, tulips and alliums, and either block moderate/high-water companions from the bulbCover factor or make the reason carry the caution ("stop watering once the foliage dies back").
scope: Freesia -> feather-reed-grass, sunflower, cleome; Fritillaria -> delphinium, hollyhock, feather-reed-grass. Any dry-dormant bulb in the catalog.
notes:
Scope the finding to the single pair freesia -> feather-reed-grass (score 46.7, freesia's #1 companion). Verified data: freesia = full-sun / moderate / zones 9-10 (plantLibrary.ts:3187); feather-reed-grass = full-sun / moderate / zones 4-9 (plantLibrary.ts:2637); intersection is zone 9 alone. The defect is that the flat "moderate" water field cannot distinguish water-in-growth from water-in-dormancy, so a summer-dormant Cape corm that needs a bone-dry rest is topped by a cool-season grass that only performs in zone 9 with summer irrigation, and the generated reason praises the grass for growing over the ripening bulb foliage (i.e. for sitting on the corms with the drip on).

DROP the Crown Imperial / delphinium / hollyhock claim entirely - Fritillaria imperialis in a mixed border with delphiniums and hollyhocks is a classic combination, and neither companion is a wet-soil plant; asserting otherwise would put a false botanical claim in the report.

Also worth noting in the writeup as mitigation: freesia's remaining companions (agave, gaillardia, blue oat grass, baptisia) are all correctly low-water dry-summer plants, so the fix is narrow - a dry-summer-dormancy flag on Cape/Mediterranean bulbs that either excludes moderate/high-water companions or makes the reason carry the caution "stop watering once the foliage dies back."

Trim two overreaches before publishing. (1) "Calamagrostis is grown precisely for even summer moisture" overstates it; sources file it as average/medium-moisture, well-drained, and it tolerates some dryness. The accurate framing is that it is a summer-active cool-season grass irrigated to medium moisture through exactly the months the freesia corm must stay bone dry. (2) The Crown Imperial half is materially weaker than the freesia half and should be demoted to a secondary mention, not a co-headline: F. imperialis wants sharp drainage but is a long-standing cottage-border bulb routinely grown with delphiniums and hollyhocks, so that is a drainage caution rather than the flat seasonal contradiction the freesia pair is. Lead with freesia -> feather-reed-grass (its #1 companion, 46.7, sole shared zone 9), where the reason clause "Shares full sun and moderate water" is affirmatively false in the season that matters and the 26-weight bulbCover factor pays full marks for precisely the summer leaf mass that comes with the irrigation that rots the corm.

Narrow the finding to the verified pair: freesia -> feather-reed-grass, score 46.7, the #1 recommendation on freesia's card. Freesia is a winter-growing Cape corm that requires a dry summer rest once the foliage yellows; Calamagrostis x acutiflora is a cool-season grass grown for even summer moisture. The engine's bulbCover factor (weight 26, documented as "the classic interplanting technique") tells the user to plant the grass over the ripening corms, so in zone 9 (their only overlap) the corms sit wet through dormancy and rot. Drop or heavily soften the Crown Imperial claim: F. imperialis with delphinium/hollyhock is mainstream border practice, its failure mode is poor drainage rather than irrigation, and hollyhock is a moderate-water, drought-tolerant plant, not a "wet" one. Also note this is a single-slot defect, not a broken factor: freesia's remaining seven companions (agave, gaillardia, blue oat grass, baptisia, pampas grass, lavender) are all low-water and appropriate. The proposed fix is still the right shape but should be scoped down: a dry-summer-dormancy flag on Cape/mediterranean geophytes (Freesia first) that either bars moderate-water covers from the bulbCover factor or appends the caution "stop watering once the foliage dies back" to the reason.

title: Full-sun plants (Gaura, Four O'Clock) are told a 6 m crabapple is "a canopy to plant beneath"
subject: gaura
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months; stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: layeringClause() (compatibility.ts line 1066) emits "a canopy to plant beneath" on a height ratio plus a height-tier test alone; nothing checks whether the shorter plant can live in shade. Gaura (Oenothera lindheimeri) is a full-sun prairie plant that flops, stops flowering and rots off in the shade and root competition under a dense crabapple. The hard filter only established that both plants want full sun in the same spot; the sentence then instructs the user to put one in the other's shade, which is the opposite. Four O'Clock -> Crabapple carries the identical string with the same problem.
fix: Only emit the canopy phrasing when the shorter plant's sun value is part-sun or lower; otherwise fall back to "taller at 6 m, for the layer behind it".
scope: Any full-sun subject paired with a companion above the canopy tier (Gaura, Four O'Clock, and Freesia -> Pampas Grass in this chunk alone).
notes:
Two corrections to carry into the report.

(a) Drop or downgrade the Four O'Clock example. Mirabilis jalapa is genuinely listed full sun TO PART SHADE (MOBOT) and performs acceptably in part shade, so it is a weak second instance even though it carries the identical string. Anchor the finding on Gaura, and if a second example is wanted use bee-balm -> crabapple and bee-balm -> redbud (both present in the data with the same "a canopy to plant beneath" clause): Monarda in a tree's shade with reduced air movement is a documented powdery-mildew disaster, which is a harder horticultural harm than gaura's flopping.

(b) Scope is larger than the two named pairs and worth one sentence: the clause fires 241 times, 171 of them with a full-sun subject. Worse, tierOf() >= 2 is satisfied by 1.5 m plants, so the engine also tells the user that a 1.5 m switchgrass or perennial sunflower is "a canopy to plant beneath" for basket-of-gold and calibrachoa, which is not a canopy at all. The proposed fix (require the shorter plant to be part-sun or lower) is right; it should be paired with raising the canopy phrasing's height floor to a genuinely woody tier (about 4 m or Tree/Conifer category), not tier >= 2.

Two corrections to carry into the report. (1) The blast radius is larger than the finding states: the identical "stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath" clause is served to crabapple for at least eight full-sun subjects - bee-balm, crocosmia, dill, potentilla, shasta-daisy, zinnia, gaura and four-oclock - so it should be written up as a systematic layeringClause bug, with gaura (full-sun + LOW water, the worst case) as the headline pair. (2) The full reason strings for gaura and four-oclock are not byte-identical: four-oclock's pollinator clause reads "both offer pollinator forage, in different months" where gaura's reads "both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months". Only the canopy clause is shared. Also worth noting the existing canopy-wording lens does NOT catch this (it only checks the reverse "carpet the ground beneath" wording on short taller-plants), so the fix at line 1066 - gate the canopy phrasing on the shorter plant being part-sun or lower, else fall back to "taller at 6 m, for the layer behind it" - is the right and complete remedy.

Confirmed verbatim; both pairs are shown (gaura -> crabapple rank 5/8, four-oclock -> crabapple rank 6/8; full reason is rendered on tap, never truncated). The defect is systemic, not isolated: the "a canopy to plant beneath" clause fires on 241 pairs, 171 of them with a FULL-SUN subject (23 part-sun, 33 part-shade, 14 full-shade). It is also wrong in a second way - the "canopy" is frequently not one: the most-emitted canopies include perennial sunflower (1.5 m), feather reed grass (1.5 m) and cosmos (1.0 m, an Annual), because tallEnoughToShelter only requires tierOf(max height) >= 2. The proposed fix is the right shape but should also add a real height floor so a 1 m cosmos is never called a canopy. Minor correction: gaura's library height is 0.8 m (ratio 7.5:1), not 0.9. Severity stays medium: the pair itself is plantable (crabapple as backdrop, gaura in sun in front), so this is a wrong-reason defect, not a wrong-pair defect.

title: "Leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage" is claimed for an evergreen succulent (Agave) and an evergreen grass (Blue Oat Grass), neither of which leafs out
subject: freesia
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; they only overlap in zones 9 to 10.
why: Agave has no bloom entry at all in plantLibrary.ts (line 3260) and is an evergreen succulent rosette: it holds the same rosette year-round and never flushes new foliage in late spring, so it cannot 'leaf out to hide' anything - the clause is simply false, and the same sentence contradicts itself by then calling the plant evergreen. coversEphemeral() accepts any Perennial, so it never checks that the cover is deciduous. Physically it is worse: you cannot plant or lift freesia corms in the skirt of a spine-tipped Agave. The identical false clause is served for Blue Oat Grass (Helictotrichon sempervirens, tagged evergreen) to both Freesia and Crown Imperial - an evergreen tuft is the same size in April as in July and conceals no dying bulb leaves.
fix: In coversEphemeral(), exclude evergreen plants and succulent/foliage-only plants; require a deciduous herbaceous cover that actually expands after the bulb flowers.
scope: Freesia -> agave, blue-oat-grass; Fritillaria -> blue-oat-grass. Any evergreen perennial/grass offered as a bulb cover.
refutations: The mechanism is real (coversEphemeral accepts any tall-enough Perennial/Fern/Grass, and coverHasSpringPresence explicitly credits evergreens), but the finding misreads it as an error when it is a documented, correct design choice, and it overstates the harm to zero-harm levels. (1) compatibility.ts:677-690 states the judgment outright: an evergreen or foliage plant is the BEST spring-present cover, and that matches standard practice - bulbs are routinely planted through evergreen groundcovers, hellebores, heuchera, carex, liriope precisely so the ripening leaves are masked. The proposed fix (exclude evergreens from coversEphemeral) would destroy genuinely good pairings (snowdrop + hellebore, crocus + heuchera) to correct a verb. (2) The benefit the sentence claims is real for both pairs: agave (0.8 m) and blue oat grass (0.7 m) do screen 0.4 m freesia leaves, and both hold winter presence. This is not "a benefit the pair does not have" - it is the right benefit with a loose verb ("leafs out" for a plant whose foliage is already up). (3) The physical objection is wrong: in the zones the reason itself names (9-10) freesia is left in the ground and naturalises, it is not lifted, and nothing tells the user to plant inside the rosette; freesia + agave is in fact a well-matched Cape/Mediterranean dry-summer-rest combination. (4) No gardener is harmed - the worst case is a mildly awkward sentence. What survives is a low-severity copy nit in bulbCoverClause (line 1082): the verb should branch on cover.evergreen.
notes: Narrow the finding to Agave. Confirmed reason served for freesia -> agave: "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; they only overlap in zones 9 to 10." Agave (plantLibrary.ts:3260) is filed cat "Perennial" with no bloom, so compatibility.ts:430 marks it foliage=true; COVER_CATEGORIES (Perennial/Fern/Grass) + a height-only coversEphemeral() check hand it a full 1.0 bulb-cover mark worth 26 pts, which is what puts it on freesia's list at all. An Agave is an evergreen succulent rosette: it never leafs out, and its stiff spine-tipped leaves are not the herbaceous cover the technique needs. The sentence then contradicts itself by calling the plant evergreen. FIX (revised): the problem is habit, not evergreenness. Do NOT exclude evergreens from coversEphemeral() - hellebore, bergenia, epimedium and heuchera are evergreen and are the classic bulb covers. Exclude succulent/architectural rosettes (agave is tagged "succulent"), and reword the template so a persistent-foliage cover reads "its foliage is up in spring and screens the ripening bulb leaves" rather than "leafs out". DROP from the finding: the Blue Oat Grass claim (Helictotrichon sempervirens is a cool-season grass that does flush fresh growth in spring and is a legitimate bulb companion) and the "cannot lift freesia corms" claim (freesia is left in the ground in zones 9-10).

Scope is larger than the finding states and should be reported as such: the "leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage" clause appears 65 times across the pairing chunks. Blue Oat Grass alone serves it to 14 bulb subjects (amaryllis, anemone, crocus, daffodil, freesia, fritillaria/Crown Imperial, glory-of-the-snow, grape-hyacinth, hyacinth, ranunculus, scilla, snowflake, tulip, windflower), and Agave serves it to freesia, amaryllis and ranunculus. Root cause is two lines: CompatPlant.foliage = !bloomMonths.some(Boolean) (compatibility.ts:430) makes any bloom-less plant, including a succulent rosette, a "foliage cover" that earns the full mark, and the template at line 1086 is never suppressed for an evergreen even though line 1107 then calls the same plant evergreen in the same sentence.

If kept at all, keep it only as a copy/wording issue, not a botanical one, and not as an engine-logic bug: bulbCoverClause() (compatibility.ts:1082-1086) emits the single template "leafs out to hide the ripening bulb foliage as it dies back" even when the cover is evergreen or foliage-only (agave, blue oat grass), producing a self-contradictory sentence ("leafs out ... evergreen"). Fix is a one-line template branch - "its evergreen foliage screens the ripening bulb leaves" when cover.evergreen is true - NOT excluding evergreens from coversEphemeral(), which would remove horticulturally correct pairings the engine deliberately scores (compatibility.ts:677-690). The freesia -> agave and freesia -> blue-oat-grass pairings themselves are sound and should not be flagged as botanically questionable.

title: Pampas Grass is recommended to Gazania and Gerbera and praised for the wind-borne seed plumes that make it invasive
subject: gazania
claim: Shares full sun and low water; stands well above at 2.5 m, a canopy to plant beneath; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a Cal-IPC High-rated invasive and is listed/regulated as a pest plant in CA, OR, WA, HI and the TX Gulf Coast. A single female plume carries on the order of 100,000 wind-dispersed seeds that travel for miles. The reason sentence praises exactly the trait that makes it a problem ('its seed heads stand through winter'), with no caution, and it is being offered to two subjects whose zone bands (gazania 9-11, gerbera 8-11) are precisely the warm-winter states where it escapes. Its leaf blades are also razor-edged, which nobody should learn from a beginner app by accident. The library already knows how to say this: taxonomy.ts line 423 carries `miscanthus: { spreader: true }, // M. sinensis self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US`, yet line 424 is `cortaderia: {}, // confidence: high - no applicable traits`. So the far worse of the two grasses scores with zero penalty (61.6 for gazania, 48.1 for gerbera) while the milder one is down-weighted. The same gap applies to `hibiscus: {}` (Rose of Sharon, a prolific self-seeder listed invasive in several mid-Atlantic/Southeast states), offered to globeflower.
fix: Set `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts (matching the miscanthus precedent), and suppress the winter-seedhead praise clause for any genus flagged as a self-seeding spreader, or replace it with a caution ('seeds around freely; deadhead the plumes').
scope: Pampas Grass is an archetype, so it can be recommended to any full-sun, low-water, zone 7-11 plant in the library; it appears in 2 of the 10 subjects in this chunk alone. Maiden Grass (Miscanthus, flagged but still recommended to ginkgo with 'its seed heads stand through winter') and Rose of Sharon are the same class.
notes: Carry these corrections. (1) Tighten the regulatory sentence: Cal-IPC's "High" rating is an advisory inventory, not a regulation, and CDFA does not list C. selloana as a noxious weed. Accurate wording: "Cal-IPC High in California, a listed noxious weed in Hawaii and Oregon, and widely naturalized/invasive on the Texas Gulf Coast and through the Southeast." The repo's own invasive-recount.json already phrases it well: "Invasive in CA, HI, TX and the Southeast; a listed noxious weed in several counties. Also physically hazardous: the leaf margins cut skin." (2) DROP the hibiscus parenthetical. I checked the globeflower packet: globeflower -> rose-of-sharon (50.8) does exist, but its reason praises pollinator forage and height layering, not self-seeding, so it is not the same "praised for the invasive trait" defect; and the implied genus-level fix `hibiscus: { spreader: true }` would wrongly penalize Hardy Hibiscus (Hibiscus moscheutos), a US native, and Tropical Hibiscus. H. syriacus is the self-seeder, the genus is not. Cortaderia is safe to flag at genus level only because all four library entries are C. selloana. (3) State the real magnitude: this is not two subjects. invasive-recount.json shows pampas-grass appearing in 191 companion lists, praised in all 191, cautioned in zero, and the same praise-without-caution pattern hits maiden-grass (68 lists, 68 praised), butterfly-bush (42), and Vinca minor (4). Gazania (9-11) and gerbera (8-11) are the sharpest examples because their zone bands sit exactly in the escape range.

Keep the gazania (61.6, no penalties) and gerbera (48.1) pairings and the taxonomy.ts:423/424 miscanthus-vs-cortaderia contrast - all verified verbatim. Strengthen with data/invasive-recount.json: pampas-grass appears on 191 companion lists, 0 cautioned, 191 praised, praise phrase "its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds". DROP the `hibiscus: {}` / globeflower -> rose-of-sharon tail: taxonomy.ts:148 is actually `hibiscus: { pruneClass: "summer-new-wood", pollinator: "some" }` and that pairing's reason praises nothing invasive. SOFTEN "no caution" to "no caution on this surface": the plant's own curated card (src/garden/intel/curated/pampas-grass.json) does say "Invasive; check local lists ... Cal-IPC rates it 'High'", which the compatibility engine never consults - so the library already holds the fact and the recommender contradicts it.

Two corrections before this goes in the report. (1) Trim the state-regulation list: Cal-IPC rates Cortaderia selloana High and it is invasive in coastal CA, HI, the TX Gulf Coast and the Southeast, but the hard ODA/WSDA noxious-weed listings attach primarily to C. jubata, not selloana. The repo's own invasive-recount.json "why" field already words it defensibly: "Invasive in CA, HI, TX and the Southeast; a listed noxious weed in several counties." (2) Drop or narrow the hibiscus / rose-of-sharon add-on: the globeflower -> rose-of-sharon reason (score 50.8) praises pollinator forage and height, NOT the seeding trait, so the "praised for the very trait" argument does not apply; and because taxonomy.ts is genus-keyed, hibiscus: { spreader: true } would wrongly penalise H. moscheutos and H. rosa-sinensis, which are not weedy self-seeders. No such caveat applies to cortaderia (selloana and jubata are both invasive, no reliably sterile cultivar is in commerce, so a genus-wide flag is correct and correctly also catches the pink/gold/dwarf cultivar slugs). Additions worth keeping: the praise clause "for the birds" is separately false here - Cortaderia is wind-dispersed and its seed is not meaningful bird forage - and the 191-lists / 0-cautions count from invasive-recount.json is the strongest single piece of evidence, so cite it. Also note the spreader flag alone only yields the lateral-crowding caution ("this one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time"), which still does not tell the truth about seeding, so the second half of the fix (suppress or replace the winter-seedhead praise clause for self-seeding genera) is the part that actually removes the harm.

title: The colour clause names the wrong colour: Goatsbeard's cream-white flowers are called 'the orange', and an orange Asiatic Lily is called 'pink'
subject: goatsbeard
