claim: Shares part shade and moderate water; opens in early spring and overlaps into this one's season; lower at 0.2 m, for the front of the border; a soft colour beside the orange.
why: Aruncus dioicus has creamy-white plumes; the library records that correctly (bloom hex #f5f0e8, tag 'white'). But `colorName()` in src/browse/plantFacets.ts only calls a hex white when `l >= 0.82 && s < 0.25`, and the HSL saturation formula returns s = 0.39 for that near-white cream, so it falls through to `h < 45 -> 'orange'`. Goatsbeard's two colour-bearing reasons (to pansy and to azalea) therefore tell the user its flowers are orange. The same function's `h < 16 && l > 0.55 -> 'pink'` rule turns the vivid orange-red Asiatic Lily (#e8643c, tag 'orange') into 'pink', producing 'pink against blue, for contrast' on glory-of-the-snow -> lily-asiatic, and it renders Switchgrass as 'pink' on geranium-annual -> switchgrass. I ran the classifier over the whole library: 421 entries get a derived colour that contradicts the plant's own colour tag, and the pattern is systematic (every off-white hex like #f4f2ea becomes 'yellow', every warm yellow like black-eyed Susan's #f4b400 becomes 'orange'). The reason then contradicts the colour swatch and colour tag shown on the very same plant card, which for the north-star user is the app plainly being wrong about something she can see.
fix: In src/browse/plantFacets.ts, gate the neutral/white test on chroma (max-min < ~0.10 -> white/grey) instead of the HSL saturation term, and move the pink/red split off raw lightness (require hue 330-360 or a lightness above ~0.65 with saturation below ~0.6). As a belt-and-braces step, drop the colour clause whenever the derived name is absent from the plant's own colour tags.
scope: 421 of the library's single-span bloom entries derive a colour name that contradicts their own colour tag; every colour clause on those plants is wrong.
notes: Survives on its two headline cases. Keep goatsbeard -> pansy / -> azalea ("a soft colour beside the orange" for a cream-white Aruncus) and glory-of-the-snow -> lily-asiatic ("pink against blue" for an orange-tagged #e8643c lily); drop the Switchgrass and Black-Eyed Susan examples, which are defensible colour calls, and keep the scale claim but state it as "over 350 library blooms derive a colour that contradicts their own colour tag" (my count: 354 of 1,440 parsed). Note the engine already detects the cream correctly as a neutral (isNeutralBloom, l > 0.85, compatibility.ts:823) and then names it "orange" anyway, which makes the fix cheap. Severity lowered to medium: the clause is botanically false and visibly contradicts the card's swatch, but colour is the 5-point factor and the pairing itself (part shade, moderate water, spring-into-summer succession, 1.2 m over 0.2 m) is sound, so no plant is lost.

Two quote-level corrections to carry into the report, neither affecting the substance: (1) the finding's "claim" field misquotes the opening clause - the real goatsbeard -> pansy reason begins "Takes part sun to part shade and moderate water" (the "Shares part shade..." wording belongs to goatsbeard -> primrose); the colour clause "a soft colour beside the orange" is verbatim. (2) The switchgrass example fires the ANALOGOUS template, not a contrast one: geranium-annual -> switchgrass reads "pink sits close to red on the wheel" (switchgrass = the "pink"). Also, my independent recount of tag-contradicting entries came to ~354 of 1,362 tagged plants rather than 421 (the finding's number likely counts multi-window blooms); cite it as "hundreds" or re-derive rather than stating 421 as exact. Severity holds at high: the defect is systematic, the false clause sits next to the contradicting colour swatch and colour tag on the same card, and colour scheming is precisely what a beginner uses this advice for.

Downgrade high -> medium: no pairing is horticulturally wrong and no plant dies; the harm is a wrong colour word sitting next to a swatch and tag the user can see on the same card, plus colour-scheme decisions taken on false information (planting a vivid orange-red #e8643c Asiatic lily among blue glory-of-the-snow because the app called it pink). Trust/aesthetics, not survival. Two corrections to carry into the report: (1) DROP the Switchgrass example - Panicum virgatum panicles genuinely read pink/mauve, so "pink" for #dcae9e is defensible, and its library tag is "green", not a colour it contradicts; the strong evidence is goatsbeard -> pansy / goatsbeard -> azalea ("a soft colour beside the orange" where neither plant is orange) and glory-of-the-snow -> lily-asiatic. (2) Note the blast radius of the proposed fix: colorName() also feeds plantColors(), used by the Browse colour filter (src/browse/BrowseTab.tsx:176) and src/shared/PlantLibraryModal.tsx:201, so white daffodils, tulips and crocus currently also surface under the YELLOW filter - rewriting the classifier changes Browse, not just the reasons. The contained, safe half of the fix is the belt-and-braces one: suppress the colour clause whenever the derived name is absent from the plant's own colour tags.

title: Full-sun, drought-loving subjects are told to plant themselves under a canopy: Gazania beneath a 3 m Lilac, Gazania and Gerbera beneath a 2.5 m Pampas Grass
subject: gazania
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: `layeringClause` (compatibility.ts ~line 1066) emits 'a canopy to plant beneath' purely on a height ratio > 6 and a tier check, with no test of whether the SUBJECT tolerates shade. The result is a sentence that contradicts itself: it opens with 'shares full sun' and closes by instructing the user to plant a full-sun, heat-loving, drought-tolerant gazania in the dry shade under a lilac. Dry shade beneath an established lilac is one of the worst positions in a garden, and a gazania planted there will stretch, refuse to open its flowers (they close in low light by nature) and rot. The same clause fires for gerbera -> pampas-grass and gazania -> pampas-grass. A beginner reading 'a canopy to plant beneath' will do exactly that.
fix: Only emit the canopy/underplanting wording when the subject's light need is part-shade or shadier (or the companion is deciduous and the subject is a spring ephemeral). For a full-sun subject, fall back to 'taller at 3 m, for the layer behind it' so it reads as backdrop, not overhead cover.
scope: Any full-sun subject paired with a companion more than 6x its height; 3 pairings in this chunk (gazania -> lilac, gazania -> pampas-grass, gerbera -> pampas-grass).
notes: Two corrections. (1) Drop "and rot" from the reasoning: under a lilac the position is DRY shade, so the real failure mode is etiolation and refusal to flower, not rot - the rot claim is an incidental overstatement that invites rebuttal. (2) For the two pampas-grass pairs the clause is wrong for a sharper reason worth stating: Cortaderia selloana's 2.5 m is plume height over a dense, razor-edged basal clump, so nothing can be underplanted beneath it at all - "a canopy to plant beneath" is physically false there, not merely a light conflict. This means the fix must gate on more than the subject's shade tolerance (a clump-forming grass is never a canopy, the same way the code already exempts Climber). Also note the gate tierOf(max) >= 2 is loose enough that the canopy/carpet wording fires over 1 m perennials (e.g. crocus -> echinops), despite the code comment claiming it guards against exactly that.

Scope is larger than the finding states and worth mentioning in the report: 241 pairings in the corpus carry the "canopy to plant beneath" clause, 171 of them with a full-sun subject and 85 with a full-sun AND low-water subject. Other clear victims: aloe -> pampas-grass, blue-fescue -> lilac, california-poppy -> pampas-grass, dianthus -> pampas-grass, dusty-miller -> pampas-grass, candytuft -> smokebush. The proposed fix (gate the canopy/underplanting wording on the subject's light need being part-shade or shadier, else fall back to "taller at X m, for the layer behind it") fixes all of them at one site.

Trim the unsupported "and rot" claim; the defensible harm is a stretched gazania whose flowers never open. Two corrections that must reach the report: (1) the same defect ships in the MIRROR branch of layeringClause - pampas-grass -> gazania reads "low at 0.25 m, to carpet the ground beneath", and 47 pairings tell the user to carpet the ground under a >=2.5 m subject with a full-sun companion (crabapple -> zinnia, crape-myrtle -> lavender, blue-spruce -> ninebark, arborvitae -> bee-balm), so gating only the "canopy" string leaves the bad advice standing. (2) A related lower-severity smell in the same clause: the canopy wording fires for companions as short as 1 m (colchicum -> echinops, "stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath"), i.e. tallEnoughToShelter is too permissive independently of the sun problem. Scale: ~218 shipped reason strings across both branches.

title: Gertrude Jekyll (English rose) is told to carpet its base with Snow-in-Summer and Sea Thrift, two lean-soil alpines that rot in rose-bed conditions
subject: gertrude-jekyll
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; its white flowers settle the pink.
why: The one-rung water band (low vs moderate) hides a genuine cultural conflict. Cerastium tomentosum and Armeria maritima both demand lean, sharply drained, low-fertility soil and open air at the crown; both are grown for scree, walls and gravel. An English rose is the opposite regime: rich moisture-retentive soil, heavy feeding, mulch and regular irrigation. In that bed Cerastium goes lax, rots out from the centre in the first humid summer, and Armeria's crown collapses. The engine also knows Cerastium is an aggressive mat-former (`cerastium: { spreader: true }` in taxonomy.ts), but the spreader penalty is exempted because the rose is a ROBUST_CATEGORY, so the reason still tells the user to run that mat right up under the rose. Standard rose culture is the reverse: keep the crown and basal canes clear and mulched, not carpeted, so air moves and blackspot spores are not splashed up off a permanently damp mat.
fix: Either exclude mat-forming lean-soil alpines from 'carpet the ground beneath' clauses for Rose subjects (use 'lower at 0.15 m, for the front of the border' instead), or add a coarse fertility/drainage trait so scree plants and rich-bed feeders do not pair. The same claim is made for gertrude-jekyll -> sea-thrift and gladiolus -> sea-thrift.
scope: gertrude-jekyll -> snow-in-summer (58.2), gertrude-jekyll -> sea-thrift (57.9), gladiolus -> sea-thrift (54.2).
notes: Reframe from "these plants cannot live in a rose bed" to "the carpet-the-ground-beneath clause puts the wrong plants at the rose's crown". Verified: gertrude-jekyll -> snow-in-summer (58.2) and -> sea-thrift (57.9) both emit "to carpet the ground beneath" (height ratios 10.0 and 7.5, over CANOPY_RATIO 6); the spreader penalty is skipped for cerastium because Rose is in ROBUST_CATEGORIES. Two defensible sub-claims: (a) Cerastium tomentosum is a documented aggressive mat-former that goes lax in rich soil and rots where soil stays wet, so directing it up against the crown of a fed/irrigated/mulched English rose is poor advice - rose guidance keeps dry-lovers 12-36 in off the crown; (b) Armeria maritima makes discrete tufts, not a carpet, so the clause is factually wrong for sea-thrift regardless of culture. Drop from the report: the "lean-soil alpine + rose = rot" class argument (lavender, Dianthus and Stachys are the classic rose companions), the "first humid summer" claim (zone intersection is 5-7), the blackspot-splash argument (groundcover under roses is recommended as living mulch), and the gladiolus -> sea-thrift example (it emits "for the front of the border", not the carpet clause). The proposed fix - suppress the carpet clause for spreader/lean-soil groundcovers under a Rose subject and use "lower at X m, for the front of the border" - remains correct.

Drop "and gladiolus -> sea-thrift" from the proposed fix: that pair's reason reads "lower at 0.2 m, for the front of the border" (ratio 1.2/0.2 = 6, not > CANOPY_RATIO 6), so it carries no carpet clause. The correct second instance is gertrude-jekyll -> sea-thrift, whose reason does read "low at 0.2 m, to carpet the ground beneath". Otherwise the finding is accurate as written: reason text verified verbatim in chunks/chunk-12.json, cerastium spreader:true at taxonomy.ts:277, Rose in ROBUST_CATEGORIES at compatibility.ts:155 suppressing the spreader penalty (penalties are ["water-differs"] only), carpet clause at compatibility.ts:1074-1078.

Recast the finding. Drop the "lean-soil alpines rot in rose-bed conditions" claim (unsupportable; both are ordinary sunny-border edgers and roses demand good drainage too) and drop sea-thrift from it (a 0.2 x 0.3 m tussock, not a mat, correctly not flagged a spreader). Keep only this: for gertrude-jekyll -> snow-in-summer the reason says "low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath" while the engine's own taxonomy flags cerastium spreader: true, and the "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging" caution is suppressed because "Rose" is in ROBUST_CATEGORIES (compatibility.ts:155, 517-519, 1190-1193) - so the one pairing that instructs a mat at a rose's crown is the one that loses the spreader warning the engine already has. Secondary: layeringClause (1077-1079) applies "to carpet the ground beneath" on height ratio alone, so narrow tussocks like sea thrift (0.3 m spread) are described as ground carpets in gertrude-jekyll -> sea-thrift and gladiolus -> sea-thrift. Fix is at the reason layer: gate the "carpet" wording on real spread/mat-forming, and let the spreader caution through when the reason itself tells the user to plant the spreader as a carpet.

title: Crocosmia is recommended to Globeflower, a bog plant: the corms rot in the permanently wet soil Trollius requires
subject: globeflower
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; blooms in midsummer as this one finishes; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: Trollius is filed correctly as water: high; it is a wet-meadow and waterside plant that must never dry out and is normally planted in heavy, damp, cool soil. Crocosmia is a corm (the library's own taxonomy flags it `tenderBulb: true`) and every source qualifies it as 'moist but WELL-DRAINED'; corms sitting in cold, saturated winter soil rot, which is the single most common way gardeners lose them in zones 5-6, exactly where this pair's zone overlap sits (5-7). The engine's water ladder treats moderate-vs-high as one rung and only applies a 0.92 multiplier, so the pair scores 49.3 and appears as a recommendation. A beginner following it will plant crocosmia corms into a bog and lose them over the first winter.
fix: Treat a high-water subject as a wet-soil site and exclude corm/tender-bulb companions from it (a `tenderBulb` plant should not pass coexistence against water: high), rather than only down-weighting by 0.92.
scope: globeflower -> crocosmia; the same water-band leniency puts every well-drained-only bulb in reach of the library's bog perennials.
refutations: All the finding's data is accurate (pairing confirmed at 49.3 with matching reason and water-differs penalty; Trollius water:high zones 3-7; Crocosmia water:moderate zones 5-9 tenderBulb:true), but the medium harm does not hold up. The load-bearing premise -- "Trollius requires permanently wet/bog soil where corms rot" -- is overstated: RHS files garden Trollius (x cultorum) as "moist but well-drained / poorly drained" and MBG says it grows in ordinary borders kept uniformly moist, with bog only optional. Crocosmia's own RHS moisture is likewise "moist but well-drained," so both plants share a soil-moisture descriptor and coexist in the realistic planting a beginner does (a moist border). Crocosmia is fully hardy in-ground in zone 7 (warm half of the 5-7 overlap); the corm-rot hazard is confined to zone 5-6 with genuinely waterlogged winter soil -- a narrow edge a gardener building a bog would knowingly avoid. The pairing is the lowest-ranked of 8 companions, one-directional (crocosmia's card does not recommend globeflower back), the reason already flags the water difference ("moderate to high water"), and the auditor's own bog-vs-xeric/xeric-vs-wetter lenses correctly did NOT flag it because moderate-vs-high is the mild, usually-fine case. Real harm requires the gardener to overread "high water" as "bog" AND be in the cold half of the overlap AND have waterlogged soil -- thin enough that this does not rise to a reportable medium.
notes: Two corrections. (1) Soften the Trollius claim: garden Trollius (T. x cultorum) does not require PERMANENTLY saturated soil - it requires soil that never dries and it tolerates/thrives in bog. The sharper, un-refutable framing is the internal contradiction: BloomsEye's own globeflower care copy (deepIntel/globeflower.json:499) tells the user to site it in "a pond or stream edge, a bog garden, a rain-garden basin", and the companion list then recommends a rot-prone corm whose own care copy (deepIntel/crocosmia.json:549) says "never waterlogged; avoid heavy clay and standing wet". (2) Add the supporting point that at the cold end of the pair's 5-7 zone overlap, crocosmia's own tenderBulb flag means the corms should be lifted for winter anyway, so the usable overlap is thinner than it looks. Severity stays medium: real harm to a beginner, but it is the lowest-scoring (49.3) of eight companions, not a headline recommendation.

Two precision fixes for the write-up. (1) Exposure: crocosmia ranks 8th of globeflower's 8 companions, so it is not in the 3-item preview and only appears after the user taps "Show all 8 companions" - real but limited reach, which is why medium (not high) is right. (2) Phrase the hazard as winter/standing waterlogging rotting the corm in the wet, poorly drained siting Trollius wants, not as "any high-water neighbour kills crocosmia" (crocosmia does want even moisture in growth; it is the "moist but WELL-DRAINED" half that the pairing breaks). Strongest citation available is in-repo, not external: src/garden/intel/deepIntel/globeflower.json ("thrives in boggy, poorly drained ground... Excellent for bog gardens, moist meadows, streamsides, and rain-garden basins") versus src/garden/intel/deepIntel/crocosmia.json ("intolerant of wet/waterlogged and clay soils; Clemson stresses good drainage" / "never waterlogged... avoid heavy clay and standing wet"). Quote those two lines side by side - the product's own research contradicts its own recommendation.

If kept at all, this is at most a low-severity edge case, and the reason should be reframed: a genuine but narrow winter-wet corm-rot risk only when Trollius is used as an obligate bog/pond-margin planting in zones 5-6. In the common case (moist border, or zone 7) the pair is compatible -- both are RHS "moist but well-drained." The finding's "will plant crocosmia into a bog and lose them" framing is not the default outcome of following the advice.

title: Colchicum is offered to Gladiolus as a 0.15 m carpet, but it is bare ground for most of the year and its spring leaf clump is twice that height
subject: gladiolus
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in early fall as this one finishes; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath.
why: The library records Colchicum at h 0.15 / w 0.10, which is the naked autumn FLOWER only. Colchicum's actual habit is the reverse of a carpet: a coarse leaf clump 0.25 to 0.35 m tall and 0.30 m or more wide appears in spring, dies away messily by early summer, and the ground is then bare until the flowers push up in September. So the clause is false twice over: it does not carpet anything (no foliage for most of the season, and no foliage at all when it flowers), and its spring leaves are the tallest thing in the front of that border, right when the gladiolus corms are pushing. Worth noting alongside: Colchicum is one of the most acutely poisonous plants in the catalog (colchicine, no antidote, corms regularly mistaken for edible bulbs), and the recommendation carries no caution.
fix: Record Colchicum's height/spread from its spring foliage (h ~0.30, w ~0.30) rather than its flower, so it stops qualifying for 'carpet the ground beneath' and front-of-border clauses; and add a toxicity note for genera in the top toxicity tier.
scope: colchicum is an archetype with 6 variants, all recorded at flower height (0.15-0.28 m); any ground-cover or front-of-border clause generated for it is wrong.
refutations: The pairing is horticulturally sound and beneficial, so no gardener is harmed. Gladiolus and Colchicum share full sun, moderate water, and overlapping zones 7-9, and the reason's dominant clause (fall bloom succession after glads finish) is correct and genuinely useful. Because colchicum leaves die by early summer, before glads reach 1.2 m, and colchicum flowers only after glads are gone, the two occupy the front space at different times with no shading/competition conflict; worst case is mild aesthetic overpromise, not a plant-killing mistake. The "carpet the ground beneath" wording is a GENERIC layering template (compatibility.ts layeringClause) fired for any low companion under a tier-2+ plant, not a colchicum-specific groundcover claim, so it is a global wording nit rather than a named-pair defect. The 0.15 m value follows the library's consistent bulb=flowering-height convention (crocus 0.10, muscari 0.20, scilla 0.15); colchicum's temporally-separated oversized foliage is a real exception but does not break THIS recommendation. Toxicity is a plant-level attribute identical across every pairing (gladiolus corms are toxic too) that the finding itself relegates to a side note, so it does not make this specific pair wrong.
notes: Core defect is the "carpet the ground beneath" clause plus the underlying data error (flower height recorded as plant height for the whole Colchicum group). Correctly a wrong-data/wrong-reason finding, not a thrive conflict, so medium is well-calibrated. The border-timing sub-claim ("tallest thing... when gladiolus corms are pushing") is somewhat speculative and could be dropped without weakening the finding; the toxicity observation is valid but secondary.

Load-bearing part is the flower-only height (0.15 m) misrepresenting the plant and producing a false 'carpet the ground beneath' clause across all gladiolus variants; recommend the proposed fix (record foliage-based h~0.30/w~0.30). Note for the report: the pair itself is culturally fine (not a thrive conflict), and the colchicine-toxicity remark is a valid but out-of-scope aside, so keep the finding centered on the wrong height data rather than the pairing being unplantable.

Botanically the observations are accurate: Colchicum flowers leafless in autumn and its spring leaf clump (~0.25-0.35 m) is larger than the 0.15 m flower the library records, so 0.15 m understates its spring footprint and "carpet the ground beneath" slightly overstates a groundcover effect it does not deliver. This is a legitimate but minor data/wording nuance, not a medium wrong-data finding: the pairing itself remains valid and delivers a real season-extension benefit, and the same overstated "carpet"/"front of the border" template wording applies library-wide to any low bulb.

title: Butterfly Bush is recommended to Grape Hyacinth and praised for pollinator forage, with no invasiveness caution and no spreader penalty
subject: grape-hyacinth
claim: Shares full sun and low water; blooms in early summer, extending the bed's season; stands well above at 2 m, a canopy to plant beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months.
why: Buddleia davidii is a quarantine-listed noxious weed in Oregon (only approved low-seed cultivars may be sold), a Class B noxious weed in Washington, and is on invasive lists across the Pacific Northwest, mid-Atlantic and parts of the Southeast; a single shrub throws hundreds of thousands of wind-borne seeds into river corridors. The reason praises it for exactly the trait that sells it and drives its escape (nectar forage), and it is a nectar trap: an adult-butterfly feeding station that hosts no North American larvae. The library is inconsistent here: taxonomy.ts:423 flags miscanthus `spreader: true` with the comment 'invasive in much of the US', while taxonomy.ts:145-146 gives buddleia/buddleja `pollinator: "high"` and no `spreader` flag at all, so the pairing takes zero penalty (penalties: []) and cautionClause() never fires. A beginner is told to plant it and told it is good for bees.
fix: Add `spreader: true` to the `buddleia`/`buddleja` rows in taxonomy.ts so the x0.8 penalty and the 'a vigorous spreader, so give it room' caution clause fire; better, add an explicit invasive flag that appends a real caution ('invasive in much of the US: plant only a seedless cultivar and deadhead') to any reason that recommends it.
scope: Every card that offers butterfly-bush as a companion, not just grape-hyacinth; it is an archetype with pollinator: high, so it will surface widely on full-sun/low-water cards.
notes: Finding survives as-is; keep at high (consistent with the audit flagging the less-regulated Miscanthus/Vinca as high). One refinement to the proposed fix for the report: adding spreader:true would not merely penalize this pair, it would HARD-BLOCK it (buddleia at 2 m overtops the 0.2 m Muscari, so spreaderSmothers fires and drops the pairing), and buddleia spreads by seed rather than vegetatively, so the generic "give it room or an edging" caution is the wrong mechanism. The explicit-invasive-flag fix the finding already prefers (append a real caution: "invasive in much of the US: plant only a seedless cultivar and deadhead") is the horticulturally correct remedy. Note also this is not a one-off: butterfly-bush is recommended in 42 lists (archetype-standing.json), so the problem recurs across the catalog with grape-hyacinth as a representative pair.

Finding is fully verified. One caveat on the proposed fix only (not the finding): the engine's `spreader` mechanic models a rhizomatous plant smothering a delicate in-bed neighbor, not the seed-dispersal escape that actually makes Buddleia invasive, so adding `spreader: true` would fire a penalty but the caution wording ("give it room or an edging") wouldn't convey the real risk, and for this specific 0.2 m bulb vs 2 m shrub pair the neighbor-smother penalty may not even trigger. The finding acknowledges this and recommends an explicit invasive flag as the better fix. Core issue stands regardless: a beginner viewing Grape Hyacinth is told to plant a regionally noxious weed and praised for the exact nectar trait that drives its escape, with no caution.

Carry two corrections into the report (verdict unchanged). (1) The PROPOSED FIX is wrong and must be rewritten: adding `spreader: true` to buddleia would DELETE this pair, not caution it - grape-hyacinth is a 0.2 m Bulb and 0.2 <= OVERTOPPED_HEIGHT_FRACTION(0.6) x 2 m, so spreaderSmothers fires the HARD blocker "spreader-smothers" (compatibility.ts:511-512, 564) and buddleia drops from every short-plant list; `spreader` also mismodels the hazard (in-bed vegetative smothering, not wind-borne seed escape). Lead with the finding's second remedy instead - an explicit invasive/regulated flag that emits its own caution clause, and/or wire the already-curated noxious-weed fact onto the companion card. (2) Wording: the reason praises the SELLING trait (nectar/pollinator forage), not literally the escape trait (prolific wind-borne seed); state it as "praised as pollinator forage, the trait that sells it, with no mention of the seed that makes it escape." Also soften "hosts no North American larvae" to "essentially no," and note the app is not wholly silent - curated/butterfly-bush.json already carries the sourced "Regulated noxious weed" fact and the "no native caterpillars" note on butterfly-bush's own plant card, so the fix is to propagate existing curated data, not invent new data. Systemic scale worth citing: butterfly-bush 42 lists / 0 cautioned, pampas-grass 191, maiden-grass 68.

title: All 8 of Hens and Chicks' companions tell the beginner to plant a full-sun succulent BENEATH a 1 m canopy
subject: hens-and-chicks
claim: Shares full sun and low water; stands well above at 1.2 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: Sempervivum is an alpine rosette succulent that needs full, direct sun and sharp drainage; in shade it etiolates, loses its colour and rots, and it is killed by the leaf litter and drip line that fall under a taller neighbour. Every one of the eight companions (cosmos, mullein, russian sage, red hot poker, cleome, asiatic lily, spirea, amaranth) is 1 to 1.2 m and every reason ends with the identical clause 'stands well above at 1 m/1.2 m, a canopy to plant beneath'. layeringClause() (compatibility.ts:1063-1067) emits that phrasing on height ratio and tier alone; nothing checks whether the SHORT plant can tolerate being shaded. For a full-sun plant the instruction is inverted: it belongs in front of these plants, not under them. Spirea (1 m tall, 1.2 m spread) would physically smother a 0.1 m rosette. Because Sempervivum is foliage-only, succession and colour drop out, so four of the eight reasons are literally two clauses long and carry no other content.
fix: In layeringClause(), suppress the 'a canopy to plant beneath' / 'carpet the ground beneath' phrasing when the shorter plant is full-sun (or category succulent) and fall back to 'for the front of the border' / 'for the layer behind it'. The same clause is wrong on gomphrena -> lilac ('stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath' for a heat-loving full-sun annual).
scope: All 8 hens-and-chicks pairings; systemically, any full-sun ground-tier subject paired with a much taller plant (also seen on gomphrena).
notes: Correct the count: it is not "four of the eight" but ALL EIGHT reasons that are only two clauses (conditions + the inverted canopy clause), because Sempervivum's blank bloom field zeroes out succession and colour for every companion. Also note the pairs themselves are horticulturally compatible (xeric full-sun plants like russian sage + sempervivum are a classic combination); the defect is purely the REASON's inverted "plant beneath" placement, correctly a wrong-reason finding. The layeringClause defect generalizes to any short full-sun plant (gomphrena -> lilac verified: "stands well above at 3 m, a canopy to plant beneath").

Confirmed against data and code. Downgrade high -> medium: the pairings are horticulturally sound (full-sun, low/moderate water — sempervivum truly belongs beside russian sage/red hot poker in a gravel bed); the defect is the placement clause, not plant compatibility. Keep the finding but reframe: layeringClause() at compatibility.ts:1063-1067 recommends underplanting on height ratio + tier alone, so it hands a full-sun, shade-intolerant succulent (and any low full-sun groundcover) the inverted instruction "a canopy to plant beneath" on 100% of its companions. Fix as proposed: when the shorter plant is full-sun (or category succulent / drought-tolerant foliage), suppress "a canopy to plant beneath" / "carpet the ground beneath" and fall back to "for the front of the border" / "for the layer behind it." Same clause misfires on gomphrena -> lilac (3 m). Note "canopy" also overstates airy 1 m companions (russian sage, cosmos); it is genuinely wrong only for the dense ones (spirea 1.2 m spread, lilac 3 m).

Verified factually: all 8 hens-and-chicks companions (cosmos, mullein, russian-sage, red-hot-poker, cleome, lily-asiatic, spirea, amaranth) carry the identical inverted clause; hens-and-chicks library data (full-sun/low/succulent/foliage, h=0.1) is correct, so the pairing selection is fine and the defect is the placement clause, not the pairs. Note that in fact ALL 8 reasons are two-clause (foliage-only kills succession/colour), not just four. Severity lowered from high to medium: the pairs can coexist if placed correctly, so this is a systemic wrong-reason/placement-inversion defect (also affecting gomphrena->lilac and, per the template, any short full-sun plant beneath a >6x taller neighbour) rather than a cannot-coexist or invasive-recommendation issue; its scope (whole companion list + general clause) and the succulent-specific rot/etiolation/smothering risk keep it well above low. Proposed fix (suppress "canopy to plant beneath" when the shorter plant is full-sun or a succulent, fall back to "for the front of the border") is sound.

title: Hellebore -> Summersweet: a well-drained, lime-loving plant paired with an acid bog shrub that suckers into a colony
subject: hellebore
claim: Shares part shade and moderate to high water; blooms in midsummer, extending the bed's season; both offer pollinator forage, in different months; taller at 1.8 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Clethra alnifolia is a streambank shrub that needs consistently moist to wet, acidic soil (about pH 5 to 6) and spreads by root suckers into a thicket. Helleborus wants humus-rich but sharply drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil, rots at the crown in wet ground, and strongly resents root disturbance and competition at the crown. One spot, one soil, one watering schedule cannot serve both: whichever regime the beginner picks, one plant is being grown wrong, and the suckering Clethra colony will close over the hellebore crowns in a few seasons. The engine cannot see this because pH is unmodelled and the 'moderate' vs 'high' water gap only costs 8%; note the library also gives clethra no spreader flag despite its colonising habit.
fix: Drop the pairing (or block a bog/acid-obligate shrub against a plant whose intel calls for sharp drainage). At minimum, add `spreader: true` to the `clethra` row so the caution clause fires next to a slow, clump-forming neighbour.
scope: hellebore -> summersweet (score 65, 5th on the card); summersweet is also offered to holly-fern where the same acid/wet clash with a shade fern is milder.
refutations: The finding's botanical facts check out against the repo's own deepIntel (hellebore: well-drained, neutral-alkaline pH ~7-8, won't sit in wet ground; summersweet: moist/wet, acidic pH ~4.5-6.5, suckering; clethra genuinely lacks a spreader flag in taxonomy.ts). The pairing is really shown (companion #5 of 8, score 65). But under the MATTERS lens it does not harm a gardener, and the finding overstates the case: (1) Both are humus-rich, part-shade woodland-edge plants. A moist-but-well-drained, organic, pH ~6-6.5 woodland border satisfies both — consistent moisture and sharp drainage are not contradictory in organic soil, and that pH is inside hellebore's tolerance and near summersweet's. "One spot cannot serve both" is false in the plants' shared niche. (2) In the actual recommendation direction (add summersweet to an existing, already well-drained hellebore bed), summersweet adapts fine; the hellebore-rots-in-bog scenario requires siting the hellebore in wet ground, which this direction never does. (3) The reason text is accurate, not misleading: bloom succession (hellebore Feb-Apr -> summersweet Jul-Aug) is real and valuable, both are genuine pollinators blooming in different months, and 1.8m shrub behind a 0.4m perennial is correct layering. (4) Clethra is a slow woody suckerer, not the herbaceous-thug class the spreader flag is reserved for (mint/vinca/ajuga/lysimachia); not flagging it is defensible. The proposed fix also misfires mechanically: because hellebore (0.4m) is overtopped by clethra (0.4 <= 0.6*1.8), adding spreader:true would trigger the spreader-smothers HARD BLOCKER, killing the pairing entirely and false-positiving on normal shrub-over-front-perennial layering, not merely adding a caution. This is a defensible woodland-border combination whose on-paper conflicts dissolve in the shared niche.
notes: Confirmed named pairing hellebore -> summersweet (chunk-13.json; score 65). Core defect is a genuine soil-chemistry + drainage conflict the engine's coarse water field hides, corroborated by the app's own deepIntel: Clethra alnifolia wants moist-to-wet acidic soil (pH 4.5-6.5, chlorotic in alkaline) while Helleborus wants sharply-drained neutral-to-alkaline soil (pH 7.0-8.0) and rots at the crown in soggy ground; the two pH ranges do not overlap. Reason ("moderate to high water") implies a shared happy medium that does not exist for a beginner picking one regime. Corrections: soften the suckering claim - Clethra suckers slowly, it will not "close over the hellebore crowns in a few seasons," so make the missing spreader flag a secondary note not the lead; and "lime-loving" should read "neutral-to-alkaline-preferring / lime-tolerant." Data claims that check out: water-differs = 8% (0.92); summersweet's aggressiveSpreader is indeed unset despite the curated intel documenting its colony-forming habit.

All data claims verified exactly (reason text verbatim; water moderate/high; both part-shade; 1.8 m; 0.92 water penalty; clethra unflagged as spreader at taxonomy.ts:150). The proposed fix is sound and targets the right place - taxonomy.ts GENUS_TRAITS clethra entry (line 150), where a `spreader: true` would set aggressiveSpreader and fire the caution clause, exactly like monarda/mentha. Downgrade to low: the moisture/pH divergence is genuine and unmodelled, but hellebore and summersweet are both classic moist part-shade woodland plants that coexist in a common humus-rich bed; hellebores tolerate acidic woodland soil (not strictly lime-loving), and clethra's suckering is slow against a long-lived hellebore clump, so the "colony closes over the crowns in a few seasons" framing is overstated. Legitimate as a missing-caution / borderline-pairing note, not a plants-cannot-coexist failure.

Residual worth at most a minor note: the on-paper pH gap (hellebore ~7-8 vs summersweet ~4.5-6.5) is one of the wider ones and the reason carries no caution. But both thrive in the shared humus-rich, moist-but-well-drained, part-shade woodland niche at ~pH 6.5, the recommendation direction (add summersweet to an existing well-drained hellebore bed) avoids the rot scenario, and the reason's positive claims (bloom succession, pollinator forage, height layering) are all true. Do NOT adopt the proposed spreader:true fix: per the engine it would hard-block via spreader-smothers (hellebore is overtopped) and misfire on normal shrub+front-perennial layering; clethra is a slow woody suckerer, not the herbaceous-thug spreader class.

title: Foliage-only subjects (Eastern Hemlock, Holly Fern) produce reasons that state no benefit at all
subject: hemlock
claim: Takes part sun to part shade and moderate water.
why: That sentence is the whole reason: it restates the hard filter the pair had to pass and names zero benefit. The beginner is told nothing about why Beautyberry belongs with a hemlock. The mechanism is exact and reproducible: hemlock has no bloom, so succession and colour are dropped; callicarpa is not flagged native, so nativeScore = 0.4 against a CLAUSE_FLOOR of 1; and layeringScore for a tier-3 canopy tapers to 0.471 at a 10:1 ratio, just under the layering floor of 0.5 (compatibility.ts:646-652, 254-262). So every clause is filtered out. The same collapse produces holly-fern -> dogwood-tree ('Takes part shade to full shade and moderate water.') and five of hemlock's eight reasons, which are all the identical near-empty 'Shares part shade and moderate water; both are native plants.'
fix: Either lower the layering floor for canopy pairs so the true 'shorter at X, an understory layer' clause survives, or refuse to publish any companion whose reason contains no clause beyond the conditions clause. Foliage-only subjects also need a factor they can actually score (foliage/texture contrast, evergreen backbone) or their cards will always read as filler.
scope: hemlock (6 of 8 reasons carry no real benefit; beautyberry carries none), holly-fern -> dogwood-tree; systemic for every foliage-only subject paired with a tree.
refutations:
The mechanism is real and accurately described (hemlock->beautyberry does render "Takes part sun to part shade and moderate water." with no benefit clause; the 0.471 layering taper just under the 0.5 floor and beautyberry's non-native archetype both check out). But under the MATTERS lens the finding does not survive as written, for three reasons.

(1) No gardener is harmed. Both title pairings are botanically SOUND and the reasons are TRUE. Eastern hemlock + beautyberry (both part-shade, moderate-water woodland plants, zones intersect 5-7; purple berries against dark evergreen) is a combination a horticulturist would happily recommend. Holly fern + dogwood is likewise fine. A conditions-only reason is thin, not wrong or misleading; for a beginner it still delivers the load-bearing fact of companion planting ("these two thrive in the same spot"). Nobody plants the wrong thing.

(2) The finding's central factual claim, that foliage subjects "state no benefit at all" and "will always read as filler," is overstated to the point of being wrong. Of hemlock's 8 reasons, 5 carry a genuine benefit clause ("both are native plants" - a real ecological selling point, gated at native floor 1) and 1 (witch-hazel) carries a layering clause; only beautyberry is truly benefit-free. For holly-fern (the other named subject) 7 of 8 reasons carry a real layering clause; only dogwood-tree is bare. So the collapse the finding describes as systematic actually affects just 2 named pairs, not the class of foliage subjects. Calling the native-clause reasons "near-empty" mischaracterizes the data.

(3) The thin reason is the accepted, documented consequence of a deliberate safety policy, not a defect. The engine's clause floors exist precisely so "a reason never claims a benefit the pair barely has," and the header comment states outright that "an invented reason is worse than no reason." The engine would rather print a bare-but-true conditions reason than fabricate a texture/contrast clause it cannot ground. Printing the honest thin reason on a couple of sound pairings is that policy working as intended.

Residual kernel: there is a minor UX-polish opportunity (2 pairs show a conditions-only reason on a feature that promises a "why"), which is worth a low-severity note at most, but it does not rise to the medium, class-wide finding as framed, and the framing's factual overstatements make it a wrong finding rather than a missing one.
notes: Scope the finding to the truly zero-benefit reasons (conditions-only, no semicolon): hemlock -> beautyberry, holly-fern -> dogwood-tree, autumn-fern -> dogwood-tree, and the broader 44-pair reason-generic set dominated by evergreen/structural subjects (blue-spruce, douglas-fir, eastern-white-pine, italian-cypress, boxwood). Drop or soften the claim that hemlock's "both are native plants" reasons are near-empty filler: the native clause is a real, useful benefit to a beginner, so only 1 of hemlock's 8 companions (beautyberry) is actually benefit-free. Root cause is accurate and worth reporting: a foliage/evergreen SUBJECT has no factor it can score into a companion clause (winter structure is suppressed by the "clause about the wrong plant" rule; layering for tier-3 canopy tapers below its floor), so its cards default to a bare conditions sentence.

Two named pairs verified exactly: hemlock -> beautyberry and holly-fern -> dogwood-tree publish reasons that restate only the shared-conditions filter and name zero benefit. Mechanism confirmed against compatibility.ts and library data (hemlock 15m / beautyberry 1.5m = exact 10:1 -> layering 0.471 < 0.5 floor; native 0.4 < 1 floor; succession 0.5 < 0.55 floor). Correction to carry into the report: the "five identical reasons" claim is overstated - only 2 of hemlock's 8 reasons are byte-identical to the quoted string, and the ~6 conditions+native reasons DO state a (minor) native-pairing benefit, so they are not benefit-free. Frame the defect as "foliage-only subjects, and non-native companions of a native foliage subject, get all factor clauses filtered and publish a reason that merely echoes the hard filter" - hemlock->beautyberry and holly-fern->dogwood-tree are the clean examples. The pairings themselves are botanically fine; this is a weak-reason/presentation defect, not a bad recommendation.

If retained at all, narrow to the two genuinely benefit-free pairs (hemlock->beautyberry, holly-fern->dogwood-tree) and correct the thesis: most foliage-subject reasons DO carry a real benefit clause (native at floor 1, or a layering/understory clause). Holly-fern's reasons are 7/8 non-filler, so it is a poor example. The pairings themselves are sound and the reasons true; this is a cosmetic "reason is thin" polish item, not a botanical or misleading-advice problem, and the thin output is the accepted result of the engine's documented "an invented reason is worse than no reason" clause-floor policy.

title: The shared `hibiscus` genus row is written for Rose of Sharon, so native Hardy Hibiscus is scored non-native with only 'some' pollinator value
subject: hardy-hibiscus
claim: taxonomy.ts:148  hibiscus: { pruneClass: "summer-new-wood", pollinator: "some" }, // rose of sharon
why: Hibiscus moscheutos (rose mallow) is a native eastern-US wetland perennial and a high-value forage plant: it is the host of the specialist rose-mallow bee Ptilothrix bombiformis and is heavily worked by bumblebees. Because the genus row is authored for the Asian shrub Hibiscus syriacus, moscheutos inherits `pollinator: "some"` and no `native` flag, so it can never earn the native clause (floor 1.0 needs both plants native) and never earns the strong-forage wording. The effect is visible across its whole card: eight companions and not one reason mentions that this is a native plant or a strong pollinator plant, while weaker forage plants elsewhere in the chunk get 'both are strong pollinator plants'. Genus-level traits cannot serve a native marsh perennial and a self-seeding Asian shrub at once.
fix: Split the trait at the species level: give hardy-hibiscus (H. moscheutos) `native: true, pollinator: "high"`, and leave the rose-of-sharon row as it is (it in turn should carry a self-seeding caution).
scope: All 8 hardy-hibiscus pairings lose their native and strong-forage clauses; any other H. moscheutos cultivar in the library inherits the same wrong genus row.
refutations: Mechanism verified (hardy-hibiscus inherits genus-row pollinator:"some" and no native flag; all 8 companions get the weaker "both offer pollinator forage", none say native), and H. moscheutos genuinely is native, so the data gap is real. But it fails the MATTERS test on three grounds. (1) It is a false negative the engine DOCUMENTS as its deliberate accepted trade-off: compatibility.ts lines 368-371 and 728-730 state an absent taxonomy flag earns no bonus and no reason clause, by design, so the engine never over-claims. That is the lens's explicit disqualifier. (2) No gardener is harmed: every reason shown is truthful and every pairing is sound (e.g. Aster + hardy hibiscus). Under-crediting native (weight 8) and pollinator can only lower a positive score, never create a wrong pairing, so at worst the card under-sells a good plant, i.e. "could be more exciting", which is an excluded category. (3) The proposed fix would itself over-claim: the hardy-hibiscus GROUP is dominated by complex interspecific hybrids ('Midnight Marvel', 'Berry Awesome', 'Kopper King', etc.) that native-plant advocates do not call native, and "high" vs "some" for moscheutos is a defensible judgment call. The observation is true but immaterial and not safely fixable as proposed.
notes: Confirmed. The clearest, unambiguous half of the finding is the missing native flag: H. moscheutos is a textbook native wetland perennial and the engine treats it as non-native only because the genus row was written for the Asian shrub H. syriacus. The pollinator "some" -> "high" upgrade is also well-supported by parity with the other native wetland pollinator plants the table already flags "high" (buttonbush, swamp milkweed, Joe-Pye, ironweed) and by the documented specialist bee Ptilothrix bombiformis; even if one conservatively kept pollinator at "some", the native error alone stands. Fix must be slug-level (as proposed) so rose-of-sharon and tropical-hibiscus are not wrongly promoted. Note the archetype "hardy-hibiscus" (the species) is the load-bearing slug for companion output; the group also contains moscheutos-dominant hybrid cultivars.

Fully verified. One calibration point worth carrying into the report: the defect is under-crediting, not a harmful pairing. The 8 companions offered (aster, chives, globeflower, colchicum, sweet alyssum, etc.) are all sound full-sun / high-water / zone 5-9 partners that coexist fine; the error only strips the deserved "native plant" and "strong pollinator" framing and prevents genuinely native companions from being elevated for hardy-hibiscus. native:false is the unambiguous library error; pollinator:"some" is a defensible-but-conservative under-rating (moscheutos merits "high"). The proposed fix (slug-level override native:true, pollinator:"high" on hardy-hibiscus, leaving the rose-of-sharon row alone) is correct and consistent with how the file already splits genera via SLUG_OVERRIDES. Severity could reasonably be scored low since no recommendation is made wrong, but medium is defensible given it is a clear-cut library error affecting the whole 12-slug variant group's cards.

Factually accurate that Hibiscus moscheutos is native and better-than-"some" for pollinators, and that a genus-level trait row cannot separate it from Asian/tropical Hibiscus. However it produces no wrong or misleading pairing (all reasons truthful; under-crediting only reorders at the margin), matches the engine's documented under-claiming stance, and the proposed group-level native:true/high fix would over-claim across the group's many complex nativar/hybrid members. If retained at all it is low severity, not medium.

title: Maiden Grass (Miscanthus sinensis) is recommended and praised for the seed heads that are its invasion vector, with no spreader flag
subject: honeysuckle
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in early fall as this one finishes; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; lower at 1.8 m, for the front of the border.
why: Miscanthus sinensis escapes garden plantings by wind-borne seed and is on invasive-species lists across the mid-Atlantic, Southeast and Midwest (regulated/banned in some jurisdictions). The one clause the engine offers as a benefit, standing seed heads through winter, is precisely the trait that seeds roadsides and old fields. The library entry (plantLibrary.ts:2621) carries tags ["plume","fall","silver"] and no aggressive-spreader flag, so there is no penalty and no caution. Telling a beginner to leave the seed heads up is telling them to run the dispersal mechanism. The same pattern repeats with Pampas Grass (Cortaderia selloana, plantLibrary.ts:2640, invasive on the Pacific coast and increasingly the Southeast), recommended to Bearded Iris and praised with the identical seed-head clause, and with Northern Sea Oats (Chasmanthium latifolium), a notorious self-sower recommended into Hosta and Impatiens shade beds where its seedlings are hardest to weed out of clumps.
fix: Flag Miscanthus sinensis, Cortaderia selloana and Chasmanthium latifolium as aggressive self-seeders in the intel data so they take the penalty and carry the caution clause. Alternatively, suppress the 'seed heads stand through winter' benefit clause on any plant flagged as a self-seeder and replace it with 'cut the seed heads back before they ripen'.
scope: 3 named plants; each is an archetype recommendable to any full-sun subject in its zone band.
refutations: The finding's central data claim is a misread. It states Miscanthus sinensis "carries tags [plume,fall,silver] and no aggressive-spreader flag, so there is no penalty and no caution." But the spreader flag does not live in plantLibrary.ts tags at all - it lives in the intel taxonomy, and Miscanthus IS flagged: taxonomy.ts:423 reads `miscanthus: { spreader: true }, // M. sinensis self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US`. The engine authors already anticipated this exact concern and added the flag with a matching comment (plant.ts:161 maps trait.spreader to aggressiveSpreader; compatibility.ts:434 propagates it). The reason the honeysuckle->maiden-grass pair carries only water-differs and no caution is NOT a missing flag: honeysuckle is a Climber, which is in ROBUST_CATEGORIES (compatibility.ts:155), so spreaderNeedsWatching (line 517) and spreaderSmothers (line 505) both correctly stay silent for a woody climber that a clumping grass cannot smother. The proposed fix ("flag Miscanthus in the intel data") is therefore already implemented for the headline plant. Separately, the pairing is horticulturally sound (matched full sun, low-to-moderate water, zones 5-9, no thriving conflict), and "leave the seed heads up for winter structure" is mainstream ornamental-grass advice; the engine documents invasiveness as deliberately not modelled, and its spreader flag models physical crowding of delicate neighbours, not wind-seed dispersal. The finding rests on the false premise that the flag is absent.
notes: Survives at high. Two minor precisions for the report (do not weaken the primary pair): (1) The Northern Sea Oats side-example is imperfect — Chasmanthium latifolium is a US NATIVE (not an exotic invasive) and it DOES already receive the engine's spreader flag in 7 of 213 pairings ("this one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time"); however that flag models bed-level smothering, not self-seeding, so the point (praised seed heads = uncautioned self-sowing) still stands. (2) The first proposed fix (add aggressiveSpreader flag) targets the wrong mechanism, since aggressiveSpreader drives the smothering lens, not seed dispersal; the finding's alternative fix (suppress the "seed heads stand through winter" praise on self-seeders / add a caution clause) is the correct remedy. Primary pair honeysuckle -> maiden-grass and the Pampas Grass example are fully verified.

Data correction for the report: Miscanthus IS flagged spreader:true at taxonomy.ts:423 (with an explicit "self-seeds and is invasive in much of the US" comment), so the headline premise is wrong and the proposed fix is already in place for this plant. The honeysuckle->maiden-grass pair shows no caution only because honeysuckle is a robust Climber, not because the flag is missing. The two secondary asides ARE data-accurate: cortaderia is unflagged (taxonomy.ts:424, `cortaderia: {}`) and chasmanthium carries only native (taxonomy.ts:429) - but note the engine's spreader flag governs physical crowding, and invasiveness/self-seeding is on the engine's documented not-modelled list, so those are non-modelling gaps rather than wrong data. If pursued at all, a much narrower low-severity note about pampas/sea-oats seed-head praise lacking a region-specific invasive caution could be argued, but the finding as written (titled on maiden-grass, high severity, "no spreader flag") is refuted.

Core pair (honeysuckle -> maiden-grass) and the pampas-grass parallel are fully verified and uncautioned. One tertiary example in the finding is slightly overstated: Northern Sea Oats (Chasmanthium latifolium) is Tier-2 and already carries the engine's spreader caution in some reasons (cautioned 7 of 211: "this one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time"), so it is not fully uncautioned like maiden-grass/pampas. This does not weaken the core finding.

title: Ice Plant (a full-sun succulent) is told to grow beneath a canopy in 7 of its 8 pairings; a 1 m Spirea is called "a canopy to plant beneath"
subject: ice-plant
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath; opens in late spring and overlaps into this one's season; echoes the pink.
why: Delosperma is a 10 cm succulent mat: it needs unshaded sun and sharp drainage, and its single reliable killer is shade plus retained moisture plus leaf litter sitting on the crowns, which rots it out in a winter. A Spirea 1 m tall by 1.2 m wide is not a canopy at all, it is a dense skirt of twigs at ground level, and the ground beneath it is exactly where a Delosperma dies. The clause fires because layeringClause (compatibility.ts:1066) only tests a height RATIO > 6 plus tier >= 2, and with a 0.1 m subject every ordinary 1 m perennial or shrub clears that bar: Globe Thistle, Rosemary, Fountain Grass, Little Bluestem, Switchgrass and Perennial Sunflower all get the same 'a canopy to plant beneath' sentence in this card. A beginner following seven of eight suggestions plants their ice plant into shade under herbaceous cover.
fix: Do not emit 'a canopy to plant beneath' when the shorter plant is full-sun (or a succulent) and the taller plant is not a genuine tree/large woody canopy (height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M, or category Tree/Conifer). For an herbaceous overtopper the honest clause is 'taller at 1 m, so keep this one to the sunny edge', not an invitation to underplant.
scope: 7 of ice-plant's 8 companions in this chunk; the clause misfires for any sun-obligate subject under ~0.2 m.
notes: Correct the count: it is 8 of 8 ice-plant companions that carry "a canopy to plant beneath" (the reasoning omitted baptisia), not 7 of 8 — this understates rather than weakens. Confirmed code anchor is compatibility.ts:1066; the missing gate is that the b-taller branch (lines 1063-1067) never checks category Tree/Conifer or STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M, unlike the a-taller branch (line 1072). This is a wrong-REASON finding; the spirea and ice-plant library values themselves are horticulturally reasonable.
Correct the count: it is 8 of 8 of ice-plant's companions that receive "a canopy to plant beneath", not 7 of 8 (the finding omits baptisia, which also gets the clause). This strengthens the finding.

Verified against the data: mechanism is exactly as described (tierOf(1.0)=2 passes tallEnoughToShelter; ratio 10 > CANOPY_RATIO 6; line 1066 fires). Correct the prevalence UP: all 8 of 8 companions carry the identical "stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath" clause (rosemary, echinops, baptisia, perennial-sunflower, fountain-grass, switchgrass, little-bluestem, spirea), not "7 of 8" as titled. The engine is internally inconsistent: layeringScore reserves the true canopy/underplanting branch for tier 3 (>3 m woody, line 646), but layeringClause emits "a canopy to plant beneath" at tier 2 (0.9-3 m, line 1061/1066), contradicting its own comment that the phrase "only makes sense when the taller plant is genuinely above the border." The defect is worse for the grass companions (a 1-1.5 m fountain-grass/switchgrass clump genuinely shades a 10 cm mat) than for the deciduous spirea named in the pair. The proposed fix is sound: gate "a canopy to plant beneath" on the taller plant being genuine woody structure (height >= STRUCTURE_HEIGHT_M or category Tree/Conifer, i.e. tier 3), and fall back to the existing "taller at N m, for the layer behind it" for sub-3 m overtoppers. Keep as a reason-quality/product-credibility finding at medium, not high.

title: Bearded Iris is told to plant beneath an Arborvitae, which guarantees rhizome rot and no bloom
subject: iris-bearded
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath.
why: Bearded iris is the one common perennial whose rhizome must sit at the soil surface and bake in direct sun: it is the standard extension advice, and the two classic failure modes for beginners are (a) no flowers from shade and crowding and (b) soft rot from rhizomes kept damp and covered. Thuja occidentalis casts dense, year-round evergreen shade, has a shallow greedy root plate, and drops a continuous mat of scale-leaf litter that will bury those rhizomes. There is nowhere in a 6 m arborvitae's footprint where a bearded iris will flower. The 'evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape' clause, true of the conifer, is the very reason the iris beneath it fails: this is the one case where the winter-structure benefit and the underplanting suggestion contradict each other.
fix: Never emit 'a canopy to plant beneath' when the canopy plant is evergreen (Conifer or evergreen shrub) and the underplanting is full-sun. Deciduous canopies are fine (Hyacinth under Mock Orange, elsewhere in this chunk, is a good pairing for exactly that reason: the bulbs flower before the shrub leafs out). An evergreen canopy has no such window.
scope: Also affects the same clause wherever a full-sun subject is paired with arborvitae, douglas-fir, or any evergreen conifer.
notes: Solid as written; if tightening the wording, "no bloom, and likely rhizome rot" is more defensible than "guarantees rhizome rot." The strongest, code-backed point to keep front-and-center: layeringClause emits "a canopy to plant beneath" with no evergreen/deciduous or full-sun-underplanting guard, which is the exact defect the proposed fix targets.

Finding is accurate as written. Minor precision note for the report: arborvitae is the lowest-scored of the 8 shown companions (46.7, rank 8/8), not a top recommendation, but it still clears the top-8 display cut from 190 candidates and is shown to users, so the harm stands. Arborvitae is columnar (spread 1.5 m) so its footprint is narrow, but the reason explicitly instructs "a canopy to plant beneath," directing the beginner into the shaded, litter-covered zone. Fix location (layeringClause line 1066 / CANOPY_RATIO gate) is correctly identified.

Verified against data and engine. Confirmed values: pair iris-bearded -> arborvitae, score 46.7, reason "Shares full sun and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; stands well above at 6 m, a canopy to plant beneath." It is shown (rank 8 of 8, no score floor). One factual refinement to carry into the report: arborvitae is the LAST of iris-bearded's 8 companions, so exposure is somewhat limited — but not filtered out. The proposed fix is well-targeted (suppress "a canopy to plant beneath" when the canopy is evergreen — Conifer or evergreen shrub — and the underplanting is full-sun; deciduous canopies retain the pre-leaf-out window). Note the fix should also address the winter-structure clause co-occurring with the canopy clause on the same evergreen, which is what makes the sentence self-contradictory.

title: Because the subject is a climber, a 3 m Lilac is described as "for the front of the border"
subject: honeysuckle
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; opens in mid spring and overlaps into this one's season; lower at 3 m, for the front of the border; both offer pollinator forage.
why: A 3 m by 2.5 m lilac belongs at the BACK of a border; nothing behind it would ever be seen again. The clause is produced by comparing bare heights against the 4 m honeysuckle, but a climber's height sits on a trellis and it occupies no ground layer, so a shorter free-standing shrub is not a front-of-border plant relative to it. The engine already knows this: layeringClause (compatibility.ts:1064-1065) explicitly says 'A climber carries height on a support, so it is never a canopy' and short-circuits, but ONLY when the climber is the companion (b). When the climber is the SUBJECT (a), control falls through to the shrub branch at line 1079 and it emits the front-of-border sentence. The same misfire hits Witch Hazel ('lower at 3 m, for the front of the border', a 3 m by 3 m shrub) and Maiden Grass ('lower at 1.8 m, for the front of the border') in the same card. A beginner acting on this puts a lilac and a witch hazel at the front of the bed.
fix: Apply the existing climber check symmetrically: if a.category === "Climber", return null (or a footprint-aware clause) instead of falling through to the shrub branch of layeringClause.
scope: 3 of honeysuckle's 8 reasons here; systematically affects every Climber subject in the library (clematis, wisteria, trumpet vine, jasmine, etc.).
notes: Diagnosis is partly overstated: the "front of the border" clause is NOT exclusively a climber effect. Line 1079 keys only on relative height (ratio < CANOPY_RATIO=6) with no absolute floor on the companion's height, so a free-standing 4m shrub subject would emit the identical clause for a 3m companion. The proposed climber-symmetric fix (return null when a.category === "Climber") only patches climber subjects; the real root cause is that a 3m plant gets labeled "front of the border" merely for being shorter than a 4m subject. Keep the finding as a confirmed wrong-reason pairing, but frame the fix around an absolute-height floor on the front-of-border phrasing, not just the climber case.

Attribution nuance worth carrying into the report: the "front of the border" clause also fires for any 4-5.9 m non-tree, non-conifer subject, not only climbers. What is uniquely wrong here is that the engine already models climber footprint (line 1064 comment + line 1065 check) but applies it asymmetrically, so a climber subject is treated as a free-standing tall plant. The proposed symmetric fix (if a.category === "Climber") is correct.

Blast radius is placement only: the honeysuckle -> lilac/witch-hazel/maiden-grass pairings are themselves valid; the defect is solely the layering clause. Fix is symmetric: in layeringClause, when a.category === "Climber" and a is the taller side, return null or a footprint-aware clause instead of falling into the shrub branch, mirroring the existing b-climber guard at line 1065.

title: Pampas Grass recommended to Jasmine and praised for the plumes that make it invasive
subject: jasmine
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; a soft colour beside the yellow.
why: Cortaderia selloana is a Cal-IPC High-rated invasive in California, invasive in Hawaii, and a problem escape on the Gulf/Atlantic coasts and coastal Pacific Northwest - i.e. across most of zones 7-11, which is exactly the zone band this pairing is offered in (jasmine 7-10, pampas 7-11). It spreads by tens of thousands of wind-borne seeds carried on the plumes, so the reason's single named benefit ('its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds') is praise for the dispersal mechanism itself. Birds do not, in fact, feed on pampas plumes - the seed is chaffy and wind-dispersed. The library entry (plantLibrary.ts, slug pampas-grass, and its 'Rosea'/'Aureolineata'/'Pumila' variants) carries no caution tag, and the engine has no invasiveness field, so a beginner in zone 9 California is told to plant it and admire the seed heads.
fix: Either drop Cortaderia selloana (and its variants) from the archetype/companion pool, or add an invasiveness flag that (a) suppresses the winter-seedhead clause for wind-dispersed invasives and (b) appends a caution clause the way cautionClause() already does for spreaders. Minimum viable fix: make pampas-grass a non-archetype so it can HAVE companions but never BE one.
scope: pampas-grass is an archetype and can surface in any full-sun/low-water list; 4 Cortaderia entries in the library, none cautioned
notes: Corroboration worth keeping: the audit's own invasive-companion lens (72 rows) flagged only maiden-grass and periwinkle and missed pampas-grass entirely, so this manual finding closes a real gap the automated lens left open. Applies to all four C. selloana library slugs (pampas-grass, -pink 'Rosea', -gold 'Aureolineata', -dwarf 'Pumila'). Separately (out of scope for this finding but noted): the reason's "a soft colour beside the yellow" is odd since Jasminum officinale is white-flowered, not yellow.

Fully corroborated by the audit's own invasive-recount.json, which ranks pampas-grass (Cortaderia selloana) the #1 invasive-recommendation problem: tier-1 invasive on 191 companion lists, all 191 praised, 0 cautioned, same "seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds" praise phrase. The winter-seedhead clause fires because the engine sets seedheads=true for every Grass category (compatibility.ts line 433), and there is no invasiveness field, so cautionClause (spreader-only) never warns. Minor caveat: the "for the birds" sub-claim is the softest part (pampas seed is wind-dispersed/chaffy, not notable bird food) but is not load-bearing. Fix scope should include all four slugs (pampas-grass, -pink, -gold, -dwarf).

Verified live: engine produces jasmine -> pampas-grass at companion #7/8 with the exact quoted reason (I ran companionsFor on the real library). Independently corroborated by the audit's invasive-recount.json (pampas-grass tier-1, 191 lists, 0 cautioned, same praise phrase; noted also as physically hazardous - cutting leaf margins). The same jasmine list additionally recommends maiden-grass and fountain-grass (also invasive), so this card carries three invasive-grass recommendations, strengthening the case. Keep severity high.

title: Butterfly Bush offered as a companion to Italian Cypress and Juniper with no invasiveness caution
subject: juniper
claim: Shares full sun and low water; shorter at 2 m, an understory layer.
why: The library entry is straight species Buddleia davidii (plantLibrary.ts line 2464), not a sterile cultivar. B. davidii is a Class B noxious weed in Washington, is quarantine-listed in Oregon (sale of the species is prohibited; only approved sterile cultivars may be sold), and is a documented riparian invader through much of the eastern and Pacific US. A single plant sets hundreds of thousands of wind-borne seeds. The engine cannot see any of this, so it recommends it in two separate lists in this chunk (italian-cypress and juniper) with a cheerful, benefit-free reason and no caution - and a beginner in Portland or Seattle would be planting a regulated weed. The 'pollinator' tag on the entry compounds it: buddleia is nectar for adult butterflies only and hosts no native larvae, which is the standard extension caveat.
fix: Replace the species entry with a sterile cultivar as the archetype (e.g. 'Miss Molly'/'Blue Chip', already in the library) and/or add an invasiveness flag that keeps regionally regulated species out of the companion pool. If B. davidii stays, it must carry a caution clause.
scope: Appears in 2 of the 10 subject lists in this chunk (italian-cypress, juniper); butterfly-bush is an archetype for the whole full-sun/low-water catalog
notes: Core verified and accurate; keep at high (regulated invasive, illegal to sell the species in Oregon, recommended in 42 lists with zero cautions to non-expert users). Two small corrections for the report: (1) for the two cited pairs the reason is neutral and DOES state a height-layering design benefit, so drop "benefit-free"/"cheerful"; (2) the pollinator-praise "compounds it" claim does not apply to these exact reasons - the pollinator clause appears in other butterfly-bush pairings, not the juniper or italian-cypress ones. The substance (straight-species B. davidii recommended with no invasiveness caution) is fully confirmed.

Finding survives fully on the DATA lens. Minor framing correction to carry into the report: in these two SPECIFIC reasons the plant is not praised for its invasive-driving trait - the reason is flat/benefit-free (sun/water + understory layer), so the "praised for the very trait that makes it invasive" language applies to OTHER lists (where a "both are strong pollinator plants" clause fires per invasive-recount.json's sampleReason), not these two. The core - a regionally-regulated invasive (straight B. davidii) recommended with zero caution, reachable by zone-8 PNW gardeners, when a sterile cultivar could be the archetype instead - is intact. invasive-recount.json shows butterfly-bush recommended across 42 lists with 0 cautioned, so juniper and italian-cypress are two representative instances of a systemic issue.

Two minor overstatements to soften in the report, neither fatal: (1) the reason is not strictly 'benefit-free' - it includes a height-layering benefit clause ('shorter at 2 m, an understory layer'); the accurate criticism is that it carries no invasiveness caution, not that it states no benefit. (2) The 'pollinator' compounding point (praising the plant for pollinators) does NOT appear in these two specific juniper/italian-cypress reasons, which are height-based; that praise shows up in other pairs within the 42-list set (invasive-recount sampleReason). The core - a regionally regulated noxious weed (WA Class B, OR quarantine, sale of the species prohibited in OR) recommended to a non-expert north-star user with zero caution, corroborated by the audit's own invasive-recount.json - is fully confirmed and high severity is warranted.

title: Joe Pye Weed paired with Tulip Tree: a 20 m forest tree recommended as a bed companion for a full-sun, wet-soil perennial
subject: joe-pye-weed
claim: Shares full sun and moderate to high water; blooms in late spring, before this one opens; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months; both are native plants.
why: Liriodendron tulipifera reaches 20-30 m with a 12 m crown and is one of the fastest-growing eastern hardwoods. Planting it as a 'companion' to a 1.8 m full-sun perennial destroys both of the conditions the reason claims they share: within a decade the tulip tree's dense canopy and root plate leave the site shaded and dry, and Eutrochium needs full sun and consistently moist soil to stand up and flower. The pollinator clause is also misleading at garden scale - a seedling tulip tree does not flower for roughly 15 years, and when it does the flowers are held high in the canopy, far above the bed. The engine's own comment on MAX_HEIGHT_RATIO says the cap exists so that 'a 20 m shade tree stops being offered as a bed companion for a 0.6 m perennial (33:1)'; at 1.8 m Joe Pye the ratio is 11:1 and the intended guard simply misses, and the pair scores 80.3, the second-highest in the list.
fix: Gate large canopy trees (height >= ~10 m, or Tree category above the STRUCTURE_TIER) out of the companion pool for herbaceous subjects, rather than relying on the 15:1 ratio alone. Same fault line would offer a 20 m tree to any 1.4 m-plus perennial.
scope: Any perennial >= ~1.35 m can be paired with the 20 m tulip tree under the current 15:1 rule
notes: Minor precision note for the report: the pollinator clause is technically true for both species (tulip tree is a well-known nectar tree), so the defect is that it is misleading at the scale recommended (canopy-height flowers, plus ~15-20 years to first bloom), not botanically false - the finding already states this correctly. The same fault line produces a family of prominent bad pairings (ironweed 80.6, and the sunflower cultivars scoring 84-87) all offering the 20 m tulip tree to 1.4-1.8 m sun perennials.

Minor supporting detail worth including: the engine awarded NO height-layering clause for this pair (the STRUCTURE_TIER_M=3 logic suppressed the 'layer behind it' claim), yet the pair still scores 80.3 and ranks #2 on bloom-succession + pollinator + native + shared-sun/water — evidence that the structure-tier handling is insufficient and the 15:1 ratio is the wrong gate for canopy trees, exactly as the proposed fix states. Also: tulip-tree carries no literal 'pollinator' tag in its tags string, but the engine's derived pollinatorValue is 'high', which is botanically defensible for tulip poplar; this does not weaken the finding.

Two strengthening corrections to carry into the report. (1) The printed reason contains NO scale/height clause at all (the layering clause was competed out by MAX_FACTOR_CLAUSES=3), so the beginner sees four purely positive statements with zero cue that 'Tulip Tree' is a 20-30 m forest hardwood - more misleading than the finding states, not less. (2) The systemic reach is confirmed and larger than 'any 1.4 m+ perennial': tulip-tree surfaces in 18 archetype-subject final lists, including #1 for sunflower (87.9), #4 for perennial-sunflower (80.8), #5 for ironweed (80.6), #2 for canna (54.8), #6 for queen-of-the-prairie - and review-packets covers only the 276 archetype subjects, so every non-archetype cultivar of these perennials inherits the same pairing. Note also this is a pairing/scale-guard gap, not a library-data error: tulip-tree's values (20 m, full sun, moderate water, native, strong pollinator) are horticulturally correct.

title: Japanese Maple paired with Cardinal Flower: sharp-drainage tree on the same watering schedule as a wet-soil obligate
subject: japanese-maple
claim: Takes part sun to part shade and moderate to high water; both offer pollinator forage; shorter at 1 m, an understory layer.
why: Lobelia cardinalis is a streambank and wet-meadow plant: it needs constantly moist to wet soil and is short-lived (often a single season) if it dries out. Acer palmatum is the opposite case - it demands well-drained soil and is the garden tree most associated with Phytophthora root rot and verticillium when planted in wet or poorly drained ground. There is no watering schedule that serves both: water for the lobelia and you risk the maple; water for the maple and the lobelia collapses. The engine's 'moderate to high water' phrasing actively hides this by presenting the one-rung gap as a shared requirement. Secondary problem in the same sentence: 'both offer pollinator forage' is asserted for a tree the library records with no bloom window at all (japanese-maple has bloom: none), so the app tells the user a plant it shows as never flowering feeds pollinators.
fix: Treat wet-soil obligates (Lobelia cardinalis, and similarly 'high' water plants whose need is wetness rather than volume) as incompatible with drainage-sensitive woody plants rather than one rung apart; and suppress the pollinator clause when either plant has no bloom window (pollinatorScore should require !foliage on both sides for the forage claim).
scope: cardinal-flower also appears in the jacobs-ladder list with the same water fudge; the no-bloom pollinator claim recurs 4 times in the japanese-maple list
refutations: Verified against the real files. The pair, reason string, and library values are read correctly, but two of the finding's load-bearing claims fail on the data. (1) The "secondary problem" is a misread: bloom:"" is not "never flowers" — the engine labels it `foliage` = "grown for leaf, needle, or structure" (compatibility.ts:362), i.e. ornamentally-insignificant flowers. The "both offer pollinator forage" clause is NOT derived from bloom; it fires only when pollinatorScore>0, which requires a documented forage value on both plants (compatibility.ts:732-735). Japanese maple earns it from a deliberate curated taxonomy trait `acer: { pollinator: "some" }, // confidence: high` (taxonomy.ts:391), which overrides the foliage→none default (plant.ts:114-117). This is botanically correct (maples are a documented early-spring bee forage source) and matches the engine's stated promise "no reason ever claims forage value we cannot cite" (compatibility.ts:730). So the forage claim is true and intentional, not false/misleading.
