The proposed fix (require !foliage on both sides) would strip correct forage credit from maples/willows and break a deliberate design. (2) The water prong is overstated: Lobelia cardinalis is facultative-wetland (FACW), a standard moist-border/rain-garden perennial that grows in ordinary evenly-moist garden soil, not a wet-soil obligate; Japanese maple's constraint is drainage, not moisture. A humus-rich, evenly-moist, well-drained woodland bed serves both (a common woodland pairing), so "no watering schedule serves both" is too strong. The engine already penalizes (water-differs x0.92) and discloses ("moderate to high water") the gap. Residual: a mild "site it in evenly-moist, well-drained soil" caveat exists, but it is a low-grade note, not a medium bad-pairing.
notes: Keep the finding, but rewrite it to stand ONLY on the moisture/drainage mismatch: a wet-obligate streambank perennial (Lobelia cardinalis) recommended as understory to a drainage-sensitive Japanese maple (Acer palmatum, prone to Phytophthora/Verticillium in wet soil), with the reason presenting the moisture gap as a shared "moderate to high water" requirement and giving no drainage caveat (shown as companion #4/8, score 43.7). DROP the pollinator sub-claim entirely: it is botanically incorrect. The "both offer pollinator forage" clause comes from a deliberate curated rating acer: { pollinator: "some" } (confidence: high), and Acer palmatum does flower and does provide early-season bee forage, so the clause is true and the proposed !foliage fix would suppress a correct statement. Down-rate to low: one-rung water gap, already penalized and low-scored, and survivable in a moist-but-drained bed; the likely outcome is an under-performing/short-lived cardinal flower rather than a killed maple.

If any note survives, it is only a mild siting caveat: cardinal-flower (water:high, FACW) prefers reliably moist soil while japanese-maple needs good drainage, so the shared spot should be evenly moist AND well-drained. Do NOT act on the finding's pollinator claim or its proposed fix: japanese maple's forage value is a correct, curated taxonomy fact (acer: pollinator "some", confidence high), and the bloom:"" field is an ornamental-display flag, not "never flowers." Suppressing the forage clause for foliage plants would remove accurate data.

Keep as medium, drainage-conflict finding. Trim/soften two overstatements: the water reason DISCLOSES the one-rung gap (and applies the water-differs penalty) rather than hiding it - reframe the real hidden axis as drainage/soil-moisture-retention, not water volume. Drop or heavily soften the pollinator claim: acer is curated pollinator:"some" (taxonomy.ts L391), Acer palmatum genuinely offers minor spring forage and does flower, so "both offer pollinator forage" is not false; it is only a mild inconsistency with the card's bloom:none, not the "app claims a never-flowering plant feeds pollinators" framing in the finding. Core survivable point: an expensive drainage-sensitive specimen tree recommended in-bed with a wet-soil obligate, where the shared "high water" cannot signal that one wants bog and the other rots in it.

title: White-flowered Jasmine described as yellow in six of its eight companion reasons
subject: jasmine
claim: a soft colour beside the yellow
why: Jasminum officinale is white-flowered (its own library tags say 'white'), and its bloom hex is #fbfbf5, an off-white. colorName() in src/browse/plantFacets.ts gates 'white' on HSL saturation (l >= 0.82 && s < 0.25), but HSL saturation blows up near l = 1: #fbfbf5 computes to h=60, s=0.43, l=0.97, so it falls straight through to the yellow bucket. The compatibility engine feeds that name into colourClause as the SUBJECT's colour, so every colour clause in the jasmine list reads 'a soft colour beside the yellow' - including the ones for maiden grass (silver), fountain grass, pampas grass and wisteria. A beginner planning a white-and-purple scented corner is being told, six times, that their jasmine is yellow. Same fault mislabels Solomon's seal (#fbfbf2 -> yellow), climbing hydrangea (#f3f0ea -> orange) and star jasmine (#f5f2ec -> orange).
fix: Gate 'white' on chroma, not HSL saturation: in colorName, return 'white' when (max-min) < ~0.06 regardless of computed s, before the hue buckets. That reclassifies every off-white bloom hex correctly and also lets the nicer 'its white flowers settle the pink' clause fire.
scope: Every plant whose bloom hex is a tinted off-white; at least jasmine, solomons-seal, climbing-hydrangea, jasmine-star, pampas-grass
notes: Finding is accurate and well-diagnosed. One framing correction: this is a colorName() classifier bug (HSL saturation blows up near lightness 1), not literally wrong library data — the library correctly stores #fbfbf5 and tags jasmine "white." The user-facing effect is exactly as described: 6 of jasmine's 8 companion reasons call the white plant "yellow." The same bug is broader than stated: every gardenia (#fbfbf5, tagged white) and other off-white/cream blooms are misclassified too, so severity medium is fair-to-conservative. Proposed fix (gate white on chroma < ~0.06 before hue buckets) is correct.

Verified exactly as written. The clause 'a soft colour beside the yellow' is grammatically about the candidate ('a soft colour') beside the subject ('the yellow'), where 'the yellow' is jasmine's own misclassified colour name; a reader on jasmine's detail card is told six times that their white jasmine is yellow. Root cause is colorName in src/browse/plantFacets.ts line 86 gating 'white' on HSL saturation, which is unstable near l=1; the fix should gate on chroma (max-min) instead. Note the same misclassification also files jasmine under the 'yellow' Browse colour facet via plantColors (though its 'white' tag keeps it in the white facet too). Medium is appropriate: systematic false plant-fact in user copy, no horticultural harm since the pairings themselves are sound.

Category should be "classifier bug / wrong reason" rather than "wrong-library-data": the library data is actually correct (jasmine is properly tagged "white" with a plausible off-white hex #fbfbf5). The defect is in colorName's white-gate in src/browse/plantFacets.ts, where the HSL saturation test fails on near-white blooms. The proposed chroma-based fix ((max-min) < ~0.06 returns "white" before hue bucketing) is sound and correctly reclassifies #fbfbf5, #fbfbf2, #f3f0ea, #f5f2ec as white while leaving true yellows like #f2c81f (chroma 0.83) unaffected.

title: Japanese Forest Grass credited with seed heads that stand through winter, though the library gives it no bloom at all
subject: lady-fern
claim: Shares part shade and moderate water; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds.
why: Hakonechloa macra is grown purely for foliage - the library entry itself carries no bloom window and is tagged 'foliage'. Its flowers are insignificant airy panicles that produce no persistent seed head; the plant goes tan and flops after frost and is cut to the ground in late winter. It offers neither winter structure nor bird seed. The claim comes from toCompatPlant(), where seedheads = (category === 'Grass'), a blanket rule that contradicts the file's own stated care over this clause (the WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA comment explains they curated a genus list precisely so the engine would not tell a gardener that English daisy holds its seed heads through winter). Here the winter-structure factor is the ONLY benefit clause in the whole reason, so the pairing's entire stated value is false.
fix: In toCompatPlant, require a bloom window for the grass branch: seedheads = (category === 'Grass' && !foliage) || WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus). That drops Hakonechloa (all 4 variants) and any other bloomless grass out of the winter-structure claim.
scope: All 4 Hakonechloa entries plus any Grass-category plant with no bloom data; the clause is load-bearing wherever it is the only factor above the floor
notes: Verified end-to-end. The false winter-structure clause is not merely cosmetic: it is both the sole stated benefit of the pairing and the largest single contributor to the pair's 38.2 score (winterStructure 1.0x15 of a 24.8 weighted sum). The defect is generic to the seedheads=(category==="Grass") rule, so it also mislabels every other bloomless/foliage grass (all 4 Hakonechloa variants at minimum); the proposed fix (require a bloom window on the grass branch) is sound. Severity medium is appropriate: the pairing itself is a valid shade combination so no plant dies, but a beginner is told the plant feeds birds and holds seed heads through winter, both false, and the "for the birds" claim is actionable.

Accurate as written. Worth recording that the PAIRING is horticulturally sound (fern + Hakone grass is a classic, well-matched part-shade/moderate-water shade combination) - the defect is purely the false reason, so no plant is harmed. That keeps it firmly a wrong-reason finding and arguably at the lower edge of medium, but medium is defensible because winter interest is the sole stated benefit and the claim is false across all Hakonechloa (and any other bloomless grass caught by the same blanket rule).

Confirmed the winter-structure clause is the only benefit clause for lady-fern -> japanese-forest-grass (conditions preamble aside). Root cause is the blanket seedheads = (category === "Grass") in toCompatPlant (line 433), which contradicts the curated WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA list the same file maintains for perennials. Not isolated: the same false clause appears for japanese-forest-grass across amaryllis, cinnamon-fern, coleus, elephant-ear, royal-fern, snowdrop, snowflake, and wild-ginger, and applies to any bloomless (foliage-only) grass. Proposed fix (require a bloom window for the grass branch) is directionally sound: showy-flowered grasses carry a bloom window (foliage=false) and would be retained, while foliage grasses like Hakonechloa are correctly dropped from the winter-seed-head claim.

title: Larkspur is told to plant Pampas Grass and is sold on the plumes, the exact organ that makes it a listed invasive
subject: larkspur
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late summer as this one finishes; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Cortaderia selloana is rated 'High' by Cal-IPC and is a serious invasive in California, the Pacific Northwest, Hawaii and the Southeast; it is a regulated/noxious plant in several jurisdictions. The invasion vector IS the winter plume: the app's own curated file (src/garden/intel/curated/pampas-grass.json) says 'each female plume can throw up to about 100,000 wind-borne seeds' and 'Invasive; check local lists'. The engine's reason praises those plumes as a benefit and adds 'for the birds', which is false twice over: the seedheads clause fires for every plant of category Grass (compatibility.ts, `seedheads = category === "Grass" || WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA.has(genus)`), pampas seed is not a notable bird food, and the plumes are what spread it. A beginner is being told to plant a state-listed weed and to leave the seed heads up all winter, in the zones (7-11) where it is hardy and fertile. The blade edges also cut skin, per the same curated file.
fix: Exclude `cortaderia` from the Grass-blanket `seedheads` flag so the 'seed heads stand through winter, for the birds' clause cannot fire for it, and either drop pampas grass from the archetype companion pool or make the reason carry its curated invasive caution. The generic Grass seedhead rule should be a curated list like WINTER_SEEDHEAD_GENERA, not a category blanket.
scope: Any subject that gets pampas-grass as a companion; the faulty 'for the birds' seedhead clause fires for every Grass in the catalog
notes: Correction to carry into the report: the finding's line "Cortaderia selloana is rated 'High' by Cal-IPC" is quoting the app's own curated file, but Cal-IPC's inventory actually rates C. selloana as Moderate; it is C. jubata that Cal-IPC rates High. This does NOT change the conclusion (selloana is still Cal-IPC-inventoried and noxious-listed in several jurisdictions; the audit's own invasive-recount.json frames it accurately as "invasive in CA, HI, TX and the Southeast; a listed noxious weed in several counties"). The report should attribute the "High" rating to the app's curated file, or drop it in favor of the recount's careful framing, rather than assert "Cal-IPC High" as its own fact. Everything else stands: this is a systemic pattern (191 praised lists, 0 cautioned), it praises the exact organ (the plume) that is the invasion vector, the "for the birds" clause is a false food claim for pampas, and the recommendation carries no caution because the curated invasive warning lives on pampas's own page, not on the subject card where the pairing is shown. Severity high is correct.

Larkspur being an Annual means the zone filter is skipped, so pampas (hardy ~7-11) is recommended even to zone-2/3 larkspur growers who cannot grow it - a secondary defect, but it does not change the finding. The core issue is unchanged: a Cal-IPC 'High' invasive is recommended to beginners across 191 lists, praised for the exact organ (winter plume/seedhead) that is its documented invasion vector, with a false "for the birds" benefit and zero caution, while the app's own curated data carries the invasive warning that the companion reason omits.

Finding survives fully. Verified: larkspur (annual, zone test skipped) -> pampas-grass ships as companion #5/8, reason exactly as quoted, penalty only "water-differs" (no caution clause). Pampas is Cal-IPC "High"; the seedhead praise is a category blanket for all Grass (line 433) and fires with no bird-forage or invasive guard (line 1109). Corroborated by the audit's own invasive-recount.json (191 lists, 191 praised, 0 cautioned) and the product's own free-tier curated warning in pampas-grass.json, which the recommendation engine bypasses. Note this is one instance of a systemic problem (also maiden-grass/Miscanthus, periwinkle/Vinca); the proposed fix — replace the Grass-category seedhead blanket with a curated list and either drop invasive archetypes from the companion pool or carry their curated caution into the reason — addresses the whole class, not just this pair. Minor: the "for the birds" clause is a weak secondary claim; the core harm (praising the invasion vector on a listed invasive with no caution) stands independently.

title: Lilac is told a suckering Campsis radicans is 'height without the footprint' - it will strangle the lilac
subject: lilac
claim: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; both offer pollinator forage, in different months; climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint.
why: Campsis radicans is a heavy woody vine that suckers from underground runners into dense colonies and climbs anything adjacent. The app's own curated file (curated/trumpet-vine.json) states it plainly: 'The headline issue: it suckers from underground runners and self-seeds into dense colonies that choke out neighbors, and it is hard to remove once established' and 'give it a strong freestanding arbor or wall, never house siding, gutters, wood trim or a small tree'. Planted behind a 3 m lilac it will use the lilac as its trellis and its woody trunks will bow and eventually kill it, and its suckers will come up through the whole bed. 'Height without the footprint' is the precise opposite of the truth; the library's spread of 2 m is also wrong for a colonising vine. taxonomy.ts DOES flag `campsis: { spreader: true }`, but the warning is suppressed because ROBUST_CATEGORIES treats a Shrub as able to 'hold its own against a running perennial' (compatibility.ts `isVulnerableTo`) - a rule written for herbaceous runners like mint, not for a woody vine that climbs.
fix: In `isVulnerableTo` / `spreaderNeedsWatching`, do not apply the ROBUST_CATEGORIES exemption when the SPREADER is itself category Climber or a woody plant; a woody suckering vine beside a shrub/tree must at minimum carry 'spreader-needs-watching'. Never emit the 'height without the footprint' clause for a spreader-flagged climber.
scope: Also Lemon Balm -> Trumpet Vine (score 50, penalties []), and any Shrub/Tree/Rose subject offered trumpet-vine or another spreader-flagged climber
notes: Core finding is correct and high severity; two wording softenings recommended before publishing. (a) Campsis is self-clinging, so 'woody trunks will bow and eventually kill it' is better stated as 'climbs into, shades, weighs down and overwhelms the lilac over years' (killing a large multi-stem lilac is plausible but at the strong end; overwhelm/severe-damage is the defensible claim). (b) The 'library spread of 2 m is wrong' point is secondary and debatable - for a Climber, spread conventionally means canopy-on-support, not colony extent - so lead with the reason-is-false and no-caution points, not the spread field. Worth noting the value: the audit's own automated invasive-companion lens did NOT catch this (Campsis is absent from its invasive list; 0 hits for trumpet-vine as a companion), so this fills a real gap. The engine-mechanics fix in the finding (do not apply the ROBUST_CATEGORIES exemption when the spreader is itself a Climber/woody vine; never emit 'height without the footprint' for a spreader-flagged climber) is on target.

Confirmed as displayed (rank 5 of 8, score 62.9, penalties empty). The defect is not merely the documented "engine ignores invasiveness" omission: the spreader flag IS present and the engine actively suppresses the caution for this pair via the ROBUST_CATEGORIES exemption written for herbaceous runners, not woody climbing vines. Reason text and all library values verified byte-for-byte against review-packets.json.

Survives at high. Two wording corrections to fold in so the report does not overstate: (1) 'strangle' slightly misnames the mechanism - Campsis radicans climbs by aerial rootlets and overwhelms a shrub by mass, shading, and bed-wide suckering, not by twining/girdling like wisteria; the damaging outcome is real but describe it as smothering/overwhelming plus colonizing the bed. (2) De-emphasize the sub-claim that 'the library's spread of 2 m is wrong' - 2 m is a defensible single-plant canopy spread on a support; the real defect is the SUPPRESSED spreader flag the engine already holds (campsis.spreader:true), not the spread field. Lead the fix with the ROBUST_CATEGORIES exemption wrongly covering a woody climbing spreader. Also note campsis is US-native, so this is correctly an aggressive-habit bad-pairing (category bad-pairing), not an exotic-invasive case. The core defects - an affirmatively false reason clause ('height without the footprint' is the inverse of the plant's headline trait) shown with no caution across 15 subjects while the app's own data flatly contradicts it - are fully confirmed.

title: Lady's Mantle is handed three running evergreen mats (Vinca minor, Pachysandra, Ajuga) with the spread warning silently switched off
subject: ladys-mantle
claim: Shares part shade and low to moderate water; opens in mid spring and overlaps into this one's season; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; lower at 0.15 m, for the front of the border.
why: Vinca minor is a stoloniferous evergreen mat that roots at every node; it is listed as invasive or 'do not plant' across much of the eastern and midwestern US, where it escapes into woodland and forms monocultures. Pachysandra terminalis (also recommended, score 54.3) is the same story, and the app's own curated file says 'it can escape into woodlands, smothering native plants; Maryland no longer recommends it'. Ajuga reptans (46.0) is flagged in curated as spreading 'aggressively'. Three of Lady's Mantle's eight companions are running mats that will simply close over a 0.4 m clump-forming Alchemilla, and NONE of the three reasons says so, while Ligularia's card DOES warn about the very same plants ('a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging'). The cause is `isVulnerableTo`'s first line, `if (neighbour.spreader) return false; // equal vigour, let them fight`, combined with `alchemilla: { spreader: true }` in taxonomy.ts. But Alchemilla mollis is a self-seeder, not a runner: it has no mechanism to hold ground against a rooting stolon mat, so 'equal vigour' is false here.
fix: Either drop `spreader: true` from `alchemilla` (it self-seeds, it does not run), or keep the 'this one spreads / give it room' clause whenever EITHER plant is spreader-flagged instead of cancelling it when both are. Separately, an invasive-in-the-US groundcover (vinca, pachysandra) should carry its curated caution in the reason rather than only being praised as an evergreen carpet.
scope: 3 of Lady's Mantle's 8 companions (periwinkle, pachysandra, ajuga); the cancel-out rule affects every spreader-flagged subject
notes: Reframe the harm from "will smother the Alchemilla clump" to the two verified problems: (1) the spreader caution clause is silently switched off whenever BOTH plants carry the spreader flag (proven by the Ligularia contrast: same ajuga/pachysandra, non-spreader subject, gets "a vigorous spreader, so give it room or an edging"), and (2) Vinca minor and Pachysandra terminalis - invasive in much of the US per the app's own curated/deepIntel files - are recommended to a beginner praised as evergreen carpets with no caution. Affects three companions of ladys-mantle (periwinkle, pachysandra, ajuga); periwinkle is the clearest case and is the one the audit's own invasive-companion lens flags as high. Do not assert the mats will "simply close over" a mature 0.4 m Alchemilla clump - it holds its own and gets invaded/crowded rather than smothered. The stronger of the two proposed fixes is to keep the caution whenever EITHER plant is spreader-flagged; dropping alchemilla's spreader flag is weaker since it is a genuine nuisance self-seeder, just by seed rather than by running.

Fix the mechanistic attribution when writing this up: the missing caution is caused by `spreaderNeedsWatching`'s `soft()` predicate (compatibility.ts line 518) returning false when BOTH plants are spreader-flagged, not by `isVulnerableTo`'s first line (line 504, which only governs the smother-block that was never triggered here). The finding's second proposed fix (keep the caution whenever EITHER plant is spreader-flagged) is the correct target. Also note: the audit's own invasive-companion lens auto-flagged only periwinkle (Vinca minor) for Lady's Mantle, not pachysandra or ajuga, but the app's own curated/deepIntel files document pachysandra as "invasive, no longer recommended (Maryland)" and ajuga as "aggressive, avoid (Maryland/Florida)", so the characterization of all three as problematic running mats is supported by the app's data even though only one tripped the lens. Soften the "will close over the clump" language: Alchemilla mollis is a self-seeder and a fairly robust clump, so the strongest angle is the invasive-praised-no-caution inconsistency, not smothering.

Verified against source. Mechanism CONFIRMED: isVulnerableTo() short-circuits on `if (neighbour.spreader) return false` and spreaderNeedsWatching()'s soft() test excludes spreaders, so when both subject and candidate are spreader-flagged the smother filter AND the caution clause are both suppressed; traced the vinca pair and it yields penalties=['water-differs'] only, matching the review packet. Data CONFIRMED: Lady's Mantle's list contains Pachysandra (54.3), Ajuga (46), Periwinkle/Vinca minor (42.3), none with a spread caution. Invasive claims CONFIRMED by the APP'S OWN curated files: periwinkle.json ("It escapes gardens across much of North America, smothering woodland floors. Trailing stems root wherever a node touches soil"), pachysandra.json ("can escape into woodlands, smothering native plants; Maryland no longer recommends it" — verbatim), ajuga.json ("spreads aggressively... Maryland and Florida say avoid it"); and the audit's own invasive-recount.json lists periwinkle as tier-1, praised 4/4, cautioned 0. RECOMMEND reframing: the solid, harmful core is that Vinca minor (a documented woodland invasive per the app's own detail card) is recommended to a beginner and praised as an evergreen front-of-border carpet with no caution, contradicting its own plant page — NOT the "alchemilla gets smothered" thesis, which is weak because alchemilla is a robust self-seeder taller than all three groundcovers. Downgraded high->medium: the specific per-pair smothering danger is overstated, vinca is a low-frequency (4 lists) regional invasive, and pachysandra/ajuga are milder/borderline. The soundest fix is the finding's second option (surface a caution when EITHER plant is spreader-flagged) plus surfacing the existing curated invasive caution — not deleting alchemilla's flag.

title: Lamium maculatum is missing the spreader flag, so a running mat is recommended over a fern and over a short-lived seeder
subject: lamium
claim: Takes part shade to full shade and moderate water; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; taller at 0.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: taxonomy.ts line 259 gives `lamium: { family: "Lamiaceae", pollinator: "some", deerResistant: true }` with no `spreader: true`, although Lamium maculatum is exactly the same kind of stoloniferous, node-rooting groundcover as ajuga, vinca, pachysandra and galium, all of which ARE flagged. Two consequences are user-visible here. (1) Christmas Fern is category Fern, so `isDelicate` returns true; had lamium been flagged, `spreaderSmothers` would have BLOCKED this pair outright, yet it is served as companion #8 with no caution: the fern's crown will be run over. (2) Lamium -> Cardinal Flower (59.2) is worse: Lobelia cardinalis is a short-lived perennial that persists only by seeding into open, moist ground (its curated file: 'It is a short-lived perennial... let some seed drop (the tiny seed needs light, so don't bury it)'), and a closed Lamium mat prevents exactly that, so the cardinal flower disappears in two or three years while the reason promises 'both offer pollinator forage'.
fix: Add `spreader: true` to the `lamium` entry in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts, matching ajuga/vinca/pachysandra. That alone blocks the fern pairing and adds the 'give it room or an edging' clause everywhere else lamium appears.
scope: Every pairing where Lamium is subject or companion (69 candidates); most acutely lamium -> christmas-fern and lamium -> cardinal-flower
refutations: Mechanically the finding is accurate (spreader = taxonomy trait; lamium unflagged while peers are; christmas-fern is companion #8 and would block if lamium were flagged; cardinal-flower is #5 at 59.2). But under the MATTERS lens it does not survive. (1) The premise is contestable: Lamium maculatum is a modest, generally well-behaved rooting groundcover, materially less aggressive than Vinca minor or Ajuga reptans (both widely invasive) and distinct from the truly invasive L. galeobdolon; leaving it unflagged is a defensible mainstream horticultural call, not a clear data error. (2) The headline harm is a false alarm: a 0.2 m lamium mat around a robust, evergreen, taller (0.5 m) Christmas fern is a standard, commonly recommended shade combination, not a smothering hazard; "the crown will be run over" is overstated, and the engine's categorical fern-is-delicate rule is conservative, so blocking this pair would lose a good rec rather than prevent harm. (3) The cardinal-flower concern is real botany but self-limiting: L. cardinalis needs consistently wet soil (high water) where lamium (moderate, dislikes wet feet) will not form an aggressive closed mat; the one-rung water mismatch is already penalized; cardinal flower is short-lived and fades from borders regardless of neighbor; and it is already a bottom-of-list 59.2. No beginner is materially hurt by either pairing.
notes: Two small corrections that do not change the verdict. (1) Under the proposed fix, the Cardinal Flower pair would be BLOCKED, not merely cautioned: its 0.4 m spread is <= DELICATE_SPREAD_M (0.5), so it is delicate and spreaderSmothers would filter it. This strengthens the finding (a pairing that should be dropped is currently served). (2) The "the fern's crown will be run over" harm is overstated: an established 0.5 m robust evergreen Christmas Fern overtops a 0.2 m lamium mat, so the real-world smothering risk to that specific fern is modest; the fern consequence rests on the engine's coarse categorical Fern=delicate rule rather than strong botany. The soundest real-garden consequence is the Cardinal Flower self-seeding suppression, and the underlying data fix (add spreader:true to lamium) is correct and consistent with the codebase's conventions.

Finding stands. Minor correction for the report: adding spreader:true to lamium would BLOCK the cardinal-flower pairing (Lobelia spread 0.4 m <= DELICATE_SPREAD_M 0.5, so isDelicate true), not just append the 'give it room or an edging' clause -- the caption clause only applies to non-delicate/robust neighbours (e.g. the shrub companions azalea, summersweet, witch-hazel). Also worth noting the ripple: lamium is served as a companion to two additional ferns (autumn-fern, holly-fern), both of which the flag would likewise block.

If retained at all, this is a low-value taxonomy-tuning note, not a medium wrong-data harm: one could argue lamium deserves a spreader flag on par with cerastium/galium (which would add the "give it room or an edging" caution wherever lamium is recommended), but the two cited consequences do not stand. The Christmas-fern pairing is a benign, common shade combination, and the cardinal-flower reseeding concern is undercut by the moderate-vs-high water mismatch (lamium will not thrive/spread aggressively in the wet site cardinal flower needs) plus cardinal flower's general short-lived, reseeding-dependent nature.

title: Colchicum is sent to the front of the border on a height that only measures its leafless autumn flower
subject: lavender
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in early fall as this one finishes; both offer pollinator forage, in different months; lower at 0.15 m, for the front of the border.
why: The library records colchicum at h=0.15 m, which is the height of the naked autumn flower only. Colchicum's leaves are the other half of the plant and the engine cannot see them: broad strap leaves ~25-30 cm tall appear in spring and then collapse into a large yellow mess through early summer (the app's own curated file: 'Strap leaves emerge in spring and die back by summer; then the flowers rise from bare ground in fall with no foliage'). The standard extension advice is therefore the exact opposite of what the reason gives: plant colchicum BEHIND or among leafy perennials that will hide the dying foliage, never at the front of a border where it is the first thing seen for six weeks. A beginner following this puts a 0.6 m lavender behind a 0.15 m 'edging' bulb and gets a bare foreground of rotting leaves in June. (A secondary point on the same pair: every part of Colchicum is deadly, colchicine-bearing, and it is placed at the border edge where children and pets reach.)
fix: Give colchicum a foliage height (or a `foliageDiesBackMessily` trait) so the layering clause uses the leaf height, not the flower height, and suppress the 'for the front of the border' clause for bulbs whose foliage ripens messily (colchicum, daffodil, hyacinth); for these say 'plant it where later foliage will cover its dying leaves'.
scope: Both colchicum pairings in this chunk (Lavender -> Colchicum 69.2, Liatris -> Colchicum 72.2), and any subject offered colchicum
notes: Tighten the framing: the stored 0.15 m is not "the flower only" but the overall flowering-plant height (which the app's own catalog audit marks as agreeing with sources); the genuinely uncaptured half is the taller SPRING FOLIAGE (~0.25-0.35 m strap leaves) that dies back messily by early summer. The real defect is that the layering clause uses the flowering-plant height and is blind to that foliage phase, so "for the front of the border" omits colchicum's most important placement caveat (site it where later/adjacent foliage masks the dying leaves). Soften "the exact opposite of extension advice" to "omits the standard caveat" since some sources do recommend front placement for the small autumn flowers. Both the messy-foliage facts and the deadly colchicine toxicity are documented in the app's own colchicum curated/deepIntel files, so the fix has in-house sourcing.

Minor precision notes, none affecting validity: the curated quote is truncated (full text ends "...with no foliage, hence 'naked ladies'"). The core defect is a wrong-reason/placement error (front-of-border for a plant whose foliage should be masked), not a thrive-killing conflict, so medium is correct. The secondary toxicity point (deadly colchicine, placed at the reachable border edge) is genuinely supported by deepIntel but is a caution-absence, not the reason's central error.

Survives at medium. Two honest qualifications to carry into the report: (1) the harm is aesthetic and seasonal, not lethal - during the Sept-Oct bloom the front placement is genuinely fine, so the reason is only wrong for the spring-to-early-summer foliage window, when the app's 3D likely shows the flower, not the coarse dying leaves, giving the user no visual warning. (2) h=0.15 is defensible as bloom height rather than flatly wrong data; the real defect is the placement clause built on it, since the plant's spring foliage (~25-30 cm) is taller and dies back messily. The proposed fix should suppress the 'front of the border' clause for bulbs with messy-ripening foliage rather than treat 0.15 as an error. Also note this same pair already carries a separate flagged issue (xeric-vs-wetter: low-water lavender on colchicum's moderate schedule) - the toxicity aside is real but the engine makes no safety claim, so it is a supporting note, not the crux.

title: Lisianthus -> Pampas Grass: a Cal-IPC-rated invasive recommended, and praised for the plumes that make it invasive
subject: lisianthus
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it.
why: Cortaderia selloana is one of the most damaging ornamental escapes on the Pacific coast (Cal-IPC 'High'), a declared noxious weed in Hawaii, and now naturalising through the Gulf/Southeast. Its female plumes are the dispersal organ: tens of thousands of wind-borne seeds per plume, carried for kilometres. The reason praises exactly that trait ('its seed heads stand through winter'), and the invented benefit 'and the birds' is false: pampas plumes are wind-dispersed chaff, not a bird seed source, and the leaf margins are serrated enough to cut hands. The zone band the library gives it (7-11) is precisely the range where it escapes. In the library, taxonomy.ts has `cortaderia: {}` - no spreader, no caution - while `miscanthus: { spreader: true }` right beside it carries an explicit 'invasive in much of the US' comment, so the maintainer's own standard was not applied here. Secondary: a 2.5 m x 1.5 m grass beside a 0.5 m cut-flower annual will simply shade and swamp it.
fix: Set `cortaderia: { spreader: true }` (or add a proper invasive/caution flag) in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts so pampas is de-scored and carries a warning clause, and drop 'and the birds' from the seed-head template unless the plant actually has birdSeedheads.
scope: Every pairing that offers pampas-grass (and its 3 cultivar variants) as a companion; the seed-head/bird clause is library-wide.
notes: Confirmed high. Trim two overstatements when writing the report so the strong core isn't diluted: (1) 'and the birds' is not uniquely invented for pampas - it is the generic winterClause template (compatibility.ts ~L1109) fired for every Grass; better framed as 'unsupported/misleading for a wind-dispersed grass that is not a notable bird-seed source' rather than 'false'. (2) The 'Secondary' scale point is weak and should be demoted or dropped: the ratio is 2.5/0.5 = 5:1, well under the 15:1 cap, and 'taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it' is ordinary border layering, not a swamp risk. The proposed fix (`cortaderia: { spreader: true }`) is consistent with the codebase's existing invasive-as-spreader convention (the miscanthus precedent) and would trigger the x0.8 penalty plus the 'give it room or an edging' caution clause; note the engine's `spreader` flag semantically means clonal smothering, whereas pampas's harm is by seed, so a dedicated invasive/caution flag would be the cleaner remedy.

Fully verified. This is the textbook irresponsible-advice case the audit brief names, and it is systemic, not a one-off: the same cortaderia: {} data gap causes pampas-grass to be praised (never cautioned) across 191 companion lists per the audit's own invasive-recount.json. The lisianthus pair is a valid, representative exemplar. Proposed fix (add spreader/caution to cortaderia, and gate the "and the birds" clause) is sound.

Verified against the data: the pairing is real (lisianthus -> pampas-grass, companion #5 of 8, score 46.6, reason quoted verbatim). Pampas library facts confirmed: Cortaderia selloana, full-sun/low water, zones 7-11, 2.5m x 1.5m vs lisianthus 0.5m Annual. Taxonomy inconsistency confirmed: cortaderia: {} (taxonomy.ts line 424) directly below miscanthus: { spreader: true } // "invasive in much of the US" (line 423) - the maintainer's own invasive-spreader standard was applied to Miscanthus but not Cortaderia, which is why the audit's invasive-companion lens catches every Miscanthus pairing but none of the 191 pampas pairings. Two corrections to fold into the report: (a) state the invasive status as "Cortaderia selloana is Cal-IPC 'Moderate' (C. jubata is 'High'), a Hawaii state noxious weed, and a serious escape on the Pacific coast and through the Gulf/Southeast" rather than "Cal-IPC 'High'"; (b) frame "and the birds" as a generic seed-head template clause that is unsupported for pampas (wind-dispersed plumes are not a documented bird food), not a pampas-specific fabrication. The core high-severity claim - a widely-recognized regional invasive recommended to a beginner, praised for the very winter-plume trait that drives its spread, with no caution, across 191 lists - is fully confirmed and matches the audit brief's own definition of irresponsible advice. Proposed fix (add cortaderia spreader/caution flag; make the "birds" clause conditional on real bird value) is sound.

title: Maidenhair Fern -> Northern Sea Oats: a prolific self-seeder offered as the top companion to a delicate fern, because chasmanthium is missing its spreader flag
subject: maidenhair-fern
claim: Takes part shade to full shade and moderate to high water; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 1 m, for the layer behind it; both are native plants.
why: Chasmanthium latifolium is the classic 'lovely but it seeds everywhere' shade grass (MOBOT and most extension services warn it self-seeds prolifically and can become weedy; standard advice is to cut the seed heads before they ripen). In taxonomy.ts it is recorded as `chasmanthium: { native: true }` with no `spreader: true`. That single missing flag is load-bearing: Maidenhair Fern has spread 0.4 m (<= DELICATE_SPREAD_M 0.5) and category Fern, so isDelicate() is true; had chasmanthium been flagged, spreaderSmothers() would have HARD-BLOCKED this pair. Instead it is ranked #1, and the one sentence the beginner reads celebrates the seed heads that will sow the grass straight into a slow, delicate Adiantum colony. (The same gap silences the 'a vigorous spreader, so give it room' clause on every sea-oats card.)
fix: Add `spreader: true` to the `chasmanthium` entry in src/garden/intel/taxonomy.ts; the existing spreader-smothers blocker then removes this pairing on its own.
scope: All northern-sea-oats pairings; directly produces the block-worthy maidenhair-fern pair and the un-cautioned lily-of-valley pair.
notes: Keep the finding, but frame the harm accurately: Chasmanthium latifolium is a US NATIVE, not an invasive species, so the problem is prolific self-seeding into a slow, delicate fern colony (a real, documented maintenance/crowding issue, worst in the moist soil the fern needs), not plant death or invasiveness. The strongest support to cite is the taxonomy's own precedent: miscanthus is flagged spreader:true explicitly because "M. sinensis self-seeds," and melissa/alchemilla are flagged for self-seeding as well, so the missing chasmanthium flag is a verifiable internal inconsistency. Severity high is defensible on the strength of the #1 ranking plus the reason praising the seed heads (the causal trait) with no caution, but it sits at the upper edge given the pair is culturally compatible.

Verified as a wrong-library-data finding. Concrete artifacts: taxonomy.ts:429 (chasmanthium missing spreader:true) vs precedent at taxonomy.ts:423 (miscanthus flagged for the same self-seeding behavior); pair is companion #1/8 for maidenhair-fern at score 43.6 in review-packets.json with the quoted reason. Fix (add spreader:true to chasmanthium) would trip the existing spreader-smothers hard blocker and remove the pair. Also correctly notes the missing flag suppresses the 'a vigorous spreader, so give it room' penalty/clause on all sea-oats cards.

Downgrade high→medium: the finding is factually airtight and does matter (it defeats a purpose-built smother block, surfaces as the #1 companion, and the one-line reason praises the exact harmful trait with no caution — plus the gap silences the 'give it room' warning on every sea-oats card). But the report's 'high' tier is reserved for exotic invasives praised for the trait that makes them invasive; chasmanthium is a US NATIVE aggressive self-seeder, and the harm to the beginner is gradual and fully recoverable with routine deadheading/seedling removal. Real issue, medium severity. Fix stands: add `spreader: true` to the chasmanthium entry in taxonomy.ts.

title: Maiden Grass -> Trumpet Vine: reason sells 'height without the footprint' for a plant the library itself flags as an aggressive spreader
subject: maiden-grass
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; opens in early summer and overlaps into this one's season; climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint.
why: Campsis radicans has one of the largest footprints of any plant a beginner can buy: it root-suckers metres from the crown, resprouts from severed roots, is near-impossible to eradicate once established, and clings by aerial rootlets to anything it can reach (siding, mortar, tree trunks). 'Height without the footprint' is the precise opposite of the truth, and the library knows it - taxonomy.ts has `campsis: { spreader: true }`. The caution is silenced by two code paths at once: layeringClause returns the Climber sentence before any spreader text, and spreaderNeedsWatching()/isVulnerableTo() both bail when the neighbour is itself a spreader or a ROBUST_CATEGORY (Climber), so Miscanthus + Campsis (both spreaders) yields zero warning. There is also no support structure implied behind a grass clump. The same vine is offered to Littleleaf Linden with only 'both are strong pollinator plants' - i.e. plant a self-clinging 8 m vine at the foot of the shade tree it will climb into.
fix: In layeringClause, do not use the 'height without the footprint' phrasing when the climber is flagged `spreader` (say 'vigorous: give it a stout support and expect suckers'), and let the spreader clause fire even when both plants are spreaders.
scope: Every trumpet-vine pairing (also linden -> trumpet-vine in this chunk); the Climber phrasing is library-wide.
notes: Verified exact. One wording fix to carry into the report: the caution is not suppressed by layeringClause ordering but solely by spreaderNeedsWatching's both-spreaders bail (soft(p)=!p.spreader is false for both). Worth noting the scope is broader than the one pair: trumpet-vine (Campsis radicans, spreader:true) is recommended as a companion in 15 packets and the spreader caution fires in none of them - woody hosts are ROBUST_CATEGORY so soft()=false, herbaceous spreaders hit the both-spreader bail - so the vine's aggressive nature is surfaced to the user nowhere in the engine's output.

Confirmed. The wrong "height without the footprint" wording is emitted for trumpet-vine (Campsis radicans, spreader:true) under 5 subjects (lilac, maiden-grass, viburnum, winterberry, witch-hazel), and trumpet-vine is recommended in 15 companion lists with zero spreader caution in any of them - so this is a systematic wrong-reason for an aggressive self-clinging, root-suckering climber, not a one-off. The fix should gate the climber layering phrasing on the spreader flag and let the caution clause fire even when both plants are spreaders.

Confirmed. maiden-grass -> trumpet-vine emits (rank 6/8, score 52.9): "Shares full sun and low to moderate water; opens in early summer and overlaps into this one's season; climbs to 8 m behind it, height without the footprint." Both genera carry spreader:true (taxonomy.ts campsis L385, miscanthus L423). The layering Climber template (compatibility.ts L1065) sells "height without the footprint" for a plant whose worst trait is a huge suckering footprint and self-clinging structure damage; the built-in spreader caution is suppressed because spreaderNeedsWatching's soft() test excludes spreader-vs-spreader (and robust-subject) pairs. Systematic, not one-off: across 15 emitted trumpet-vine companion lists, 0 carry any spreader caution, and 5 (maiden-grass, lilac, viburnum, winterberry, witch-hazel) print "height without the footprint" verbatim; linden gets only "both are strong pollinator plants." This is a wrong/irresponsible reason for a beginner audience, not an un-modelled-factor gap. Fix per the finding: in layeringClause, drop "height without the footprint" when the climber is flagged spreader, and let the spreader caution fire even when both plants are spreaders.

title: Littleleaf Linden -> Butterfly Bush: a Washington Class B noxious weed recommended, uncautioned, and praised for its pollinator value
subject: linden
claim: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; both are strong pollinator plants; low at 2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it.
why: Buddleja davidii is a Class B noxious weed in Washington and is under an Oregon Department of Agriculture quarantine (only approved seedless/sterile cultivars may be sold); it has escaped along river gravels and disturbed ground across the Pacific Northwest, the mid-Atlantic and the UK. The library's own entry (`butterfly-bush`, Buddleia davidii, tags: pollinator) is the straight seeding species, not a sterile cultivar, and taxonomy.ts gives `buddleia` a `pollinator: "high"` credit with no spreader or caution flag at all. So the app hands a beginner in Seattle or Portland a plant they may not legally buy, and the single reason they read is a compliment about its nectar. It is also an ecological trap: nectar-rich but a larval host for essentially nothing, which is the opposite of what 'strong pollinator plant' implies to a novice.
fix: Flag `buddleia` in taxonomy.ts (spreader/invasive) so it is de-scored and carries a caution, and either point the archetype at a sterile cultivar (e.g. 'Blue Chip'/'Miss Molly', already in the library) or suppress it for the regions where it is listed.
scope: All butterfly-bush pairings (the archetype plus its 10 variants inherit the same trait row).
notes: Confirmed high severity. Concrete surfaced instance: linden ("Littleleaf Linden", Tilia cordata) card shows butterfly-bush as companion #7 of 8. Root cause is a single missing flag: taxonomy.ts:145 buddleia has no spreader/invasive attribute, so the engine's de-score and caution never apply, and the archetype (plantLibrary.ts:2464) is the fertile straight species Buddleia davidii rather than a sterile cultivar. Fix is two-part as proposed: add a spreader/invasive flag to buddleia in taxonomy.ts (de-scores + attaches a caution across all 42 lists), and/or repoint the archetype to an approved sterile cultivar already in the library (Blue Chip, Miss Molly, Pugster Blue). Note the same class of defect affects other uncautioned tier-1 invasives in invasive-recount.json (pampas-grass 191 lists, maiden-grass 68, periwinkle/Vinca minor 4), but this finding is scoped correctly to a named pair.

Finding is accurate as written. Minor calibration: the invasiveness mechanism is buddleia's prolific self-seeding, not its pollinator value; the pollinator-praise clause is the specific harm here (uncautioned nectar compliment on an ecological-trap noxious weed), which the finding states correctly. The audit's automated invasive-companion lens missed this because it keys off the spreader flag that buddleia lacks in taxonomy.ts, so the named pairing is a real gap, not a duplicate.

Two precision refinements for the report (verdict unchanged): (1) In OR/WA the restriction is on the SALE of non-approved (non-sterile) Buddleja davidii, not on consumer possession/growing — so phrase it as "the straight species you may not legally buy in OR/WA" rather than implying possession is illegal. (2) "Ecological trap" is slightly strong; the accurate critique is that it provides abundant adult nectar but essentially no larval-host value for North American butterflies, which is what makes the bare label "strong pollinator plant" misleading to a novice who might plant it instead of a native host. Also worth noting the same defect is systemic: butterfly-bush is recommended, uncautioned, to 11 subjects (allium, candytuft, creeping-phlox, juniper, linden, sage, sea-thrift, snow-in-summer, etc.), so the fix should target the buddleia taxonomy flag / archetype, not just this one pair.

title: Layering clause calls a 2 m shrub a ground carpet under any tree subject
subject: linden
claim: low at 2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it
why: A 2 m butterfly bush is a substantial shrub; nothing about it 'carpets the ground'. In layeringClause (compatibility.ts ~line 1070) the Tree/Conifer branch chooses the carpet wording on the height RATIO alone (r > CANOPY_RATIO = 6), with no absolute ceiling on the companion's height - unlike the border branch, which additionally requires tallEnoughToShelter. Under a 15 m linden, everything below 2.5 m is described as a ground carpet. The code comment two lines above shows the author was alert to exactly this failure ('nobody carpets the ground beneath a black-eyed Susan') but the guard was not applied to the tree branch. It reads as nonsense to the north-star user and quietly mis-teaches what a groundcover is; it also implies the shrub belongs directly under the canopy of a densely shading, shallow-rooted lime, which is a poor place for a full-sun Buddleja.
fix: In the Tree/Conifer branch of layeringClause, require an absolute ceiling on b.height (say b.height <= 0.4 m, or a groundcover tag) before using the 'carpet the ground beneath it' wording; otherwise fall through to 'shorter at X m, an understory layer'.
scope: Every Tree/Conifer (or >= 6 m) subject paired with a companion more than 6x shorter.
notes: Survives. One imprecision to fix in the write-up: the finding frames the fix as applying the border branch's tallEnoughToShelter guard to the tree branch. That guard tests the TALLER plant's tier (tierOf(max(a.height,b.height)) >= 2), which is trivially true for a 15m tree, so it would not fix the tree branch. The finding's own proposed fix (an absolute b.height ceiling, e.g. <=0.4m, or a groundcover tag) is the correct remedy; keep that, drop the "same guard was not applied" framing. Also note the defect is systemic across 19 tree/conifer/climber subjects, not a lone linden case.

One descriptive imprecision to fix in the report: the guiding comment is not "two lines above" the tree branch; it sits ~12 lines above, directly over the tallEnoughToShelter definition (lines 1057-1060, tree branch at 1073). Substance is unchanged. Otherwise accurate; the pair passes all hard filters and has real merit (both strong pollinators, compatible sun/water/zones), so this is correctly a weak-reason/wording defect, not a bad-pair kill.

Verified against data: linden (Tilia cordata, Tree, 15 m) -> butterfly-bush (Buddleia davidii, Shrub, 2 m) is displayed companion #7/8 (score 44.7), reason clause "low at 2 m, to carpet the ground beneath it". Root cause: layeringClause Tree/Conifer branch (compatibility.ts ~1072-1076) chooses the "carpet" wording on height ratio (r > CANOPY_RATIO = 6) with no absolute ceiling on the COMPANION height, so any 1-2.5 m companion under a 15 m tree is mislabelled a ground carpet. This variant is NOT caught by the audit's canopy-wording rule (which only fires when the taller plant is too short), so it is an uncaught gap. Fix note: tallEnoughToShelter guards the taller plant, not the companion, so the correct remedy is the proposed absolute b.height ceiling (e.g. <= 0.4 m or a groundcover tag), else fall through to "shorter at X m, an understory layer".

title: Lisianthus -> Gardenia: an acid-soil, constantly-moist shrub paired with an alkaline-soil, rot-prone annual on one watering line
subject: lisianthus
claim: Takes full sun to part sun and moderate to high water; opens in late spring and overlaps into this one's season; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; taller at 1.2 m, for the layer behind it.
why: These two want opposite soil. Gardenia jasminoides is an obligate acid-lover (pH 5.0-6.5; it chloroses and sulks above ~6.5) that needs constantly moist, humus-rich soil and high humidity. Eustoma is a plant of the limestone prairies of the southern Great Plains: it wants neutral to slightly alkaline soil (about pH 6.5-7.0) and sharp drainage, and it is notoriously susceptible to Fusarium/Pythium crown and root rot when kept wet - the single most common way home growers lose it. On one drip line, the regime that keeps the gardenia happy rots the lisianthus, and the soil amendment that keeps the gardenia green (sulphur/acidifier) is the wrong direction for the lisianthus. The reason papers this over as 'moderate to high water', and it also puts a part-shade shrub in front of an annual that needs full sun to stand up straight. Note the contrast: the other companions offered here (rosemary, candytuft, rock cress) are lime-tolerant sharp-drainage plants and are genuinely fine - gardenia is the one real outlier in this list.
fix: Give the intel layer a coarse acid-obligate flag (gardenia, rhododendron/azalea, camellia, blueberry) and block or heavily penalise pairing an acid obligate with a lime/sharp-drainage plant; at minimum, do not let a 'high' water shrub read as compatible with a rot-prone 'moderate' water annual.
scope: Gardenia, and the acid-obligate shrubs generally, paired with Mediterranean/prairie sharp-drainage subjects.
notes: Uphold as-is but tighten two wording slips: gardenia is placed BEHIND (taller back layer), not "in front of" the lisianthus; and the library value is "part-sun," not "part-shade." Keep the core thrust (acid-obligate, constantly-moist, rot-inducing regime for a neutral-alkaline, sharp-drainage, Fusarium-prone annual on one line). Optionally note the automated soil-ph lens missed this because it only checks acid-lover subjects, not acid-lover companions.

Confirmed real. Tighten two details before publishing: (1) gardenia's library sun value is "part-sun," not "part-shade" (it is one rung from lisianthus's full-sun, which is why it passes the filter); (2) the reason places gardenia in the layer BEHIND the lisianthus ("taller at 1.2 m, for the layer behind it"), not "in front," so lead with the soil-pH + moisture/drainage conflict (the solid point) rather than the shading claim. Core finding: an acid-obligate, high-water, constantly-moist evergreen shrub (Gardenia jasminoides) is recommended as a companion to a rot-prone, sharp-drainage, neutral-to-alkaline annual (Eustoma), and the only penalty the engine applies is a single water-differs x0.92, with the reason wording "moderate to high water" masking the mismatch.

Keep as a valid pairing finding but trim to the defensible core: lisianthus (rot-prone, sharp-drainage, neutral-to-alkaline prairie annual, filed moderate water) is recommended alongside gardenia (acid-obligate shrub filed high water) on one bed/watering line; keeping the soil constantly moist for the gardenia can rot the lisianthus, a hazard the engine's 1-rung water gap hides. Drop the pH-lethality claim (lisianthus tolerates ~6.0-7.0) and the light/shading claim (gardenia is part-sun and is layered behind, not in front). Note gardenia is a marginal suggestion here (rank 7 of 8, score 46.1) and is over-recommended generally (204 cards), so a coarse acid-obligate + high-water flag would help systemically, not just for this pair.

title: Monkshood's top recommendations are dry-soil Mediterranean/alpine plants (creeping sedum, rock cress, thyme, sea thrift, lavender): opposite soil-moisture regimes
subject: monkshood
claim: Creeping Sedum (score 71.1, the #1 pick): "Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; opens in early summer and overlaps into this one's season; low at 0.1 m, to carpet the ground beneath" - and the same for Rock Cress (65.8), Thyme (65.4), Sea Thrift (65.0), Lavender (59.1).
why: Five of monkshood's eight companions are xeric plants explicitly tagged "drought tolerant". Aconitum is a cool-climate woodland-edge perennial that demands deep, humus-rich soil kept evenly moist all season and collapses in hot, lean, sharply drained ground. Sedum, Aubrieta, Armeria and Lavandula need exactly the opposite: gritty, lean, sharp drainage and a dry summer, and they rot at the crown under the steady irrigation monkshood requires. The library's one-rung water gap (moderate vs low) hides a genuine either/or: on one watering schedule, in one soil, one of the two dies. This is the coarse-band failure, not a taste call - a beginner told to carpet sedum around monkshood loses one or the other in season one.
fix: Two options, either is enough: (a) raise monkshood's library water from "moderate" to "high" so the low-water xerics fall outside the one-rung filter; or (b) make the "drought tolerant" tag a hard exclusion against moisture-dependent subjects (a soil-moisture-regime axis: dry/sharp-drainage vs moist/humus-rich) rather than a soft x0.92 water-differs penalty.
scope: 5 of monkshood's 8 companions; the same low-vs-moderate collapse will hit every moisture-dependent woodlander in the library (Thalictrum, Astilbe-type plants) that sits at water="moderate".
notes: Finding is accurate as written. Optional strengthening: (1) monkshood's own library water value is arguably under-set — reputable sources say its soil must not dry out, so "high" is defensible and would itself push the xerics outside the one-rung filter (fix a). (2) There is a compounding, unclaimed light conflict: the subject is part-sun (cool woodland edge) while all five xerics are full-sun, so the pairing fails on exposure as well as moisture. The per-instance automated lens rated these "medium"; high is justified here by the systematic pattern (the #1 pick plus 4 of 8 companions) and the beginner-directed "carpet the ground beneath" wording with zero caution.

Data fully verified. Minor precision note: the "drought tolerant" tag is not hand-written on each entry but auto-derived from water:"low" (plantLibrary.ts line 1741) - the finding's wording is still accurate. Severity high is defensible because 5 of 8 companions including the #1 pick are moisture-mismatched, and the three moisture-compatible options (sweet-alyssum, lobelia, pansy) actually rank BELOW the xerics, so the ranking is inverted from correct.

Real conflict, but downgrade to medium and re-frame. Monkshood (Aconitum) water='moderate' is CORRECT data (moisture-retentive, drought-intolerant, cool-climate; not a bog plant), so do NOT apply proposed fix (a) raising it to 'high' — that mis-files it. The genuine issue is a soil-moisture-REGIME mismatch the coarse low-vs-moderate water band hides (fix b direction). 5 of 8 top companions (creeping-sedum, rock-cress, thyme, sea-thrift, lavender) are low-water drought-tolerant xerics, so the recommended bed tilts xeric while monkshood needs cool, humus-rich, evenly-moist soil. Sharpest, most defensible single pairing is monkshood->lavender (opposite soil/drainage/light-heat regimes; lavender rots in monkshood's conditions and vice versa). The other three xerics are adaptable to moderate well-drained beds, so 'one dies in season one' is overstated for them. The engine's own xeric-vs-wetter lens already classifies these at medium.

title: Monkshood + Thyme: the app tells a beginner to carpet a harvested culinary herb under one of the most poisonous plants in the garden, with no caution
subject: monkshood
claim: Thyme (score 65.4): "...blooms in late spring, before this one opens; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months."
why: Every part of Aconitum contains aconitine; it is one of the few garden plants where extension guidance is to wear gloves because the toxin is absorbed through intact skin, and where fatal poisonings are on record. Standard advice is to site it away from herb and vegetable beds and from anywhere children graze. The engine's advice is the exact inverse: it recommends interplanting an edible-tagged herb, which the gardener harvests by hand and eats unwashed, as a mat covering monkshood's crown and root zone (its most toxic part). Thyme foliage intermingles with monkshood's basal leaves, so the harvest is contaminated by touch at minimum. The reason offers no caution of any kind.
fix: The curated data already carries a toxicity severity (curatedStore.ts: "serious" vs "mild"). Consume it in compatibility.ts: block any companion tagged "edible" against a subject with serious toxicity (and vice versa), or at minimum emit a caution clause. cautionClause() currently only ever fires for spreaders.
scope: One named pair here, but the same hole exists for every edible-tagged companion beside a seriously toxic subject (foxglove, lily-of-the-valley, castor bean and so on).
notes: Keep as high-severity irresponsible-advice. One framing tightening: the headline leans on skin-absorption fatality, but the documented FATAL route for aconitine is overwhelmingly ingestion (tuberous roots mistaken for horseradish/Jerusalem artichoke, per monkshood.json's own c-root-confusion claim), not brief dermal contact; topical poisoning is documented but rarely fatal from casual handling. Frame the acute harm as (1) the app contradicting its own curated protocol to keep monkshood "well away from the vegetable garden," (2) elevated root-confusion risk when weeding/harvesting a mat interplanted over the tubers, and (3) hand-contact contamination of a herb harvested and eaten unwashed, rather than "a beginner will die from touching thyme." Bonus supporting point (not needed for the ruling): the two are also culturally opposed, monkshood wants rich, humus-rich, consistently-moist, cool-rooted part-shade while thyme wants lean, gritty, sharply-drained, dry full-sun, so the pairing is weak on growing conditions too despite passing the engine's coarse 1-rung water filter.

Confirmed against files. Direction is monkshood(subject) -> thyme(companion), score 65.4, rank #3/8; reason = "Takes full sun to part sun and low to moderate water; blooms in late spring, before this one opens; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; both offer pollinator forage, in different months." Monkshood curated safety level is "serious"; engine (compatibility.ts) does not import or use curatedStore safety, and cautionClause() only fires for spreaders. Minor caveat for the report: soften "absorbed through intact skin" (mainstream guidance is gloves/handle-with-care; primary danger is ingestion and contact via cuts), and note the reverse pairing (thyme card recommending monkshood) does not occur — the bad advice appears only on monkshood's card. Neither affects severity.

Anchor the rationale to the verifiable basis rather than the speculative poisoning chain: the app's own curated data (safety.level "serious", "Keep it from vegetables", "toxins soak through skin") directly contradicts the engine recommending an edible herb (thyme, Thymus vulgaris, tagged "Edible and pet-safe") to carpet the ground beneath monkshood's most-toxic root zone, with no caution. The engine already has the toxicity data (curated safety.level) it fails to consume; cautionClause currently fires only for spreaders. Proposed fix (gate/caption serious-toxicity subjects paired with edible companions) is feasible against existing data.
